source.title: Kernel variant split-testing and safety contract
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary

This document defines the safety contract, telemetry requirements, and a step-by-step split-testing procedure for rolling out new kernel variants in the serving runtime. It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency, correctness, and resource-stability properties while providing deterministic rollback triggers.

Goals

- Allow safe A/B (split) testing of kernel variants (e.g., tiled-attention-v2, quantized-matmul-approx, async-dispatch) against production workloads.
- Define automated and human-reviewed rollback criteria that preserve customer SLOs and prevent resource exhaustion.
- Standardize telemetry and alerting required to make split decisions reproducible and auditable.
- Provide a compact, executable checklist for runbook-driven canary experiments.

Scope and non-goals

- Scope: kernel-level variants and scheduler policy plugins deployed through the runtime-coordinator. Applies to hosted and dedicated environments; private deployments may adopt with adjustments for networked telemetry.
- Non-goals: model topology changes, dataset-level model weight variations, or hardware firmware updates (those follow separate change control).

Safety contract (mandatory)

All kernel variant rollouts must satisfy the following contract before traffic is diverted beyond a baseline canary:

1) Binary and configuration signoff: build artifact signed and checksum verified.
2) Unit/regression tests: pass the kernel microbench suite (latency delta < +5% for median on representative microbench) and correctness tests (numerical tolerance/bitmask pass).
3) Telemetry gates: new telemetry keys instrumented (see Telemetry section) and validated in dev shadow traffic.
4) Resource guardrails: per-GPU memory delta must be within +/- 8% of baseline under synthetic steady-state; no additional persistent allocations.

Telemetry (required signals)

All kernel variants must emit the following metrics to the observability-bridge with the specified cardinality and dimensions:

- kernel.exec.latency.p50/p95/p99 (ns) — dims: model, sequence_len_bucket, cohort, kernel_variant
- kernel.exec.gpu_memory.delta (MB) — dims: node_id, kernel_variant
- kernel.exec.error.rate (count / 1m) — dims: error_code, kernel_variant
- kernel.exec.preemptions (count) — dims: kernel_variant, priority_class
- kernel.exec.cpu-offload.time (ms) — dims: kernel_variant, host_type

Validation requirements

- Telemetry ingest must be validated end-to-end for a sample of traffic before any gradual ramp: test query must appear within 90s in the monitoring pipeline and show correct tags.
- Aggregation freshness: p50/p95 must update within 120s for canary cohorts.

Split-testing procedure (step-by-step)

Preflight (offline)

1) Build and sign kernel artifact. Record SHA256 in release notes.
2) Run microbench suite (baseline vs candidate) across sequence length buckets: 8, 32, 128, 512. Attach results to experiment ticket.
3) Validate correctness test harness (token-level diffs on deterministic prompts).

Canary (controlled production)

1) Canary cohort creation: create a dedicated cohort named cvt-YYYYMMDD-<short> limited to 0.5% traffic or up to 3 clients, whichever is smaller.
2) Deploy kernel variant to a subset of dispatcher shards via runtime-coordinator with flag kernel_variant=candidate; ensure a rollback flag is present in the same change set.
3) Shadow traffic warmup: mirror production requests for 10 minutes to validate end-to-end trace tagging and K/V cache behavior.
4) Ramp schedule: 0.5% -> 2% (30m) -> 5% (1h) -> 10% (2h). Pause at each step for at least one monitoring evaluation interval (see Monitoring checks).

Monitoring checks (automated)

At each ramp step the following automated checks run and must all pass for the ramp to continue:

- Latency: p95(candidate)/p95(baseline) <= 1.15 for the canary cohort across sequence_len_bucket median.
- Error rate: error.rate candidate <= baseline + 0.05% absolute and not > 2x baseline for any error_code.
- Memory: rolling 5m mean gpu_memory.delta within +/- 8% of baseline.
- Preemptions: preemptions increase <= 20% relative baseline.
- SLO proxy: request-level SLO (90th percentile latency for end-to-end) must not regress by more than 8%.

Monitoring checks (human review)

- After the 5% and 10% steps, an engineer from the owning team must perform a manual review of traces, histograms, and example failure traces. Approvers: the release owner and at least one peer reviewer from kernel-scheduling.

Rollback and abort criteria (automated immediate abort)

Any of the following triggers an immediate automated rollback to the previous variant and pages on-call:

1) p99 latency increase > 35% over baseline for >3 minutes.
2) absolute error rate increase of >0.2% and sustained for 2 minutes.
3) GPU memory delta > +15% for any node for 60s (indicates potential leak).
4) Sudden spike in preemptions causing >30% throughput drop for >2 minutes.

Abort (human-initiated)

- A release owner or SRE may abort the rollout at any time based on qualitative evidence in traces or customer reports. Document rationale in the experiment ticket.

Postmortem and artifact capture

- On any rollback, capture the following artifacts automatically into the experiment ticket: kernel logs (last 1h), kernel telemetry snapshots, runtime-coordinator change set, and example trace IDs (at least 5).
- Run the failure triage checklist (see Triage section) within 24 hours.

Configuration matrix (example)

| Parameter | Baseline value | Candidate constraints | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| initial_cohort_size | 0.5% | 0.5% | Small exposure for first validation |
| max_cohort_size | 10% | <= 10% | Default upper bound for automated ramp |
| max_ramp_duration | 4h | <= 8h | Total time from canary start to max_cohort_size |
| memory_delta_threshold | +/-8% | candidate must be within | Guardrail measured on synthetic steady-state |
| error_rate_abs_threshold | +0.05% | smaller is better | Absolute increase over baseline |

Example CLI and config snippet

YAML used by runtime-coordinator to create a canary: 

```yaml
canary: 
  name: cvt-20260215-async-tiling
  kernel_variant: tiled-attention-v2
  cohort: cvt-20260215
  initial_weight: 0.005
  ramp_steps:
    - weight: 0.02
      duration_minutes: 30
    - weight: 0.05
      duration_minutes: 60
    - weight: 0.10
      duration_minutes: 120
  rollback_policy: automated
  telemetry_tags: ["kernel_variant", "cohort", "release_owner"]
```

CLI example (ops):

$ rictl canary apply -f cvt-20260215-async-tiling.yaml
$ rictl canary status cvt-20260215-async-tiling

Triage checklist (on rollback)

1) Confirm rollback completed and previous variant active.
2) Collect last 60m of kernel logs and telemetry snapshots.
3) Identify top 5 trace IDs correlated with p99 latency or errors.
4) Run microbench candidate vs baseline with match of failing sequence lengths.
5) Decide next step: revert permanently, patch and retry, or abort experiment.

Ownership and escalation

- Release owner: assign in the experiment ticket (engineer responsible for rollout).
- Kernel-scheduling on-call: first responder for automated pages triggered by rollback.
- Runtime SRE: responsible for cross-cluster mitigations and capacity actions.

Rollout signoff checklist (pre-production to production)

- Dev and CI: microbench and unit tests green.
- Observability: telemetry keys instrumented and pipe validated.
- Capacity: synthetic steady-state memory validation complete.
- Security: artifact signed and signed-by key verified.
- Approvals: release owner + kernel-scheduling peer reviewer approve in ticket.

References and related pages

- Scheduler policy language and declarative profiles (see internal contract)
- Observability bridge telemetry tagging guide
- Runtime-coordinator deployment and change set conventions

Appendix: example alerts to create for canary cohorts

- alert: CanaryKernelLatencyP99Spike
  expr: increase(kernel_exec_latency_p99{cohort="cvt-20260215"}[2m]) > 0.35
  for: 3m
  labels: {severity: page}

- alert: CanaryKernelMemoryLeakCandidate
  expr: kernel_exec_gpu_memory_delta_bytes{kernel_variant="tiled-attention-v2"} > 15728640
  for: 1m
  labels: {severity: page}

Notes and rationale

This spec attempts to codify pragmatic thresholds identified from historical regressions: latency tail behavior, memory leaks manifesting under steady-state, and preemption amplification when background CPU offload patterns change. Thresholds prioritize customer-facing SLO preservation over incremental throughput gains.

If changes to the contract are proposed, they must be reviewed by kernel-scheduling and runtime-sre and published with a changelog entry and example experiment ticket templates.

Document history

- 2025-11-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added memory guardrail guidance and CLI examples (review by Evan Clark).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized automated rollback thresholds and published.

source.title: Representative Workload Suite (v1) — Perf Baseline & Regression Detection
source.author: Rina Kobayashi
source.body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Representative Workload Suite (v1)** used for:
- establishing the **Q1 2026 performance baselines** (latency/throughput) across regions and GPU SKUs
- detecting performance regressions in CI/perf-canary with production-like traffic shapes
- generating consistent workload cohorts for model catalog performance profiles

The suite is designed to mirror **production traffic shapes** without using or reconstructing sensitive prompt content (see ADR-0173). The workload definitions are stable, versioned, and fully reproducible via run-manifest capture (see ADR-0172).

## Non-goals
- This suite is not a customer-facing benchmark and is not optimized for headline numbers.
- v1 does not cover multimodal, audio, speculative decoding experiments, or fine-tuning endpoints.

---

## Suite structure at a glance
The suite is a set of workload generators + request-mix definitions. Each workload has:
- a stable **workload_id** (versioned)
- a **traffic model** (arrival/concurrency)
- prompt/output length distributions (token bins)
- streaming vs non-streaming behavior
- cache scenarios (cold vs warm) where applicable
- mapping to a production cohort derived from telemetry

### Workloads included in v1
| Workload ID | Name | Mode | Primary metrics | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1 | Chat streaming (short + medium) | streaming | TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, decode tokens/s | Dominant production pattern; sensitive to scheduling, batching, and decode efficiency |
| RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1 | Long-context summarization (prefill stress) | non-streaming (default) + optional streaming | Prefill latency p95/p99, end-to-end latency p95/p99, queue wait | Exposes KV/cache pressure, prefill kernels, overlap behavior; drives tail latency |
| RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1 | Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn) | streaming | TTFT p95/p99 under burst, queue wait, overload behavior | Captures burst dynamics and fairness/backpressure; stresses admission and queue disciplines |
| RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1 | Embeddings (high QPS small payload) | non-streaming | req/s, p95/p99 latency, CPU overhead signals | Throughput-oriented; highlights dispatch, batching, and networking overheads |

---

## Canonical measurement boundaries (v1)
All metrics in this suite follow the canonical definitions in **ADR-0172**. This section calls out the boundaries that frequently cause confusion.

### Streaming TTFT
- **TTFT (time-to-first-token)** is measured from **server request accept** (runtime receives request) to **first token on wire** (server-side flush to streaming transport).
- Client-side consumption delays must not be included in TTFT. For harness implementations, we use a reader that continuously drains the stream to avoid measuring client backpressure.

### TPOT (time per output token)
- TPOT is computed over the streamed decode portion only (excluding prefill), using server-side timestamps.
- For non-streaming requests, TPOT is derived from decode duration / output_tokens.

### Cache scenarios
Where caching materially changes runtime behavior (e.g., prefix caching or KV reuse patterns), we report **two scenarios**:
- **Cold:** no pre-warming; treat each request as cache-miss
- **Warm:** controlled warmup phase; then repeat requests to produce predictable cache-hit behavior

The warm scenario is explicitly tagged in the run-manifest (cache_scenario=warm) and should not be conflated with production hit rates.

---

## Models covered (suite default)
The suite is designed to be model-agnostic, but the default baseline sweeps target the top deployed families.

**Default model set (Q1 2026 baseline sweep):**
- Llama-family: Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, Llama 3.1 70B Instruct
- Qwen-family: Qwen2.5 7B Instruct, Qwen2.5 72B Instruct
- Mistral-family: Mixtral 8x7B Instruct (or current catalog equivalent)

Notes:
- Exact model identifiers are captured in the run-manifest (model_id, revision, quantization profile).
- When a model is unavailable on a specific SKU/region, the suite still runs with remaining models; gaps must be annotated in the run tracker.

---

## Production cohort mapping (telemetry-derived)
Workload shapes and token-length distributions are derived from telemetry cohorts, aggregated into bins. We do not store prompts; we store only:
- token count histograms (prompt_tokens, output_tokens)
- request type labels (chat streaming vs non-streaming, embeddings)
- concurrency/arrival summaries by endpoint
- tool-calling incidence and burstiness metrics

**Cohort tags used in manifests:**
- cohort=chat_stream_shortmed
- cohort=long_context_summarization
- cohort=toolcall_bursty_multiturn
- cohort=embeddings_smallpayload_highqps

---

## Traffic model conventions (applies to all workloads)
To improve reproducibility and comparability across environments, v1 standardizes:

1) **Warmup:**
- Minimum warmup duration: 5 minutes or 200 requests per worker (whichever is larger)
- Warmup results are discarded

2) **Steady-state run:**
- Minimum measurement window: 15 minutes or 2,000 requests per worker
- For tail percentiles (p99), require enough samples to stabilize (see perf regression policy)

3) **Concurrency vs arrival rate:**
- Chat/tool workloads use **closed-loop concurrency** (N concurrent sessions) because it better reflects user-facing latency sensitivity.
- Embeddings use **open-loop arrival rate** (target QPS) because it better reflects batch/throughput regimes.

4) **Ramp:**
- Standard ramp: 2 minutes linear ramp from 0% to target load

5) **Randomness + determinism:**
- Each run uses fixed seeds for:
  - token-length sampling
  - tool-call branching (when applicable)
  - per-session think time
- Seeds are recorded in the run-manifest (workload_seed).

---

## Workload definitions (v1)
Below are the exact v1 definitions. These are the source of truth for the harness generators.

### 1) RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1 — Chat streaming (short + medium)
**Production cohort:** chat_stream_shortmed

**Description**
Represents the majority of interactive chat traffic: short prompts, moderate response lengths, streaming enabled. Sensitive to scheduler batching decisions and decode kernel choices.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/chat.completions (stream=true)
- Turns: single-turn for v1 (multi-turn is covered in toolcall workload)
- System prompt: short fixed template (non-sensitive, synthetic)

**Token distributions (sampled per request)**
- Prompt tokens (prefill):
  - 40%: 32–96
  - 45%: 97–256
  - 15%: 257–512
- Output tokens (decode):
  - 55%: 64–192
  - 35%: 193–512
  - 10%: 513–1024

**Streaming cadence**
- Server emits tokens as soon as available.
- Harness drains stream continuously; no artificial client throttling.

**Load model**
- Closed-loop sessions
- Target concurrency levels (per model/SKU run): 8, 16, 32 (select highest stable without overload)
- Session think time: 0ms (v1 isolates serving latency; product-like think time is a v2 candidate)

**Reported metrics**
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95
- decode tokens/s (aggregate)
- scheduler queue_wait p95/p99 (if instrumentation enabled)

**Cache scenario**
- Report both cold and warm (warm = repeated identical synthetic template prefix to exercise prefix caching, if enabled).

---

### 2) RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1 — Long-context summarization (prefill stress)
**Production cohort:** long_context_summarization

**Description**
Represents long-context summarization/analysis where prefill dominates. This workload is designed to surface tail latency issues from queueing, backpressure, KV cache pressure, and kernel cliffs in long sequence regimes.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/chat.completions
- Default mode: stream=false (to focus on prefill + full completion)
- Optional variant for debugging: stream=true (same token lengths)

**Token distributions**
- Prompt tokens (prefill):
  - 20%: 2,048–4,096
  - 50%: 4,097–8,192
  - 30%: 8,193–16,384
- Output tokens:
  - 70%: 128–384
  - 25%: 385–768
  - 5%: 769–1,536

**Load model**
- Closed-loop concurrency
- Target concurrency levels: 1, 2, 4, 8 (stop at first overload per policy; record overload threshold)

**Reported metrics**
- Prefill latency p50/p95/p99
- End-to-end latency p50/p95/p99
- TTFT p50/p95/p99 (only for streaming variant)
- queue_wait p95/p99
- KV cache eviction/pressure counters (if available)

**Cache scenario**
- Cold only for baseline. Warm can be misleading for long-context and is run only when explicitly investigating caching behavior.

---

### 3) RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1 — Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn)
**Production cohort:** toolcall_bursty_multiturn

**Description**
Models tool/function calling patterns with multi-turn sessions and burstiness (e.g., many sessions initiating within a short interval). It stresses fairness, backpressure, and scheduler behavior under sudden load changes.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/chat.completions (stream=true)
- Session length: 3 turns
- Tool calls:
  - Turn 1: 30% chance of 1 tool call
  - Turn 2: 20% chance of 1 tool call
  - Turn 3: no tool calls (forces final response)
- Tool payloads are synthetic JSON with bounded size (no PII)

**Token distributions**
- Prompt tokens per turn:
  - 60%: 64–192
  - 30%: 193–512
  - 10%: 513–1,024
- Output tokens per turn:
  - 70%: 32–128
  - 25%: 129–256
  - 5%: 257–512

**Burst model**
- Closed-loop sessions with bursty arrivals:
  - Burst period: 60s
  - Burst shape: 3x baseline session start rate for 15s, then 0.8x for 45s
- Target baseline concurrency band: 16–64 (selected per SKU to reach ~70% utilization without overload during non-burst portion)

**Reported metrics**
- TTFT p50/p95/p99 (overall and burst-window only)
- queue_wait p95/p99 (overall and burst-window only)
- drop/load-shed counters (if overload policy triggers)
- fairness indicators: per-tenant or per-session starvation signals (when enabled)

**Cache scenario**
- Cold only (v1). Tool call branching makes warm definitions less stable; revisit in v2.

---

### 4) RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1 — Embeddings (high QPS small payload)
**Production cohort:** embeddings_smallpayload_highqps

**Description**
High-throughput embeddings traffic with small input payloads. Captures dispatch overhead, batching efficiency, and tail latency under high request rates.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/embeddings
- Input: synthetic text strings sized to hit token bins

**Token distributions**
- Input tokens:
  - 80%: 16–64
  - 18%: 65–128
  - 2%: 129–256

**Load model**
- Open-loop QPS targets (per model/SKU run): 200, 500, 1,000 req/s (stop if saturation causes sustained errors)
- Request jitter: Poisson arrivals with bounded jitter (+/- 10%)

**Reported metrics**
- req/s achieved
- latency p50/p95/p99
- error rate (timeouts, 429/overload)
- CPU overhead indicators (if enabled): request parse time, queue wait

**Cache scenario**
- Not applicable (no prefix/KV caching). Warmup still applies to stabilize clocks and memory.

---

## Request mixes (v1)
The suite supports named mixes for different run contexts (baseline sweeps vs PR gating). Mixes are applied at orchestration time.

### Mix A: MIX-PRODLIKE-V1 (default)
Used for nightly baseline tracking and most release-branch canary runs.
- 55% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 20% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 15% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 10% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

### Mix B: MIX-CHATHEAVY-V1
Used when validating decode-path or streaming changes.
- 75% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 10% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 10% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 5% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

### Mix C: MIX-LONGPREFILL-STRESS-V1
Used to gate long-context kernel/scheduler changes.
- 20% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 60% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 10% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 10% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

### Mix D: MIX-EMB-THROUGHPUT-V1
Used for embeddings infrastructure changes.
- 10% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 5% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 5% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 80% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

Mix selection rules (recommended):
- Runtime scheduler changes: Mix A + Mix C
- Kernel selection changes: Mix A + workload-specific (e.g., LongPrefill)
- Networking/ingress changes: Mix A + Embeddings

---

## Versioning + compatibility
- This document defines **Representative Workload Suite v1**.
- Workload IDs include a version suffix; any behavioral change requires incrementing the ID (e.g., RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V2) rather than silently modifying v1.
- Harness must emit both:
  - suite_version=1
  - workload_id per request stream/bucket

---

## Reproducibility requirements (must-haves)
A run is considered valid only if the run-manifest includes (see ADR-0172):
- runtime build id / git SHA and build flags
- GPU SKU, driver version, CUDA version, container image digest
- region/cluster identifier and node instance type
- scheduler policy config (or config hash)
- kernel selection mode + any pins/overrides
- workload_id + suite_version + mix_id
- workload_seed and length distribution parameters
- cache_scenario (cold/warm) where applicable

If any required field is missing, the run must be tagged **invalid_manifest** and excluded from baseline comparisons.

---

## Validation checklist (how we confirm “representative”)
We validate v1 representativeness at two levels:

1) **Shape match** (telemetry → bins)
- prompt/output token histograms overlap with cohort histograms within tolerance
- streaming incidence matches cohort
- tool-call rate approximates cohort (within +/- 5pp)

2) **System behavior match**
- utilization and batch size distributions are within expected operating regimes for the target SKU
- queue wait is non-zero but not dominating under steady-state for chat workloads (i.e., not accidentally under/over-driving)

Any significant mismatch should be filed as a v2 candidate change (do not mutate v1).

---

## Known limitations (v1)
- Multi-tenant fairness is only partially exercised; workloads run with a limited set of synthetic “tenants.”
- Think time and client pacing are simplified to isolate server behavior.
- Long-context workload does not include extremely long outputs (e.g., >2k tokens) by default.
- Warm-cache definitions are intentionally conservative; they are not intended to reflect real hit rates.

---

## Operational notes (perf-canary + CI)
- **PR gating:** typically runs MIX-CHATHEAVY-V1 at reduced scale on one SKU/region (noise-aware thresholds apply).
- **Nightly canary:** runs MIX-PRODLIKE-V1 across the baseline matrix (see baseline plan) with strict environment capture.
- **Release signoff:** requires MIX-PRODLIKE-V1 + MIX-LONGPREFILL-STRESS-V1 on the release-candidate tag.

Escalation and triage are handled per the Perf Regression Policy page and the profiling playbook.

---

## Contact / ownership
- Workload suite + harness: Rina Kobayashi
- Methodology + representativeness: Claire Dubois, Jonas Weber
- Scheduler behavior: Sofia Ivanova
- SRE integration + fleet stability: Sean Gallagher

For proposed changes, open a PR against benchmark-lab and tag the owners above; any change that affects behavior must introduce a new workload_id or suite_version.

## Purpose
This page defines the **Representative Workload Suite (v1)** used for:
- establishing the **Q1 2026 performance baselines** (latency/throughput) across regions and GPU SKUs
- detecting performance regressions in CI/perf-canary with production-like traffic shapes
- generating consistent workload cohorts for model catalog performance profiles

The suite is designed to mirror **production traffic shapes** without using or reconstructing sensitive prompt content (see ADR-0173). The workload definitions are stable, versioned, and fully reproducible via run-manifest capture (see ADR-0172).

## Non-goals
- This suite is not a customer-facing benchmark and is not optimized for headline numbers.
- v1 does not cover multimodal, audio, speculative decoding experiments, or fine-tuning endpoints.

---

## Suite structure at a glance
The suite is a set of workload generators + request-mix definitions. Each workload has:
- a stable **workload_id** (versioned)
- a **traffic model** (arrival/concurrency)
- prompt/output length distributions (token bins)
- streaming vs non-streaming behavior
- cache scenarios (cold vs warm) where applicable
- mapping to a production cohort derived from telemetry

### Workloads included in v1
| Workload ID | Name | Mode | Primary metrics | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1 | Chat streaming (short + medium) | streaming | TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, decode tokens/s | Dominant production pattern; sensitive to scheduling, batching, and decode efficiency |
| RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1 | Long-context summarization (prefill stress) | non-streaming (default) + optional streaming | Prefill latency p95/p99, end-to-end latency p95/p99, queue wait | Exposes KV/cache pressure, prefill kernels, overlap behavior; drives tail latency |
| RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1 | Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn) | streaming | TTFT p95/p99 under burst, queue wait, overload behavior | Captures burst dynamics and fairness/backpressure; stresses admission and queue disciplines |
| RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1 | Embeddings (high QPS small payload) | non-streaming | req/s, p95/p99 latency, CPU overhead signals | Throughput-oriented; highlights dispatch, batching, and networking overheads |

---

## Canonical measurement boundaries (v1)
All metrics in this suite follow the canonical definitions in **ADR-0172**. This section calls out the boundaries that frequently cause confusion.

### Streaming TTFT
- **TTFT (time-to-first-token)** is measured from **server request accept** (runtime receives request) to **first token on wire** (server-side flush to streaming transport).
- Client-side consumption delays must not be included in TTFT. For harness implementations, we use a reader that continuously drains the stream to avoid measuring client backpressure.

### TPOT (time per output token)
- TPOT is computed over the streamed decode portion only (excluding prefill), using server-side timestamps.
- For non-streaming requests, TPOT is derived from decode duration / output_tokens.

### Cache scenarios
Where caching materially changes runtime behavior (e.g., prefix caching or KV reuse patterns), we report **two scenarios**:
- **Cold:** no pre-warming; treat each request as cache-miss
- **Warm:** controlled warmup phase; then repeat requests to produce predictable cache-hit behavior

The warm scenario is explicitly tagged in the run-manifest (cache_scenario=warm) and should not be conflated with production hit rates.

---

The suite is designed to be model-agnostic, but the default baseline sweeps target the top deployed families.

- Qwen-family: Qwen2.5 7B Instruct, Qwen2.5 72B Instruct
- Mistral-family: Mixtral 8x7B Instruct (or current catalog equivalent)

Notes:
- Exact model identifiers are captured in the run-manifest (model_id, revision, quantization profile).
- When a model is unavailable on a specific SKU/region, the suite still runs with remaining models; gaps must be annotated in the run tracker.

3) **Concurrency vs arrival rate:**

- Embeddings use **open-loop arrival rate** (target QPS) because it better reflects batch/throughput regimes.

4) **Ramp:**

The suite is designed to mirror **production traffic shapes** without using or reconstructing sensitive prompt content (see ADR-0173). The workload definitions are stable, versioned, and fully reproducible via run-manifest capture (see ADR-0172).

## Non-goals
- This suite is not a customer-facing benchmark and is not optimized for headline numbers.
- v1 does not cover multimodal, audio, speculative decoding experiments, or fine-tuning endpoints.

---

## Suite structure at a glance
The suite is a set of workload generators + request-mix definitions. Each workload has:
- a stable **workload_id** (versioned)
- a **traffic model** (arrival/concurrency)
- prompt/output length distributions (token bins)
- streaming vs non-streaming behavior
- cache scenarios (cold vs warm) where applicable
- mapping to a production cohort derived from telemetry

### Workloads included in v1
| Workload ID | Name | Mode | Primary metrics | Why it exists |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1 | Chat streaming (short + medium) | streaming | TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, decode tokens/s | Dominant production pattern; sensitive to scheduling, batching, and decode efficiency |
| RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1 | Long-context summarization (prefill stress) | non-streaming (default) + optional streaming | Prefill latency p95/p99, end-to-end latency p95/p99, queue wait | Exposes KV/cache pressure, prefill kernels, overlap behavior; drives tail latency |
| RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1 | Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn) | streaming | TTFT p95/p99 under burst, queue wait, overload behavior | Captures burst dynamics and fairness/backpressure; stresses admission and queue disciplines |
| RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1 | Embeddings (high QPS small payload) | non-streaming | req/s, p95/p99 latency, CPU overhead signals | Throughput-oriented; highlights dispatch, batching, and networking overheads |

---

## Canonical measurement boundaries (v1)
All metrics in this suite follow the canonical definitions in **ADR-0172**. This section calls out the boundaries that frequently cause confusion.

### Streaming TTFT
- **TTFT (time-to-first-token)** is measured from **server request accept** (runtime receives request) to **first token on wire** (server-side flush to streaming transport).
- Client-side consumption delays must not be included in TTFT. For harness implementations, we use a reader that continuously drains the stream to avoid measuring client backpressure.

### TPOT (time per output token)
- TPOT is computed over the streamed decode portion only (excluding prefill), using server-side timestamps.
- For non-streaming requests, TPOT is derived from decode duration / output_tokens.

### Cache scenarios
Where caching materially changes runtime behavior (e.g., prefix caching or KV reuse patterns), we report **two scenarios**:
- **Cold:** no pre-warming; treat each request as cache-miss
- **Warm:** controlled warmup phase; then repeat requests to produce predictable cache-hit behavior

The warm scenario is explicitly tagged in the run-manifest (cache_scenario=warm) and should not be conflated with production hit rates.

---

## Models covered (suite default)
The suite is designed to be model-agnostic, but the default baseline sweeps target the top deployed families.

**Default model set (Q1 2026 baseline sweep):**
- Llama-family: Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, Llama 3.1 70B Instruct
- Qwen-family: Qwen2.5 7B Instruct, Qwen2.5 72B Instruct
- Mistral-family: Mixtral 8x7B Instruct (or current catalog equivalent)

Notes:
- Exact model identifiers are captured in the run-manifest (model_id, revision, quantization profile).
- When a model is unavailable on a specific SKU/region, the suite still runs with remaining models; gaps must be annotated in the run tracker.

---

## Production cohort mapping (telemetry-derived)
Workload shapes and token-length distributions are derived from telemetry cohorts, aggregated into bins. We do not store prompts; we store only:
- token count histograms (prompt_tokens, output_tokens)
- request type labels (chat streaming vs non-streaming, embeddings)
- concurrency/arrival summaries by endpoint
- tool-calling incidence and burstiness metrics

**Cohort tags used in manifests:**
- cohort=chat_stream_shortmed
- cohort=long_context_summarization
- cohort=toolcall_bursty_multiturn
- cohort=embeddings_smallpayload_highqps

---

## Traffic model conventions (applies to all workloads)
To improve reproducibility and comparability across environments, v1 standardizes:

1) **Warmup:**
- Minimum warmup duration: 5 minutes or 200 requests per worker (whichever is larger)
- Warmup results are discarded

2) **Steady-state run:**
- Minimum measurement window: 15 minutes or 2,000 requests per worker
- For tail percentiles (p99), require enough samples to stabilize (see perf regression policy)

3) **Concurrency vs arrival rate:**
- Chat/tool workloads use **closed-loop concurrency** (N concurrent sessions) because it better reflects user-facing latency sensitivity.
- Embeddings use **open-loop arrival rate** (target QPS) because it better reflects batch/throughput regimes.

4) **Ramp:**
- Standard ramp: 2 minutes linear ramp from 0% to target load

5) **Randomness + determinism:**
- Each run uses fixed seeds for:
  - token-length sampling
  - tool-call branching (when applicable)
  - per-session think time
- Seeds are recorded in the run-manifest (workload_seed).

---

## Workload definitions (v1)
Below are the exact v1 definitions. These are the source of truth for the harness generators.

### 1) RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1 — Chat streaming (short + medium)
**Production cohort:** chat_stream_shortmed

**Description**
Represents the majority of interactive chat traffic: short prompts, moderate response lengths, streaming enabled. Sensitive to scheduler batching decisions and decode kernel choices.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/chat.completions (stream=true)
- Turns: single-turn for v1 (multi-turn is covered in toolcall workload)
- System prompt: short fixed template (non-sensitive, synthetic)

**Token distributions (sampled per request)**
- Prompt tokens (prefill):
  - 40%: 32–96
  - 45%: 97–256
  - 15%: 257–512
- Output tokens (decode):
  - 55%: 64–192
  - 35%: 193–512
  - 10%: 513–1024

**Streaming cadence**
- Server emits tokens as soon as available.
- Harness drains stream continuously; no artificial client throttling.

**Load model**
- Closed-loop sessions
- Target concurrency levels (per model/SKU run): 8, 16, 32 (select highest stable without overload)
- Session think time: 0ms (v1 isolates serving latency; product-like think time is a v2 candidate)

**Reported metrics**
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95
- decode tokens/s (aggregate)
- scheduler queue_wait p95/p99 (if instrumentation enabled)

**Cache scenario**
- Report both cold and warm (warm = repeated identical synthetic template prefix to exercise prefix caching, if enabled).

---

### 2) RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1 — Long-context summarization (prefill stress)
**Production cohort:** long_context_summarization

**Description**
Represents long-context summarization/analysis where prefill dominates. This workload is designed to surface tail latency issues from queueing, backpressure, KV cache pressure, and kernel cliffs in long sequence regimes.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/chat.completions
- Default mode: stream=false (to focus on prefill + full completion)
- Optional variant for debugging: stream=true (same token lengths)

**Token distributions**
- Prompt tokens (prefill):
  - 20%: 2,048–4,096
  - 50%: 4,097–8,192
  - 30%: 8,193–16,384
- Output tokens:
  - 70%: 128–384
  - 25%: 385–768
  - 5%: 769–1,536

**Load model**
- Closed-loop concurrency
- Target concurrency levels: 1, 2, 4, 8 (stop at first overload per policy; record overload threshold)

**Reported metrics**
- Prefill latency p50/p95/p99
- End-to-end latency p50/p95/p99
- TTFT p50/p95/p99 (only for streaming variant)
- queue_wait p95/p99
- KV cache eviction/pressure counters (if available)

**Cache scenario**
- Cold only for baseline. Warm can be misleading for long-context and is run only when explicitly investigating caching behavior.

---

### 3) RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1 — Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn)
**Production cohort:** toolcall_bursty_multiturn

**Description**
Models tool/function calling patterns with multi-turn sessions and burstiness (e.g., many sessions initiating within a short interval). It stresses fairness, backpressure, and scheduler behavior under sudden load changes.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/chat.completions (stream=true)
- Session length: 3 turns
- Tool calls:
  - Turn 1: 30% chance of 1 tool call
  - Turn 2: 20% chance of 1 tool call
  - Turn 3: no tool calls (forces final response)
- Tool payloads are synthetic JSON with bounded size (no PII)

**Token distributions**
- Prompt tokens per turn:
  - 60%: 64–192
  - 30%: 193–512
  - 10%: 513–1,024
- Output tokens per turn:
  - 70%: 32–128
  - 25%: 129–256
  - 5%: 257–512

**Burst model**
- Closed-loop sessions with bursty arrivals:
  - Burst period: 60s
  - Burst shape: 3x baseline session start rate for 15s, then 0.8x for 45s
- Target baseline concurrency band: 16–64 (selected per SKU to reach ~70% utilization without overload during non-burst portion)

**Reported metrics**
- TTFT p50/p95/p99 (overall and burst-window only)
- queue_wait p95/p99 (overall and burst-window only)
- drop/load-shed counters (if overload policy triggers)
- fairness indicators: per-tenant or per-session starvation signals (when enabled)

**Cache scenario**
- Cold only (v1). Tool call branching makes warm definitions less stable; revisit in v2.

---

### 4) RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1 — Embeddings (high QPS small payload)
**Production cohort:** embeddings_smallpayload_highqps

**Description**
High-throughput embeddings traffic with small input payloads. Captures dispatch overhead, batching efficiency, and tail latency under high request rates.

**Request shape**
- Endpoint: /v1/embeddings
- Input: synthetic text strings sized to hit token bins

**Token distributions**
- Input tokens:
  - 80%: 16–64
  - 18%: 65–128
  - 2%: 129–256

**Load model**
- Open-loop QPS targets (per model/SKU run): 200, 500, 1,000 req/s (stop if saturation causes sustained errors)
- Request jitter: Poisson arrivals with bounded jitter (+/- 10%)

**Reported metrics**
- req/s achieved
- latency p50/p95/p99
- error rate (timeouts, 429/overload)
- CPU overhead indicators (if enabled): request parse time, queue wait

**Cache scenario**
- Not applicable (no prefix/KV caching). Warmup still applies to stabilize clocks and memory.

---

## Request mixes (v1)
The suite supports named mixes for different run contexts (baseline sweeps vs PR gating). Mixes are applied at orchestration time.

### Mix A: MIX-PRODLIKE-V1 (default)
Used for nightly baseline tracking and most release-branch canary runs.
- 55% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 20% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 15% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 10% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

### Mix B: MIX-CHATHEAVY-V1
Used when validating decode-path or streaming changes.
- 75% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 10% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 10% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 5% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

### Mix C: MIX-LONGPREFILL-STRESS-V1
Used to gate long-context kernel/scheduler changes.
- 20% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 60% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 10% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 10% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

### Mix D: MIX-EMB-THROUGHPUT-V1
Used for embeddings infrastructure changes.
- 10% RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V1
- 5% RWK-LONGPREFILL-V1
- 5% RWK-TOOLCALL-BURST-V1
- 80% RWK-EMB-HIQPS-V1

Mix selection rules (recommended):
- Runtime scheduler changes: Mix A + Mix C
- Kernel selection changes: Mix A + workload-specific (e.g., LongPrefill)
- Networking/ingress changes: Mix A + Embeddings

---

## Versioning + compatibility
- This document defines **Representative Workload Suite v1**.
- Workload IDs include a version suffix; any behavioral change requires incrementing the ID (e.g., RWK-CHAT-STREAM-V2) rather than silently modifying v1.
- Harness must emit both:
  - suite_version=1
  - workload_id per request stream/bucket

---

## Reproducibility requirements (must-haves)
A run is considered valid only if the run-manifest includes (see ADR-0172):
- runtime build id / git SHA and build flags
- GPU SKU, driver version, CUDA version, container image digest
- region/cluster identifier and node instance type
- scheduler policy config (or config hash)
- kernel selection mode + any pins/overrides
- workload_id + suite_version + mix_id
- workload_seed and length distribution parameters
- cache_scenario (cold/warm) where applicable

If any required field is missing, the run must be tagged **invalid_manifest** and excluded from baseline comparisons.

---

## Validation checklist (how we confirm “representative”)
We validate v1 representativeness at two levels:

1) **Shape match** (telemetry → bins)
- prompt/output token histograms overlap with cohort histograms within tolerance
- streaming incidence matches cohort
- tool-call rate approximates cohort (within +/- 5pp)

2) **System behavior match**
- utilization and batch size distributions are within expected operating regimes for the target SKU
- queue wait is non-zero but not dominating under steady-state for chat workloads (i.e., not accidentally under/over-driving)

Any significant mismatch should be filed as a v2 candidate change (do not mutate v1).

---

## Known limitations (v1)
- Multi-tenant fairness is only partially exercised; workloads run with a limited set of synthetic “tenants.”
- Think time and client pacing are simplified to isolate server behavior.
- Long-context workload does not include extremely long outputs (e.g., >2k tokens) by default.
- Warm-cache definitions are intentionally conservative; they are not intended to reflect real hit rates.

---

## Operational notes (perf-canary + CI)
- **PR gating:** typically runs MIX-CHATHEAVY-V1 at reduced scale on one SKU/region (noise-aware thresholds apply).
- **Nightly canary:** runs MIX-PRODLIKE-V1 across the baseline matrix (see baseline plan) with strict environment capture.
- **Release signoff:** requires MIX-PRODLIKE-V1 + MIX-LONGPREFILL-STRESS-V1 on the release-candidate tag.

Escalation and triage are handled per the Perf Regression Policy page and the profiling playbook.

---

## Contact / ownership
- Workload suite + harness: Rina Kobayashi
- Methodology + representativeness: Claire Dubois, Jonas Weber
- Scheduler behavior: Sofia Ivanova
- SRE integration + fleet stability: Sean Gallagher

For proposed changes, open a PR against benchmark-lab and tag the owners above; any change that affects behavior must introduce a new workload_id or suite_version.

source.title: Runbook: Runtime overload mitigation (Hosted API)
source.author: Lena Fischer
source.body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, triage, and mitigate **Serving Runtime overload** for Redwood Hosted API. It focuses on the runtime layer (scheduler, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization signals) and how to apply mitigations safely without causing retry storms or noisy-neighbor regressions.

This runbook is used when:
- p95/p99 latency spikes and/or timeouts correlate with runtime saturation.
- Error rates increase due to runtime admission rejecting work (shed) or the gateway/routing breaker opening.
- One or more GPU pools show persistent queue growth or memory pressure.

## Scope
In scope:
- Runtime overload symptoms and saturation metrics
- Runtime mitigations (throttle/admission, batching caps, feature reduction toggles exposed to runtime)
- When/how to coordinate with Gateway and Smart Routing

Out of scope:
- Customer comms/status page updates (see Hosted circuit breaker runbook)
- Long-term capacity changes / procurement

## Roles and escalation
- **Runtime oncall (primary):** serving-runtime engineer on rotation
- **SRE oncall (incident commander):** for SEV2+ or multi-region impact
- **Gateway/control plane:** for admission-layer changes
- **Smart Routing:** for fallback routing / regional steering

Escalate when:
- Multi-tenant impact in a region for >10 minutes
- Shed rate > 2% sustained for a tier that should not shed under normal bursts (Enterprise Hosted)
- Suspected regression (post deploy) correlates with overload signals

## Safety principles (read first)
1. **Prefer early shedding over tail-latency collapse.** If runtime is overloaded, accepting more work usually makes recovery harder.
2. **Avoid retry storms.** Any change that increases 429/503 must ensure stable Retry-After behavior at the edge (Gateway) and bounded retries in clients.
3. **Change one thing at a time.** Apply a single mitigation, wait 3–5 minutes, then evaluate.
4. **Preserve tenant isolation.** Do not globally increase batching to “fix” one tenant if it degrades others.

## Quick decision tree
Use this to choose the right mitigation quickly.

### A) Queue depth climbing and latency increasing; GPU utilization high
Likely **compute saturation** (tokens/sec bound) or under-provisioned pool.
- First actions: tighten admission / reduce concurrency; confirm gateway breaker is protecting.
- Secondary: reduce max_tokens caps / streaming concurrency; adjust batching caps conservatively.

### B) Queue depth climbing; GPU utilization moderate; memory usage high / OOMs
Likely **KV cache pressure** / memory fragmentation / long-context load.
- First actions: cap max_context or max_prefill_tokens; reduce parallelism for long prompts.
- Secondary: adjust KV cache policy; reduce batch size or prefill concurrency.

### C) Queue depth stable; latency spikes; error rate increases in bursts
Likely **dependency brownout** (model storage, tokenizer service, auth, or routing) or **scheduler stall**.
- First actions: identify dependency errors; consider temporary feature reduction that reduces dependency load.
- Secondary: coordinate with SRE; consider draining affected shards.

### D) Shed rate high but saturation metrics look normal
Likely **bad thresholds** or **bad signal** / instrumentation issue.
- First actions: validate signal freshness and aggregation; check if a single shard is bad.
- Secondary: roll back threshold changes; page runtime instrumentation owner.

## Where to look (dashboards & logs)
### Dashboards
Open the “Overload Protection” dashboard and then drill into “Serving Runtime” panels.
Recommended views (filters): `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `model_family`, `tier`.

Key panels to review (names may vary by region):
- **Runtime admission / shed rate** (requests rejected at runtime)
- **Prefill queue depth** (pending prefill jobs)
- **Decode queue depth** (pending decode steps)
- **Scheduler backlog / loop time** (scheduler tick latency)
- **Token throughput** (tokens/sec by pool)
- **GPU utilization** (avg and p95 across replicas)
- **KV cache pressure** (usage ratio, eviction rate)
- **GPU memory usage** and **OOM count**
- **P99 end-to-end latency** split by stage (gateway, routing, runtime)

### Logs / traces
- Runtime service logs: `serving-runtime` (and model-specific workers)
- Look for: `OOM`, `cuda out of memory`, `scheduler lag`, `queue full`, `admission denied`, `kv_cache` warnings
- Trace a few slow requests to see whether time is spent in prefill, decode, or waiting.

## Metrics glossary and how to interpret
Use these to confirm you are dealing with runtime overload (vs gateway/routing/dependency).

### 1) Prefill queue depth
What it means: number of requests waiting for prefill (prompt processing).
Interpretation:
- Rising steadily with increasing prefill latency indicates prefill saturation (often long prompts).
- If prefill queue grows but decode queue is flat, you are prefill-bound.

### 2) Decode queue depth
What it means: backlog of decode steps (streaming generation) waiting for compute.
Interpretation:
- Rising decode queue with high GPU util indicates tokens/sec saturation.
- Rising decode queue with moderate GPU util can indicate scheduler overhead or throttling.

### 3) Token/sec saturation
What it means: measured throughput approaching pool’s steady-state ceiling.
Interpretation:
- If tokens/sec is near ceiling and p99 worsens, prioritize shedding / concurrency reduction.
- If tokens/sec is low but queues grow, suspect stalls, memory, or dependency issues.

### 4) KV cache pressure
Signals:
- `kv_cache_usage_ratio` high (near configured limit)
- high `kv_cache_evictions` and increasing latency
Interpretation:
- High KV usage correlates with long contexts and many concurrent streams.
- Evictions can cause performance cliffs; prefer limiting long-context concurrency rather than only increasing batching.

### 5) Memory pressure / fragmentation proxy
Signals:
- increasing allocation failures / “reserved but unavailable” patterns
- frequent OOM recoveries/restarts
Interpretation:
- Treat as urgent; restarts can cause thundering herd if the gateway keeps admitting.

## Standard triage procedure
### Step 1 — Confirm impact and scope (2–3 minutes)
1. Identify region(s), model(s), and tiers affected.
2. Confirm if the gateway breaker is open and shedding is happening at the edge.
3. Check whether impact is localized to a pool/shard or systemic.

Record in the incident doc (or Slack thread):
- Start time
- Affected region + model + tier
- Current shed rate and p99 latency

### Step 2 — Determine saturation type (5 minutes)
Use the “Metrics glossary” above to classify as:
- Compute saturation (decode-bound)
- Prefill saturation (prompt-bound)
- Memory/KV pressure
- Dependency brownout / scheduler stall

### Step 3 — Apply the least risky mitigation
Prefer mitigations in this order (lowest blast radius first):
1) Tighten admission for the impacted pool/route
2) Reduce expensive features (streaming concurrency, tool-calling complexity)
3) Reduce max_tokens / max_context caps (tier-aware)
4) Tune batching caps
5) Drain/cordon bad replicas/shards

### Step 4 — Validate recovery
After each change:
- Wait 3–5 minutes (longer for long-context workloads)
- Confirm queue depth trends down
- Confirm p99 latency improves
- Confirm shed rate stabilizes (not oscillating)
- Confirm no new error class/regression is introduced (e.g., missing Retry-After at edge)

## Mitigation actions (how-to)
Notes:
- Exact knobs vary by deployment. When in doubt, coordinate with Gateway oncall before changing customer-visible behavior.
- All changes should be logged in the incident timeline with time, actor, knob, previous value, new value.

### Mitigation 1 — Reduce runtime admission / concurrency (preferred first response)
Goal: stop backlog growth and allow queues to drain.

Common actions:
- Reduce `runtime_max_inflight_requests` for the impacted pool.
- Reduce `decode_concurrency_per_gpu` or `streams_per_replica`.

Operational guidance:
- If queue depth is climbing: cut concurrency by 10–25% first.
- If OOMs are occurring: cut concurrency by 25–50% and reduce long-context traffic.

Example (illustrative) command patterns:
- View current pool config:
  - `rwctl runtime pools get --region us-east --pool hosted-a10g-1`
- Patch concurrency cap:
  - `rwctl runtime pools patch --region us-east --pool hosted-a10g-1 --set decode_concurrency_per_gpu=6`

Rollback:
- Restore previous values after queues stabilize for at least 30 minutes.

### Mitigation 2 — Feature reduction toggles (reduce expensive work)
Goal: reduce per-request cost while keeping service available.

Supported feature reduction modes (must be policy-guarded):
- Lower streaming concurrency (limits concurrent active streams per tenant)
- Reduce tool-calling complexity (e.g., cap tool calls or tool schema size)
- Apply stricter `max_tokens` caps during overload

When to use:
- Decode-bound saturation (tokens/sec ceiling)
- Tenant with unusually heavy tool-calling causing noisy-neighbor behavior

How to execute:
- Confirm the feature flag is enabled for overload mode (policy engine / gateway).
- Apply only to affected tier/route/model if possible.

Notes:
- Customer-visible behavior may change. Ensure error semantics remain stable and documented (ADR-014).

### Mitigation 3 — Cap long-context prefill (KV/memory pressure response)
Goal: reduce KV cache footprint and avoid OOM/fragmentation.

Actions:
- Reduce `max_context_tokens` (or `max_prompt_tokens`) for the affected model route.
- Reduce parallel prefill concurrency (if exposed separately).

When to use:
- Prefill queue depth + memory pressure rising
- OOMs correlated with long prompts

Tradeoffs:
- More requests may be rejected early (prefer 429/503 overload with clear guidance) rather than timing out.

### Mitigation 4 — Adjust continuous batching caps (use with caution)
Goal: improve throughput efficiency without causing latency cliffs.

Actions:
- Increase `max_batch_tokens` slightly (5–15%) if GPU util is high but scheduler overhead is low.
- Decrease `max_batch_tokens` if tail latency is exploding due to too-large batches.

When to use:
- Stable traffic with predictable sequence lengths
- You have clear evidence of batching inefficiency rather than pure saturation

Warnings:
- Over-aggressive batching can worsen p99, especially with mixed prompt lengths.
- Changing batching interacts with KV cache behavior.

### Mitigation 5 — Drain/cordon unhealthy replicas or shards
Goal: remove pathological workers (memory leak, bad GPU, driver issues) that create localized overload.

Indicators:
- One replica shows abnormal queue depth, errors, or restarts.
- Pool-wide metrics look fine but tail latency remains high.

Steps (generic Kubernetes pattern; adapt to your environment):
1. Identify unhealthy pods:
   - `kubectl -n serving-runtime get pods -l pool=<pool> -o wide`
2. Check logs for repeated OOM or crash loops.
3. Cordon/drain (if supported via runtime orchestration):
   - `rwctl runtime replicas cordon --replica <id> --reason "oom-loop"`
4. Verify load rebalances and queues recover.

### Mitigation 6 — Coordinate with Smart Routing for fallback/steering
Goal: shift load away from an overloaded region/pool or to a compatible model variant.

When to page Smart Routing:
- Region capacity dip persists >10 minutes
- Shed rate remains >1–2% after local mitigations
- You see a single hot model family saturating a region

What to provide:
- Region/pool/model impacted
- Current saturation signals (queue depth, tokens/sec)
- Desired action (regional shift vs model fallback)

Constraints:
- Ensure loop prevention and bounded retries are working (routing should not bounce between regions/models).

## Common failure modes and fixes
### Failure mode: Shed rate oscillation (flapping)
Symptoms:
- Breaker opens/closes repeatedly; queues never fully drain.
Fixes:
- Add hysteresis: raise close threshold, increase min-open duration.
- Reduce concurrency slightly more to create headroom.
- Confirm signal is smoothed (avoid reacting to 10–30s spikes).

### Failure mode: Timeouts increase but shed rate is low
Symptoms:
- Requests pile up; gateway keeps admitting; runtime queues spike.
Fixes:
- Coordinate with Gateway to increase edge shedding and set Retry-After.
- Ensure runtime admission is wired to saturation signals (not just static caps).

### Failure mode: OOM storm after a deployment
Symptoms:
- Frequent worker restarts; partial outage.
Fixes:
- Reduce max_context and concurrency immediately.
- Roll back the offending runtime change if correlated with a specific deploy.
- Keep gateway shedding on until restart rate normalizes.

### Failure mode: Metric cardinality / delayed signals
Symptoms:
- Dashboards stale; decisions based on old data.
Fixes:
- Check metric freshness panels.
- Use aggregated pool-level metrics rather than per-request labels.
- Page telemetry platform if ingestion is degraded.

## Verification checklist (post-mitigation)
- Queue depths trending down (prefill and/or decode)
- OOMs stopped; restart rate normal
- p95/p99 improved and stable for 15+ minutes
- Shed rate stable (not flapping) and within acceptable tier policy
- No spike in client retries / upstream request rate amplification
- Correlation IDs present in overload responses (spot-check sample logs)

## Post-incident follow-ups (required)
Create follow-up tasks when any of the below occur:
- Shed rate exceeded tier expectations
- False positive overload (shed without saturation)
- Missing Retry-After or inconsistent error payloads observed
- Need for new/clearer dashboards or better signal smoothing

Capture:
- Peak queue depth, peak tokens/sec, max KV usage ratio
- Time-to-recovery after mitigation
- Which mitigation(s) were applied and their effect

## Appendix A — Reference thresholds (starting points)
These are *guidelines*; actual thresholds vary by model family and GPU type.
- Sustained rising queue depth for >3 minutes + p99 rising: treat as overload
- OOM count > 0 in 5 minutes: treat as urgent; reduce concurrency and long-context caps
- KV cache usage ratio > 0.90 sustained: reduce long-context concurrency/caps
- Scheduler loop time p95 > 2x baseline: suspect scheduler stall or pathological batching

## Appendix B — What to tell SRE/Gateway when you need help
Provide:
- Region + pool + model route
- Saturation classification (compute vs prefill vs memory vs dependency)
- Current mitigations attempted
- Requested change (increase edge shedding, enable fallback, adjust tier policy)

## Document history
- 2025-10-21: Initial publication for overload protection dark launch.
- 2025-11-29: Added feature reduction modes and coordination notes for canary rollout.
- 2026-02-14: Updated triage decision tree and post-incident checklist based on winter traffic spikes.

title: Runbook: Runtime overload mitigation (Hosted API)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, triage, and mitigate **Serving Runtime overload** for Redwood Hosted API. It focuses on the runtime layer (scheduler, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization signals) and how to apply mitigations safely without causing retry storms or noisy-neighbor regressions.

This runbook is used when:
- p95/p99 latency spikes and/or timeouts correlate with runtime saturation.
- Error rates increase due to runtime admission rejecting work (shed) or the gateway/routing breaker opening.
- One or more GPU pools show persistent queue growth or memory pressure.

## Scope
In scope:
- Runtime overload symptoms and saturation metrics
- Runtime mitigations (throttle/admission, batching caps, feature reduction toggles exposed to runtime)
- When/how to coordinate with Gateway and Smart Routing

Out of scope:
- Customer comms/status page updates (see Hosted circuit breaker runbook)
- Long-term capacity changes / procurement

## Roles and escalation
- **Runtime oncall (primary):** serving-runtime engineer on rotation
- **SRE oncall (incident commander):** for SEV2+ or multi-region impact
- **Gateway/control plane:** for admission-layer changes
- **Smart Routing:** for fallback routing / regional steering

Escalate when:
- Multi-tenant impact in a region for >10 minutes
- Shed rate > 2% sustained for a tier that should not shed under normal bursts (Enterprise Hosted)
- Suspected regression (post deploy) correlates with overload signals

## Safety principles (read first)
1. **Prefer early shedding over tail-latency collapse.** If runtime is overloaded, accepting more work usually makes recovery harder.
2. **Avoid retry storms.** Any change that increases 429/503 must ensure stable Retry-After behavior at the edge (Gateway) and bounded retries in clients.
3. **Change one thing at a time.** Apply a single mitigation, wait 3–5 minutes, then evaluate.
4. **Preserve tenant isolation.** Do not globally increase batching to “fix” one tenant if it degrades others.

## Quick decision tree
Use this to choose the right mitigation quickly.

### A) Queue depth climbing and latency increasing; GPU utilization high
Likely **compute saturation** (tokens/sec bound) or under-provisioned pool.
- First actions: tighten admission / reduce concurrency; confirm gateway breaker is protecting.
- Secondary: reduce max_tokens caps / streaming concurrency; adjust batching caps conservatively.

### B) Queue depth climbing; GPU utilization moderate; memory usage high / OOMs
Likely **KV cache pressure** / memory fragmentation / long-context load.
- First actions: cap max_context or max_prefill_tokens; reduce parallelism for long prompts.
- Secondary: adjust KV cache policy; reduce batch size or prefill concurrency.

### C) Queue depth stable; latency spikes; error rate increases in bursts
Likely **dependency brownout** (model storage, tokenizer service, auth, or routing) or **scheduler stall**.
- First actions: identify dependency errors; consider temporary feature reduction that reduces dependency load.
- Secondary: coordinate with SRE; consider draining affected shards.

### D) Shed rate high but saturation metrics look normal
Likely **bad thresholds** or **bad signal** / instrumentation issue.
- First actions: validate signal freshness and aggregation; check if a single shard is bad.
- Secondary: roll back threshold changes; page runtime instrumentation owner.

## Where to look (dashboards & logs)
### Dashboards
Open the “Overload Protection” dashboard and then drill into “Serving Runtime” panels.
Recommended views (filters): `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `model_family`, `tier`.

Key panels to review (names may vary by region):
- **Runtime admission / shed rate** (requests rejected at runtime)
- **Prefill queue depth** (pending prefill jobs)
- **Decode queue depth** (pending decode steps)
- **Scheduler backlog / loop time** (scheduler tick latency)
- **Token throughput** (tokens/sec by pool)
- **GPU utilization** (avg and p95 across replicas)
- **KV cache pressure** (usage ratio, eviction rate)
- **GPU memory usage** and **OOM count**
- **P99 end-to-end latency** split by stage (gateway, routing, runtime)

### Logs / traces
- Runtime service logs: `serving-runtime` (and model-specific workers)
- Look for: `OOM`, `cuda out of memory`, `scheduler lag`, `queue full`, `admission denied`, `kv_cache` warnings
- Trace a few slow requests to see whether time is spent in prefill, decode, or waiting.

## Metrics glossary and how to interpret
Use these to confirm you are dealing with runtime overload (vs gateway/routing/dependency).

### 1) Prefill queue depth
What it means: number of requests waiting for prefill (prompt processing).
Interpretation:
- Rising steadily with increasing prefill latency indicates prefill saturation (often long prompts).
- If prefill queue grows but decode queue is flat, you are prefill-bound.

### 2) Decode queue depth
What it means: backlog of decode steps (streaming generation) waiting for compute.
Interpretation:
- Rising decode queue with high GPU util indicates tokens/sec saturation.
- Rising decode queue with moderate GPU util can indicate scheduler overhead or throttling.

### 3) Token/sec saturation
What it means: measured throughput approaching pool’s steady-state ceiling.
Interpretation:
- If tokens/sec is near ceiling and p99 worsens, prioritize shedding / concurrency reduction.
- If tokens/sec is low but queues grow, suspect stalls, memory, or dependency issues.

### 4) KV cache pressure
Signals:
- `kv_cache_usage_ratio` high (near configured limit)
- high `kv_cache_evictions` and increasing latency
Interpretation:
- High KV usage correlates with long contexts and many concurrent streams.
- Evictions can cause performance cliffs; prefer limiting long-context concurrency rather than only increasing batching.

### 5) Memory pressure / fragmentation proxy
Signals:
- increasing allocation failures / “reserved but unavailable” patterns
- frequent OOM recoveries/restarts
Interpretation:
- Treat as urgent; restarts can cause thundering herd if the gateway keeps admitting.

## Standard triage procedure
### Step 1 — Confirm impact and scope (2–3 minutes)
1. Identify region(s), model(s), and tiers affected.
2. Confirm if the gateway breaker is open and shedding is happening at the edge.
3. Check whether impact is localized to a pool/shard or systemic.

Record in the incident doc (or Slack thread):
- Start time
- Affected region + model + tier
- Current shed rate and p99 latency

### Step 2 — Determine saturation type (5 minutes)
Use the “Metrics glossary” above to classify as:
- Compute saturation (decode-bound)
- Prefill saturation (prompt-bound)
- Memory/KV pressure
- Dependency brownout / scheduler stall

### Step 3 — Apply the least risky mitigation
Prefer mitigations in this order (lowest blast radius first):
1) Tighten admission for the impacted pool/route
2) Reduce expensive features (streaming concurrency, tool-calling complexity)
3) Reduce max_tokens / max_context caps (tier-aware)
4) Tune batching caps
5) Drain/cordon bad replicas/shards

### Step 4 — Validate recovery
After each change:
- Wait 3–5 minutes (longer for long-context workloads)
- Confirm queue depth trends down
- Confirm p99 latency improves
- Confirm shed rate stabilizes (not oscillating)
- Confirm no new error class/regression is introduced (e.g., missing Retry-After at edge)

## Mitigation actions (how-to)
Notes:
- Exact knobs vary by deployment. When in doubt, coordinate with Gateway oncall before changing customer-visible behavior.
- All changes should be logged in the incident timeline with time, actor, knob, previous value, new value.

### Mitigation 1 — Reduce runtime admission / concurrency (preferred first response)
Goal: stop backlog growth and allow queues to drain.

Common actions:
- Reduce `runtime_max_inflight_requests` for the impacted pool.
- Reduce `decode_concurrency_per_gpu` or `streams_per_replica`.

Operational guidance:
- If queue depth is climbing: cut concurrency by 10–25% first.
- If OOMs are occurring: cut concurrency by 25–50% and reduce long-context traffic.

Example (illustrative) command patterns:
- View current pool config:
  - `rwctl runtime pools get --region us-east --pool hosted-a10g-1`
- Patch concurrency cap:
  - `rwctl runtime pools patch --region us-east --pool hosted-a10g-1 --set decode_concurrency_per_gpu=6`

Rollback:
- Restore previous values after queues stabilize for at least 30 minutes.

### Mitigation 2 — Feature reduction toggles (reduce expensive work)
Goal: reduce per-request cost while keeping service available.

Supported feature reduction modes (must be policy-guarded):
- Lower streaming concurrency (limits concurrent active streams per tenant)
- Reduce tool-calling complexity (e.g., cap tool calls or tool schema size)
- Apply stricter `max_tokens` caps during overload

When to use:
- Decode-bound saturation (tokens/sec ceiling)
- Tenant with unusually heavy tool-calling causing noisy-neighbor behavior

How to execute:
- Confirm the feature flag is enabled for overload mode (policy engine / gateway).
- Apply only to affected tier/route/model if possible.

Notes:
- Customer-visible behavior may change. Ensure error semantics remain stable and documented (ADR-014).

### Mitigation 3 — Cap long-context prefill (KV/memory pressure response)
Goal: reduce KV cache footprint and avoid OOM/fragmentation.

Actions:
- Reduce `max_context_tokens` (or `max_prompt_tokens`) for the affected model route.
- Reduce parallel prefill concurrency (if exposed separately).

When to use:
- Prefill queue depth + memory pressure rising
- OOMs correlated with long prompts

Tradeoffs:
- More requests may be rejected early (prefer 429/503 overload with clear guidance) rather than timing out.

### Mitigation 4 — Adjust continuous batching caps (use with caution)
Goal: improve throughput efficiency without causing latency cliffs.

Actions:
- Increase `max_batch_tokens` slightly (5–15%) if GPU util is high but scheduler overhead is low.
- Decrease `max_batch_tokens` if tail latency is exploding due to too-large batches.

When to use:
- Stable traffic with predictable sequence lengths
- You have clear evidence of batching inefficiency rather than pure saturation

Warnings:
- Over-aggressive batching can worsen p99, especially with mixed prompt lengths.
- Changing batching interacts with KV cache behavior.

### Mitigation 5 — Drain/cordon unhealthy replicas or shards
Goal: remove pathological workers (memory leak, bad GPU, driver issues) that create localized overload.

Indicators:
- One replica shows abnormal queue depth, errors, or restarts.
- Pool-wide metrics look fine but tail latency remains high.

Steps (generic Kubernetes pattern; adapt to your environment):
1. Identify unhealthy pods:
   - `kubectl -n serving-runtime get pods -l pool=<pool> -o wide`
2. Check logs for repeated OOM or crash loops.
3. Cordon/drain (if supported via runtime orchestration):
   - `rwctl runtime replicas cordon --replica <id> --reason "oom-loop"`
4. Verify load rebalances and queues recover.

### Mitigation 6 — Coordinate with Smart Routing for fallback/steering
Goal: shift load away from an overloaded region/pool or to a compatible model variant.

When to page Smart Routing:
- Region capacity dip persists >10 minutes
- Shed rate remains >1–2% after local mitigations
- You see a single hot model family saturating a region

What to provide:
- Region/pool/model impacted
- Current saturation signals (queue depth, tokens/sec)
- Desired action (regional shift vs model fallback)

Constraints:
- Ensure loop prevention and bounded retries are working (routing should not bounce between regions/models).

## Common failure modes and fixes
### Failure mode: Shed rate oscillation (flapping)
Symptoms:
- Breaker opens/closes repeatedly; queues never fully drain.
Fixes:
- Add hysteresis: raise close threshold, increase min-open duration.
- Reduce concurrency slightly more to create headroom.
- Confirm signal is smoothed (avoid reacting to 10–30s spikes).

### Failure mode: Timeouts increase but shed rate is low
Symptoms:
- Requests pile up; gateway keeps admitting; runtime queues spike.
Fixes:
- Coordinate with Gateway to increase edge shedding and set Retry-After.
- Ensure runtime admission is wired to saturation signals (not just static caps).

### Failure mode: OOM storm after a deployment
Symptoms:
- Frequent worker restarts; partial outage.
Fixes:
- Reduce max_context and concurrency immediately.
- Roll back the offending runtime change if correlated with a specific deploy.
- Keep gateway shedding on until restart rate normalizes.

### Failure mode: Metric cardinality / delayed signals
Symptoms:
- Dashboards stale; decisions based on old data.
Fixes:
- Check metric freshness panels.
- Use aggregated pool-level metrics rather than per-request labels.
- Page telemetry platform if ingestion is degraded.

## Verification checklist (post-mitigation)
- Queue depths trending down (prefill and/or decode)
- OOMs stopped; restart rate normal
- p95/p99 improved and stable for 15+ minutes
- Shed rate stable (not flapping) and within acceptable tier policy
- No spike in client retries / upstream request rate amplification
- Correlation IDs present in overload responses (spot-check sample logs)

## Post-incident follow-ups (required)
Create follow-up tasks when any of the below occur:
- Shed rate exceeded tier expectations
- False positive overload (shed without saturation)
- Missing Retry-After or inconsistent error payloads observed
- Need for new/clearer dashboards or better signal smoothing

Capture:
- Peak queue depth, peak tokens/sec, max KV usage ratio
- Time-to-recovery after mitigation
- Which mitigation(s) were applied and their effect

## Appendix A — Reference thresholds (starting points)
These are *guidelines*; actual thresholds vary by model family and GPU type.
- Sustained rising queue depth for >3 minutes + p99 rising: treat as overload
- OOM count > 0 in 5 minutes: treat as urgent; reduce concurrency and long-context caps
- KV cache usage ratio > 0.90 sustained: reduce long-context concurrency/caps
- Scheduler loop time p95 > 2x baseline: suspect scheduler stall or pathological batching

## Appendix B — What to tell SRE/Gateway when you need help
Provide:
- Region + pool + model route
- Saturation classification (compute vs prefill vs memory vs dependency)
- Current mitigations attempted
- Requested change (increase edge shedding, enable fallback, adjust tier policy)

## Document history
- 2025-10-21: Initial publication for overload protection dark launch.
- 2025-11-29: Added feature reduction modes and coordination notes for canary rollout.
- 2026-02-14: Updated triage decision tree and post-incident checklist based on winter traffic spikes.
