source.title: Runtime Experiment Scorecard and Telemetry SLA
source.author: Asha Mehta
source.content:
Summary
-------
This document defines a unified experiment scorecard and a minimal telemetry SLA for runtime experiments, kernel-level canaries, and benchmark-driven rollouts. It bridges architecture, kernel scheduling, profiling, benchmarking, release gating, and on-call runbooks so that engineers and SREs can run reproducible experiments with clear pass/fail criteria.

Scope
-----
- Applies to: runtime feature experiments, kernel variant canaries, quantization rollouts, and model-variant A/B tests run in hosted and dedicated deployments.
- Excludes: user-facing product A/B tests unrelated to inference runtime behavior.

Why this exists
----------------
We observed variability between bench microbenchmarks and live traffic (tail latency regressions, subtle token-cost changes, KV-cache warmup differences). That undermines rollouts. The scorecard + telemetry SLA ensures experiments have signal fidelity, observability, and clear remediation steps.

Scorecard: pass/fail axes
------------------------
Each experiment run must record a scorecard. Minimum axes (all numeric, reported at 99th, 95th, and mean as indicated):

1) Latency (ms)
   - p99 latency vs baseline (target: <= +5% delta)
   - p95 latency vs baseline (target: <= +3% delta)
   - mean latency (target: <= +2% delta)
2) Throughput (req/s)
   - Sustained throughput under canonical load (target: >= 98% of baseline)
3) Token unit cost (USD/token or internal cost units)
   - Per-token cost change (target: <= +4% delta)
4) Failure rate (5xx/timeout)
   - Error rate (target: < 0.1% absolute increase)
5) Kernel preemption count / backpressure events
   - Absolute counts and rate per 10k requests (target: no increase beyond historical 95th percentile)
6) KV-cache hit rate and cold-start warmup curve
   - Hit-rate curve and time-to-90%-steady-state (target: within baseline envelope)
7) Quality-relevant regressions (output-level metrics, tying to evaluation harness)
   - BLEU/ROUGE/embedding-sim delta or application-specific eval set pass rate (target: no regression beyond alert threshold)

Each axis is graded as: pass / warn / fail with a short rationale field. The final decision is: fail if any axis is fail OR more than two axes are warn.

Telemetry SLA (minimal contract)
--------------------------------
The experiment telemetry must be available in the canonical observability stack within the following windows:
- Metrics (timeseries): within 1 minute of generation.
- Traces (sampled detailed traces): available in trace storage within 5 minutes.
- Kernel-level events (preempt, handoff, slot-abort): ingested and queryable within 30 minutes.
- Bench artifacts (microbench outputs, raw traces, sample payloads): uploaded to experiment object store within 1 hour.

Required telemetry labels/tags (schema)
- experiment_id: string
- run_id: string
- kernel_variant: string
- model_variant: string
- node_pool: {dedicated, hosted, private} (string)
- kvcache_epoch: integer (stringified)
- sequence_length_bucket: {short, medium, long} (string)
- traffic_cohort: string

Example metric naming conventions (timeseries)
- runtime.latency.p99.{kernel_variant}.{model_variant}
- runtime.kvcache.hit_rate.{node_pool}.{sequence_length_bucket}
- runtime.kernel.preempt.count.{kernel_variant}
- runtime.cost.per_token.{model_variant}

Experiment orchestration (procedural checklist)
------------------------------------------------
1) Define hypothesis and scorecard (owner: experiment author).
2) Register experiment in experiment ledger with experiment_id and target cohorts.
3) Provision canary capacity (node_pool and kernel_variant pinned) and record kernel_variant metadata.
4) Run synthetic benchmark (bench harness) for at least N=3 sustained loops; collect microbench metrics.
5) Shadow traffic or low-percentage real traffic for 24 hours (or N requests if traffic low).
6) Collect telemetry and compute scorecard axes.
7) If pass, promote to wider canary (30% traffic) and repeat step 6 for 48 hours.
8) If pass again, initiate rollout with staged increments and continuous monitoring.
9) If any stage fails, follow rollback runbook immediately.

Benchmarking harness: minimum requirements
----------------------------------------
Table: harness config (as a simple ASCII table)

| key                     | expected value / example                             | rationale |
|-------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| workload_profile        | conversational-chat, short-completion, reranking      | cover common shapes |
| request_rate            | synthetic: 100–200 RPS per node; canary: mirror live  | stress check |
| sequence_length_dist    | short: 1–32, medium: 33–256, long: 257+              | bucket coverage |
| replication_factor      | 3                                                  | reduce sample noise |
| telemetry_sampling_rate | 100% metrics, 10% traces, 1% full kernel traces      | cost tradeoff |
| run_duration           | microbench: 15m; full: 24h                          | warmup capture |

Benchmark outputs (artifacts)
- summary.json (scorecard axes, percentiles)
- traces.tar.gz (sampled traces mapped to requests)
- microbench.csv (per-run detailed timings)
- kernel_events.log (preempt/handoff records)

Kernel scheduling considerations for experiments
------------------------------------------------
- Avoid mixing kernel variants with very different preemption semantics in the same node pool during an experiment. This creates hard-to-interpret backpressure signals.
- When testing preemptive kernel variants, include a control cohort pinned to a non-preemptive kernel to differentiate scheduler-side artifacts from upstream workload variability.
- Record kernel credit accounting deltas and slot occupancy histograms. These must be included in the scorecard telemetry.

Profiling playbook (quick-action steps)
--------------------------------------
1) Detect: anomaly triage starts when p99 latency delta > +8% or error rate delta > +0.2%.
2) Collect: increase trace sampling for experiment_id to 100% for 10 minutes and trigger immediate kernel_events collection.
3) Snapshot: create a bench snapshot (replay corpus of 10k requests captured from canary).
4) Isolate: replicate offending workload in sandbox with a 1:1 traffic replay. Capture GPU occupancy, kernel latencies, preempt counts.
5) Hypothesize: use heap map and NVLink counters to check for interconnect or memory-copy hotspots.
6) Mitigate: throttle offending traffic cohort or fallback to baseline kernel_variant.
7) Remediate: iterate on kernel parameter, or revert rollout; document in the experiment ledger.

Sample CLI snippets
-------------------
- Run bench harness:
  
  ./bench-runner --experiment-id exp-2025-0423 --profile conversational-chat --rps 150 --duration 15m --output ./artifacts/exp-2025-0423/

- Increase trace sampling for experiment:
  
  rb-control telemetry set-sampling --experiment exp-2025-0423 --traces 100 --duration 10m

- Pull kernel events for run:
  
  rb-control events fetch --run exp-2025-0423 --type kernel_events --out kernel_events.log

Release gating and rollbacks
-----------------------------
Release gates are evaluated against the scorecard and telemetry SLA. Gate rules:
- Canary -> 30% -> wide rollout. Each gate requires: telemetry SLA met, scorecard pass, no outstanding high-severity known issues.
- Automatic rollback triggers:
  - p99 latency regression > 12% sustained for 10 minutes
  - error-rate increase > 0.5% absolute sustained for 5 minutes
  - kernel preemption rate spike beyond 5x baseline with correlated tail-latency increase

Known issues to monitor during experiments
-----------------------------------------
- KV-cache cold-start nonlinearity: some large-context workloads exhibit long tail warmup spikes. Mitigation: pre-warm cache with shadow traffic and set warmup threshold in deployment config.
- Slot-abort cascade under microburst: certain scheduler configurations can trigger cascading slot aborts, visible as short-lived error bursts. Mitigation: admission-controller smoothing and burst token bucket.
- NVLink blips impacting cross-GPU attention kernels: monitor nvlink_retransmit_rate metric and correlate with kernel latency. Mitigation: fall back to single-GPU execution for affected kernels.

Runbooks (links to actionable pages)
-----------------------------------
- Immediate rollback runbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix-2026.json
- Kernel preemption investigation: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/priority-inversion-detection-and-mitigation-playbook-2026.json

Owner and escalation
--------------------
- Experiment owner: author listed in experiment registration (primary contact).
- Runtime oncall: first escalation for perf regressions.
- Kernel scheduler engineering: secondary escalation for kernel-level anomalies.

Appendix: artifact examples
--------------------------
Example summary.json (minimal):

{
  "experiment_id": "exp-2025-0423",
  "run_id": "r1",
  "latency": {"p99": 152.3, "p95": 83.1, "mean": 31.4},
  "kvcache": {"hit_rate": 0.86, "time_to_90": 420},
  "kernel_preempt_count": 23
}

Contact and ownership
---------------------
For questions or to propose changes to the scorecard or telemetry SLA, open a PR against this doc and notify reviewers. For operational exceptions, request a documented exception with a proposed compensating control from runtime SRE.

Revision history
----------------
- 2024-04-15: initial draft (Asha Mehta)
- 2024-09-02: added kernel-event ingestion SLA and sample naming conventions (Ethan Park)
- 2025-11-02: tightened canary gating thresholds and added CLI snippets (Sofia Ruiz)

title: Runtime Experiment Scorecard and Telemetry SLA
preview:
Summary
-------
This document defines a unified experiment scorecard and a minimal telemetry SLA for runtime experiments, kernel-level canaries, and benchmark-driven rollouts. It bridges architecture, kernel scheduling, profiling, benchmarking, release gating, and on-call runbooks so that engineers and SREs can run reproducible experiments with clear pass/fail criteria.

Scope
-----
- Applies to: runtime feature experiments, kernel variant canaries, quantization rollouts, and model-variant A/B tests run in hosted and dedicated deployments.
- Excludes: user-facing product A/B tests unrelated to inference runtime behavior.

Why this exists
----------------
We observed variability between bench microbenchmarks and live traffic (tail latency regressions, subtle token-cost changes, KV-cache warmup differences). That undermines rollouts. The scorecard + telemetry SLA ensures experiments have signal fidelity, observability, and clear remediation steps.

Scorecard: pass/fail axes
------------------------
Each experiment run must record a scorecard. Minimum axes (all numeric, reported at 99th, 95th, and mean as indicated):

1) Latency (ms)
   - p99 latency vs baseline (target: <= +5% delta)
   - p95 latency vs baseline (target: <= +3% delta)
   - mean latency (target: <= +2% delta)
2) Throughput (req/s)
   - Sustained throughput under canonical load (target: >= 98% of baseline)
3) Token unit cost (USD/token or internal cost units)
   - Per-token cost change (target: <= +4% delta)
4) Failure rate (5xx/timeout)
   - Error rate (target: < 0.1% absolute increase)
5) Kernel preemption count / backpressure events
   - Absolute counts and rate per 10k requests (target: no increase beyond historical 95th percentile)
6) KV-cache hit rate and cold-start warmup curve
   - Hit-rate curve and time-to-90%-steady-state (target: within baseline envelope)
7) Quality-relevant regressions (output-level metrics, tying to evaluation harness)
   - BLEU/ROUGE/embedding-sim delta or application-specific eval set pass rate (target: no regression beyond alert threshold)

Each axis is graded as: pass / warn / fail with a short rationale field. The final decision is: fail if any axis is fail OR more than two axes are warn.

Telemetry SLA (minimal contract)
--------------------------------
The experiment telemetry must be available in the canonical observability stack within the following windows:
- Metrics (timeseries): within 1 minute of generation.
- Traces (sampled detailed traces): available in trace storage within 5 minutes.
- Kernel-level events (preempt, handoff, slot-abort): ingested and queryable within 30 minutes.
- Bench artifacts (microbench outputs, raw traces, sample payloads): uploaded to experiment object store within 1 hour.

Required telemetry labels/tags (schema)
- experiment_id: string
- run_id: string
- kernel_variant: string
- model_variant: string
- node_pool: {dedicated, hosted, private} (string)
- kvcache_epoch: integer (stringified)
- sequence_length_bucket: {short, medium, long} (string)
- traffic_cohort: string

Example metric naming conventions (timeseries)
- runtime.latency.p99.{kernel_variant}.{model_variant}
- runtime.kvcache.hit_rate.{node_pool}.{sequence_length_bucket}
- runtime.kernel.preempt.count.{kernel_variant}
- runtime.cost.per_token.{model_variant}

Experiment orchestration (procedural checklist)
------------------------------------------------
1) Define hypothesis and scorecard (owner: experiment author).
2) Register experiment in experiment ledger with experiment_id and target cohorts.
3) Provision canary capacity (node_pool and kernel_variant pinned) and record kernel_variant metadata.
4) Run synthetic benchmark (bench harness) for at least N=3 sustained loops; collect microbench metrics.
5) Shadow traffic or low-percentage real traffic for 24 hours (or N requests if traffic low).
6) Collect telemetry and compute scorecard axes.
7) If pass, promote to wider canary (30% traffic) and repeat step 6 for 48 hours.
8) If pass again, initiate rollout with staged increments and continuous monitoring.
9) If any stage fails, follow rollback runbook immediately.

Benchmarking harness: minimum requirements
----------------------------------------
Table: harness config (as a simple ASCII table)

| key                     | expected value / example                             | rationale |
|-------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| workload_profile        | conversational-chat, short-completion, reranking      | cover common shapes |
| request_rate            | synthetic: 100–200 RPS per node; canary: mirror live  | stress check |
| sequence_length_dist    | short: 1–32, medium: 33–256, long: 257+              | bucket coverage |
| replication_factor      | 3                                                  | reduce sample noise |
| telemetry_sampling_rate | 100% metrics, 10% traces, 1% full kernel traces      | cost tradeoff |
| run_duration           | microbench: 15m; full: 24h                          | warmup capture |

Benchmark outputs (artifacts)
- summary.json (scorecard axes, percentiles)
- traces.tar.gz (sampled traces mapped to requests)
- microbench.csv (per-run detailed timings)
- kernel_events.log (preempt/handoff records)

Kernel scheduling considerations for experiments
------------------------------------------------
- Avoid mixing kernel variants with very different preemption semantics in the same node pool during an experiment. This creates hard-to-interpret backpressure signals.
- When testing preemptive kernel variants, include a control cohort pinned to a non-preemptive kernel to differentiate scheduler-side artifacts from upstream workload variability.
- Record kernel credit accounting deltas and slot occupancy histograms. These must be included in the scorecard telemetry.

Profiling playbook (quick-action steps)
--------------------------------------
1) Detect: anomaly triage starts when p99 latency delta > +8% or error rate delta > +0.2%.
2) Collect: increase trace sampling for experiment_id to 100% for 10 minutes and trigger immediate kernel_events collection.
3) Snapshot: create a bench snapshot (replay corpus of 10k requests captured from canary).
4) Isolate: replicate offending workload in sandbox with a 1:1 traffic replay. Capture GPU occupancy, kernel latencies, preempt counts.
5) Hypothesize: use heap map and NVLink counters to check for interconnect or memory-copy hotspots.
6) Mitigate: throttle offending traffic cohort or fallback to baseline kernel_variant.
7) Remediate: iterate on kernel parameter, or revert rollout; document in the experiment ledger.

Sample CLI snippets
-------------------
- Run bench harness:
  
  ./bench-runner --experiment-id exp-2025-0423 --profile conversational-chat --rps 150 --duration 15m --output ./artifacts/exp-2025-0423/

- Increase trace sampling for experiment:
  
  rb-control telemetry set-sampling --experiment exp-2025-0423 --traces 100 --duration 10m

- Pull kernel events for run:
  
  rb-control events fetch --run exp-2025-0423 --type kernel_events --out kernel_events.log

Release gating and rollbacks
-----------------------------
Release gates are evaluated against the scorecard and telemetry SLA. Gate rules:
- Canary -> 30% -> wide rollout. Each gate requires: telemetry SLA met, scorecard pass, no outstanding high-severity known issues.
- Automatic rollback triggers:
  - p99 latency regression > 12% sustained for 10 minutes
  - error-rate increase > 0.5% absolute sustained for 5 minutes
  - kernel preemption rate spike beyond 5x baseline with correlated tail-latency increase

Known issues to monitor during experiments
-----------------------------------------
- KV-cache cold-start nonlinearity: some large-context workloads exhibit long tail warmup spikes. Mitigation: pre-warm cache with shadow traffic and set warmup threshold in deployment config.
- Slot-abort cascade under microburst: certain scheduler configurations can trigger cascading slot aborts, visible as short-lived error bursts. Mitigation: admission-controller smoothing and burst token bucket.
- NVLink blips impacting cross-GPU attention kernels: monitor nvlink_retransmit_rate metric and correlate with kernel latency. Mitigation: fall back to single-GPU execution for affected kernels.

Runbooks (links to actionable pages)
-----------------------------------
- Immediate rollback runbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix-2026.json
- Kernel preemption investigation: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/priority-inversion-detection-and-mitigation-playbook-2026.json

Owner and escalation
--------------------
- Experiment owner: author listed in experiment registration (primary contact).
- Runtime oncall: first escalation for perf regressions.
- Kernel scheduler engineering: secondary escalation for kernel-level anomalies.

Appendix: artifact examples
--------------------------
Example summary.json (minimal):

{
  "experiment_id": "exp-2025-0423",
  "run_id": "r1",
  "latency": {"p99": 152.3, "p95": 83.1, "mean": 31.4},
  "kvcache": {"hit_rate": 0.86, "time_to_90": 420},
  "kernel_preempt_count": 23
}

Contact and ownership
---------------------
For questions or to propose changes to the scorecard or telemetry SLA, open a PR against this doc and notify reviewers. For operational exceptions, request a documented exception with a proposed compensating control from runtime SRE.

Revision history
----------------
- 2024-04-15: initial draft (Asha Mehta)
- 2024-09-02: added kernel-event ingestion SLA and sample naming conventions (Ethan Park)
- 2025-11-02: tightened canary gating thresholds and added CLI snippets (Sofia Ruiz)

source.title: Fidelity budgeting and runtime cost governor manual
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend across mixed workloads.

Intended audience
- Runtime and scheduler engineers
- Product owners for Dedicated/Private customers
- SRE and capacity-planning teams
- Benchmark and profiling engineers

Why this exists
- Inference cost is variable by model variant, sequence length, batching shape and kernel choices.
- We need a single, auditable mechanism to trade fidelity for cost under pressure (autoscale, thermal events, capacity constraints).
- RCG ties scheduling signals, kernel hints, and quantization/variant routing decisions to a budget and provides a deterministic downgrade strategy.

Principles
- Predictability: budget enforcement must be explainable and reproducible.
- Observability: every fidelity decision must be logged with provenance (trigger, score, pre/post metrics).
- Minimize user impact: preservation of critical tokens and short requests is prioritized over long/low-priority generation.
- Fast rollback: policy changes must be reversible within a single canary window.

Key concepts and terminology
- Fidelity budget: numeric allowance per time window (tokens or token-equivalents) that a tenant/route can spend.
- Fidelity credit: per-request currency derived from model variant cost and sequence projection.
- Degradation ladder: ordered list of fidelity actions (e.g., small-quantize -> shorter-prefix-cache -> model-variant -> response-truncation).
- Governor evaluation window: default 30s sliding window for budget accounting.

Metrics, signals and SLOs
- Primary SLOs: 99th-pct tail latency <= 250ms (interactive tier), ERR_RATE <= 0.5%
- Budget signals: token_consumed, credit_burn_rate, on_gpu_slots, queue_depth, kernel_wait_time_ms
- Observability artifacts: fidelity_decision events (json), pre/post latency histograms, regression score (quality delta vs baseline prompts).

Architecture overview
- Inputs: request classifier (priority, route), model catalog pricing table (tokens / cost), runtime telemetry (slot usage, kernel queue), profiling hints (sequence length distribution).
- Decision engine: lightweight policy evaluator that produces an ordered list of degradation actions and an urgency score.
- Enforcers: scheduler (admission control and slot assignment), kernel adapter (per-request kernel flags), router (model variant routing), kvcache layer (prefix eviction hints).
- Audit sink: fidelity-decision store and metrics exporter.

Budgeting controls and policy primitives
- static_budget: fixed tokens per-minute (example: 1e6 tokens/min)
- proportional_budget: fraction of cluster budget for shared pools (0.02 = 2%)
- emergency_buffer: reserved credits only used when latency or error budgets are breached

Simple policy example (YAML)
--------------------------
policy_name: interactive-default
owner: team-runtime
window_seconds: 30
budget_type: token_quota
budget_amount: 50000
degradation_ladder:
  - action: prefer-quantized-variant
    min_quality_delta: -0.02
  - action: reduce-max-output-tokens
    reduction_percent: 25
  - action: route-to-small-variant
    acceptable_loss: -0.05
priority_overrides:
  - route: /chat/completion
    min_reserve: 2000

Scheduler integration
- Admission checks use the RCG decision as a prefilter. If a tenant is above budget, admission returns PROBATION with a recommended action list.
- Scheduler applies soft-slot labels (e.g., fidelity=low) to place downgraded requests on consolidation pools to reduce interference.
- Kernel hints are conveyed via request metadata: {kernel_hint: "prefetch_off,quantize_a2"}.

Profiling & benchmarking guidance
- For each candidate policy change run:
  1) Local microbenchmark with synthetic sequences (short, medium, long) to measure latency and kernel divergence.
  2) Shadow traffic pass (1 hour minimum) to observe real distribution effects.
  3) Quality regression suite (prompt set) measuring ROUGE/BLEU and model-specific evals.
- Required artifacts to land a policy: before/after cost-per-token, latency p50/p95/p99, quality regressions per prompt set, and fidelity_decision trace samples.

Operational runbook: budget breach (quick triage)
1) Detection
  - Alert: fidelity_budget_burn_rate > 120% for 1 minute or fidelity_decision_failure_count > 5/min
2) Immediate actions (first 5 minutes)
  - Escalate to runtime-oncall and set channel #runtime-fidelity with incident tag.
  - Switch policy to safe-mode: static_budget=nominal/4, enforce-first-ladder-only.
  - Create shadow traces for 5 minutes: set header X-Trace-Fidelity-Detail=true.
3) Investigation (5–30 minutes)
  - Check recent deployments: kernel or scheduler changes in the last 30 minutes (kubectl/CI links).
  - Correlate with capacity metrics: GPU_utilization, kernel_wait_time_ms, queue_depth.
  - Run profiling job: sample 2% of traffic for deep trace (use trace-capture CLI below).
4) Mitigation (30–120 minutes)
  - If degraded by thermal or hardware: move high-priority tenants to reserved pools.
  - If caused by regression: revert policy or kernel flag and roll forward safe-mode config.
  - If intentional load spike: negotiate temporary budget increase with product (document in audit).
5) Postmortem requirements
  - Within 48 hours: publish summary, root cause, timeline, and any policy code changes.

Trace capture CLI (example)
- - -
# Capture 10k fidelity_decision events for tenant=acme
runtctl trace capture --tenant acme --events fidelity_decision --limit 10000 --out /tmp/acme-fidelity-trace.ndjson

Policy rollout checklist
1) Author policy with rationale and expected cost delta.
2) Unit tests for policy parser and fallback actions.
3) Local benchmark (p50/p99, cost delta).
4) Canary deploy (1% traffic, 30m), monitor fidelity_decisions and latency SLOs.
5) 10% shadow for 2 hours, examine quality regressions.
6) Gradual 20% -> 50% -> 100% steps with automated rollback thresholds (p99 latency +10% or error_rate > 0.2%).

Example governance matrix (text table)
- Name: interactive-default | Owner: team-runtime | Window: 30s | Budget: 50k tokens
- Rollout gate: p99_latency_delta <= +10ms & quality_regression <= 1%

Telemetry and audit
- fidelity_decision schema (logged): request_id, tenant_id, route, budget_window_start, budget_remaining, chosen_action, reason_code, decision_score, timestamp
- Export to: metrics (Prom), events (kafka topic: fidelity-decisions), long-term store for 90d.

Ownership & contacts
- Policy author: Ava Chen (runtime@redwood.ai)
- Scheduler integration owner: Marcus Patel
- Oncall for fidelity incidents: runtime-oncall rotation (oncall@redwood.ai)

Appendix A — configuration samples
- Governor config (JSON snippet):
{
  "policy_name":"interactive-default",
  "window_seconds":30,
  "budget_amount":50000,
  "degradation_ladder":["prefer-quantized-variant","reduce-max-output-tokens","route-to-small-variant"]
}

Appendix B — quality regression example
- For the "prefer-quantized-variant" action we allow a maximum QA score delta of -0.03 as measured on the interactive-quality prompt set. If measured delta exceeds threshold during canary, the canary aborts.

Related work and references
- See runtime-architecture predictive-prefetch doc for sequence projection inputs.
- See benchmarking suite docs for how to generate the required before/after artifacts.

Change log
- 2025-01-15: created (A. Chen)
- 2025-06-12: added emergency_buffer section and scheduler hints (M. Patel)
- 2025-10-02: clarified rollout gates and updated trace capture CLI (L. Ortiz)

End of document

- Primary SLOs: 99th-pct tail latency <= 250ms (interactive tier), ERR_RATE <= 0.5%
- Budget signals: token_consumed, credit_burn_rate, on_gpu_slots, queue_depth, kernel_wait_time_ms
- Observability artifacts: fidelity_decision events (json), pre/post latency histograms, regression score (quality delta vs baseline prompts).

Architecture overview
- Inputs: request classifier (priority, route), model catalog pricing table (tokens / cost), runtime telemetry (slot usage, kernel queue), profiling hints (sequence length distribution).
- Decision engine: lightweight policy evaluator that produces an ordered list of degradation actions and an urgency score.
- Enforcers: scheduler (admission control and slot assignment), kernel adapter (per-request kernel flags), router (model variant routing), kvcache layer (prefix eviction hints).
- Audit sink: fidelity-decision store and metrics exporter.

Budgeting controls and policy primitives
- static_budget: fixed tokens per-minute (example: 1e6 tokens/min)
- proportional_budget: fraction of cluster budget for shared pools (0.02 = 2%)
- emergency_buffer: reserved credits only used when latency or error budgets are breached

Simple policy example (YAML)
--------------------------
policy_name: interactive-default
owner: team-runtime
window_seconds: 30
budget_type: token_quota
budget_amount: 50000
degradation_ladder:
  - action: prefer-quantized-variant
    min_quality_delta: -0.02
  - action: reduce-max-output-tokens
    reduction_percent: 25
  - action: route-to-small-variant
    acceptable_loss: -0.05
priority_overrides:
  - route: /chat/completion
    min_reserve: 2000

Scheduler integration
- Admission checks use the RCG decision as a prefilter. If a tenant is above budget, admission returns PROBATION with a recommended action list.
- Scheduler applies soft-slot labels (e.g., fidelity=low) to place downgraded requests on consolidation pools to reduce interference.
- Kernel hints are conveyed via request metadata: {kernel_hint: "prefetch_off,quantize_a2"}.

Profiling & benchmarking guidance
- For each candidate policy change run:
  1) Local microbenchmark with synthetic sequences (short, medium, long) to measure latency and kernel divergence.
  2) Shadow traffic pass (1 hour minimum) to observe real distribution effects.
  3) Quality regression suite (prompt set) measuring ROUGE/BLEU and model-specific evals.
- Required artifacts to land a policy: before/after cost-per-token, latency p50/p95/p99, quality regressions per prompt set, and fidelity_decision trace samples.

Operational runbook: budget breach (quick triage)
1) Detection
  - Alert: fidelity_budget_burn_rate > 120% for 1 minute or fidelity_decision_failure_count > 5/min
2) Immediate actions (first 5 minutes)
  - Escalate to runtime-oncall and set channel #runtime-fidelity with incident tag.
  - Switch policy to safe-mode: static_budget=nominal/4, enforce-first-ladder-only.
  - Create shadow traces for 5 minutes: set header X-Trace-Fidelity-Detail=true.
3) Investigation (5–30 minutes)
  - Check recent deployments: kernel or scheduler changes in the last 30 minutes (kubectl/CI links).
  - Correlate with capacity metrics: GPU_utilization, kernel_wait_time_ms, queue_depth.
  - Run profiling job: sample 2% of traffic for deep trace (use trace-capture CLI below).
4) Mitigation (30–120 minutes)
  - If degraded by thermal or hardware: move high-priority tenants to reserved pools.
  - If caused by regression: revert policy or kernel flag and roll forward safe-mode config.
  - If intentional load spike: negotiate temporary budget increase with product (document in audit).
5) Postmortem requirements
  - Within 48 hours: publish summary, root cause, timeline, and any policy code changes.

Trace capture CLI (example)
- - -
# Capture 10k fidelity_decision events for tenant=acme
runtctl trace capture --tenant acme --events fidelity_decision --limit 10000 --out /tmp/acme-fidelity-trace.ndjson

Policy rollout checklist
1) Author policy with rationale and expected cost delta.
2) Unit tests for policy parser and fallback actions.
3) Local benchmark (p50/p99, cost delta).
4) Canary deploy (1% traffic, 30m), monitor fidelity_decisions and latency SLOs.
5) 10% shadow for 2 hours, examine quality regressions.
6) Gradual 20% -> 50% -> 100% steps with automated rollback thresholds (p99 latency +10% or error_rate > 0.2%).

Example governance matrix (text table)
- Name: interactive-default | Owner: team-runtime | Window: 30s | Budget: 50k tokens
- Rollout gate: p99_latency_delta <= +10ms & quality_regression <= 1%

Telemetry and audit
- fidelity_decision schema (logged): request_id, tenant_id, route, budget_window_start, budget_remaining, chosen_action, reason_code, decision_score, timestamp
- Export to: metrics (Prom), events (kafka topic: fidelity-decisions), long-term store for 90d.

Ownership & contacts
- Policy author: Ava Chen (runtime@redwood.ai)
- Scheduler integration owner: Marcus Patel
- Oncall for fidelity incidents: runtime-oncall rotation (oncall@redwood.ai)

Appendix A — configuration samples
- Governor config (JSON snippet):
{
  "policy_name":"interactive-default",
  "window_seconds":30,
  "budget_amount":50000,
  "degradation_ladder":["prefer-quantized-variant","reduce-max-output-tokens","route-to-small-variant"]
}

Appendix B — quality regression example
- For the "prefer-quantized-variant" action we allow a maximum QA score delta of -0.03 as measured on the interactive-quality prompt set. If measured delta exceeds threshold during canary, the canary aborts.

Related work and references
- See runtime-architecture predictive-prefetch doc for sequence projection inputs.
- See benchmarking suite docs for how to generate the required before/after artifacts.

Change log
- 2025-01-15: created (A. Chen)
- 2025-06-12: added emergency_buffer section and scheduler hints (M. Patel)
- 2025-10-02: clarified rollout gates and updated trace capture CLI (L. Ortiz)

End of document
text:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend across mixed workloads.

Intended audience
- Runtime and scheduler engineers
- Product owners for Dedicated/Private customers
- SRE and capacity-planning teams
- Benchmark and profiling engineers

Why this exists
- Inference cost is variable by model variant, sequence length, batching shape and kernel choices.
- We need a single, auditable mechanism to trade fidelity for cost under pressure (autoscale, thermal events, capacity constraints).
- RCG ties scheduling signals, kernel hints, and quantization/variant routing decisions to a budget and provides a deterministic downgrade strategy.

Principles
- Predictability: budget enforcement must be explainable and reproducible.
- Observability: every fidelity decision must be logged with provenance (trigger, score, pre/post metrics).
- Minimize user impact: preservation of critical tokens and short requests is prioritized over long/low-priority generation.
- Fast rollback: policy changes must be reversible within a single canary window.

Key concepts and terminology
- Fidelity budget: numeric allowance per time window (tokens or token-equivalents) that a tenant/route can spend.
- Fidelity credit: per-request currency derived from model variant cost and sequence projection.
- Degradation ladder: ordered list of fidelity actions (e.g., small-quantize -> shorter-prefix-cache -> model-variant -> response-truncation).
- Governor evaluation window: default 30s sliding window for budget accounting.

Metrics, signals and SLOs

source.title: Execution Fidelity Probe Catalog and Operational Playbook
source.author: Priya Kaur
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This document catalogs the set of small, reproducible probes we run against the serving runtime to measure execution fidelity, kernel-scheduling sensitivity, and workload-driven regression risk. The catalog is intended for SREs, runtime engineers, and experimenters who need a concise set of checks to detect performance, correctness, or quality drift after kernel changes, model swaps, or deployment topology updates.

Scope: probes exercise kernel-dispatcher, kvcache behavior, sequence-length scaling, quantization alternatives, and cross-tenant memory policies. Each probe entry includes: objective, trigger conditions, exact workload or CLI invocation, expected telemetry signatures, SLO thresholds, and immediate remediation runbook.

Ownership and cadence
- Owners: runtime-observability (primary), kernel-scheduling (secondary)
- Run cadence: nightly synthetic sweep + post-deploy smoke (canary) + ad-hoc before kernel rollouts
- Storage: probe results are written to metrics: fidelity.probe.<probe_name>.{latency,cost,error_rate} and traces under trace.service=runtime-probe

Probe Catalog (short index)
1) kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy-probe
2) short-seq-preemption-sensitivity
3) quantized-degrade-compare
4) multi-tenant-memory-eviction-sim
5) kernel-context-switch-density
6) small-burst-concurrency-spike

Detailed probes
---
Probe: kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy-probe
Objective: Validate that kvcache warmup reduces repeated prompt token costs and that prefetch hints are honored by the kernel scheduler.
Trigger: nightly sweep; also on any change to kvcache prefetch logic or cache-shard topology.
Workload (CLI example):
  ./tools/probes/run_probe.sh --name kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy --model gpt-open-2B --requests 500 --seq-dist small
Expected telemetry: token_save_ratio >= 0.15, median_latency_drop >= 10ms for warm requests, cache_miss_rate < 5%.
SLOs: token_save_ratio >= 0.12 (warning at 0.10).
Remediation (quick):
  1) Confirm kvcache shard placement with `kubectl get pods -l app=kvcache -o wide` and verify annotations kernel.kvcache/warm=true.
  2) Check dispatcher logs `prod-dispatcher` for prefetch hints being accepted.
  3) If prefetch hints rejected, rollback last kernel build or apply kernel flag `--kvcache-accept-prefetch=true` behind feature-flag.
  4) Re-run probe and escalate to kernel-scheduling if persistent.

---
Probe: short-seq-preemption-sensitivity
Objective: Measure tail-latency impact for sub-64-token sequences when preemption and cooperative-shepherding are active.
Trigger: before any scheduler-change rollout.
Workload: 2000 short requests at 5rps with randomized priority headers. Example request snippet: `{"input": "translate: hello" , "priority": "low"}`
Expected telemetry: 99.9p latency < 60ms on priority=high, 99.9p < 120ms on priority=low. Preemption_drop_count = 0.
SLOs: If high-priority 99.9p exceeds 80ms, block rollout.
Remediation: runbook steps:
  - Check scheduler queue-lengths and kernel credit usage: `metrics.query('kernel.credit.usage')`
  - Temporarily increase scheduler window for high-priority classes: patch config `scheduler.window_ms=40`
  - If credit starvation observed, throttle low-priority ingress or scale dedicated GPU pool.

---
Probe: quantized-degrade-compare
Objective: Detect quality regressions introduced by new quantized-kernel paths (q8/q4 variants) that change decoding probability mass.
Trigger: model-variant onboarding, quantization profile changes.
Workload: run eval-set N=500 examples; compare LM log-prob delta and top-1 answer parity. CLI example:
  ./tools/eval/run_eval.py --evalset fidelity-qprobe-v2 --model-variant gpt-open-2B-q8 --baseline gpt-open-2B-fp16 --metric top1_parity
Expected telemetry: top1_parity >= 0.985 and mean_logprob_delta >= -0.06.
SLOs: parity >= 0.98 required for automatic rollout.
Remediation: revert to fp16 kernel for the variant and open ticket in kernel-regressions with attached eval trace.

---
Probe: multi-tenant-memory-eviction-sim
Objective: Simulate concurrent tenants with adversarial memory patterns to validate eviction fairness and tenant isolation.
Trigger: cluster config changes, eviction logic updates.
Workload description: synthetic tenants A..H with heavy KV allocations and periodic large context requests; run for 30 minutes.
Telemetry: cross_tenant_evictions < 0.5% of requests; tenant_p99_memory_spike_delta < 10%.
Runbook: if violations => pin affected tenant to isolation pool and engage capacity-planning.

---
Probe: kernel-context-switch-density
Objective: Surface regressions where kernel plugin handoffs increase GPU context-switch overhead.
Trigger: new kernel plugin merges or handshake changes.
Workload: synthetic long sequences with interleaved plugin calls; sample traces.
Expected telemetry: context_switch_per_second <= 40 on p99; if > 60, investigate plugin handshake path.

---
Probe: small-burst-concurrency-spike
Objective: Ensure admission controller and cooperative preemption prevent cascading tail latencies during short microbursts.
Trigger: load balancer or admission-controller changes.
Workload: replay recorded microburst trace from production mirrored traffic (use `probes/microburst-corpus`).
Key metric: cascade_failure_ratio < 0.01; upstream_error_rate unaffected.

Operational patterns and probing pipelines
- Synthetic sweep pipeline: nightly-runner constructs a job per probe; jobs are orchestrated in Airflow under DAG: runtime.probes.daily.
- Canary smoke: post-deploy job runs quick set (kv-prefetch, short-seq) and posts alert if any SLO violated.
- Signal aggregation: we maintain consolidated view in Grafana dashboard `Runtime / Probes / Fidelity L0`. Probe-run logs are retained 30 days.

Telemetry signatures and anomaly detection
- Each probe emits three canonical metrics: <probe>.latency_ms, <probe>.error_rate, <probe>.quality_delta. Use anomaly detector `anomaly-engine-v2` with thresholding: 3-sigma over a 14-day baseline for latency, and 5% relative delta for quality metrics.
- Trace anchors: every probe attaches `probe.name` and `probe.run_id` to spans to enable quick trace drilldowns.

Benchmarking and profiling integration
- Bench harness: the probes are complementary to the microbenchmark suite. For kernel authors, run `./bench/runner --probe kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy --platform g4dn` to collect kernel-level counters.
- Profiling playbook excerpt:
  1) Start with stratified tracing (20% sample) to correlate scheduler decisions to kernel latency.
  2) Add per-kernel counters: prefetch_accepted, prefetch_rejected, kvcache_eviction_count.
  3) Use `profiling/convert_to_hdf5.py` to convert raw traces for offline analysis.

Release gating and automated blocking rules
- Pre-merge gating: any PR touching kernel-scheduler or kvcache must include probe-run results on bb-stage for the three core probes.
- Canary gating: if short-seq-preemption-sensitivity or kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy fail in canary, the release is automatically paused by runtime-ci and the rollout coordinator is notified.

Known issue matrix (current as of last_updated)
- Issue: intermittent prefetch hint rejection on gtx-cluster-3a after kernel v1.4.2. Symptom: kv-prefetch warmup token_save_ratio drop to 6%. Workaround: force-enable legacy-prefetch-mode via config map. Owner: kernel-scheduling.
- Issue: rare priority inversion when low-priority background jobs are pinned to the same GPU as high-priority inference, p99 increases ~30ms. Workaround: enforce strict affinity for high-priority with `affinity.strict=true`. Owner: runtime-core.

Runbook excerpts (for urgent triage)
1) Canary probe fails (automated alert)
  - Acknowledge alert in PagerDuty and post to #runtime-canary with run_id.
  - Run `./tools/probes/replay.sh --run-id <run_id> --store traces/tmp` to capture failing trace.
  - If probe shows scheduler credit exhaustion, scale GPU pool or patch scheduler window.
  - If quality metric dropped (quantized-degrade-compare), revert model variant and open unblock in release pipeline.

2) Production spike resembling microburst
  - Check `fidelity.probe.small-burst-concurrency-spike.error_rate` and compare to last 2 hours.
  - If cascade_failure_ratio > 0.01: enable burst-mitigation-mode (toggle in config) which reduces admission concurrency by 30%.

Appendices
A) Probe CLI reference
- `./tools/probes/run_probe.sh --name <probe> --model <model> --requests <N> --seq-dist {small,medium,long} --priority {low,medium,high}`
- Example output: JSON lines with keys: run_id, probe_name, metric_snapshots.

B) Minimal schema for probe telemetry (for downstream consumers)
- All fields are strings / numbers. Example (JSON):
  {"run_id":"20250901-kv-01", "probe_name":"kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy", "metrics": [{"name":"token_save_ratio", "value": 0.17}], "tags": ["canary", "g4dn"] }

C) FAQs
Q: How often should we add new probes?
A: Add probes when introducing a new execution path (kernel plugin, quantization path, eviction policy) or when a new class of regressions is observed. Keep the catalog lean (<= 20 probes).

References and links
- Runtime decision graph: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Latency fingerprinting guide: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/latency-fingerprint-kernel-sensitivity-index-2026

Document change log
- 2025-03-12: created by Priya Kaur
- 2025-06-18: added multi-tenant-memory-eviction-sim probe and runbook excerpts (review by Daniel Mendoza)
- 2025-09-01: updated known issue matrix and CLI examples (review by Ava Chen)

End of document

- Each probe emits three canonical metrics: <probe>.latency_ms, <probe>.error_rate, <probe>.quality_delta. Use anomaly detector `anomaly-engine-v2` with thresholding: 3-sigma over a 14-day baseline for latency, and 5% relative delta for quality metrics.
- Trace anchors: every probe attaches `probe.name` and `probe.run_id` to spans to enable quick trace drilldowns.

Benchmarking and profiling integration
- Bench harness: the probes are complementary to the microbenchmark suite. For kernel authors, run `./bench/runner --probe kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy --platform g4dn` to collect kernel-level counters.
- Profiling playbook excerpt:
  1) Start with stratified tracing (20% sample) to correlate scheduler decisions to kernel latency.
  2) Add per-kernel counters: prefetch_accepted, prefetch_rejected, kvcache_eviction_count.
  3) Use `profiling/convert_to_hdf5.py` to convert raw traces for offline analysis.

Release gating and automated blocking rules
- Pre-merge gating: any PR touching kernel-scheduler or kvcache must include probe-run results on bb-stage for the three core probes.
- Canary gating: if short-seq-preemption-sensitivity or kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy fail in canary, the release is automatically paused by runtime-ci and the rollout coordinator is notified.

Known issue matrix (current as of last_updated)
- Issue: intermittent prefetch hint rejection on gtx-cluster-3a after kernel v1.4.2. Symptom: kv-prefetch warmup token_save_ratio drop to 6%. Workaround: force-enable legacy-prefetch-mode via config map. Owner: kernel-scheduling.
- Issue: rare priority inversion when low-priority background jobs are pinned to the same GPU as high-priority inference, p99 increases ~30ms. Workaround: enforce strict affinity for high-priority with `affinity.strict=true`. Owner: runtime-core.

Runbook excerpts (for urgent triage)
1) Canary probe fails (automated alert)
  - Acknowledge alert in PagerDuty and post to #runtime-canary with run_id.
  - Run `./tools/probes/replay.sh --run-id <run_id> --store traces/tmp` to capture failing trace.
  - If probe shows scheduler credit exhaustion, scale GPU pool or patch scheduler window.
  - If quality metric dropped (quantized-degrade-compare), revert model variant and open unblock in release pipeline.

2) Production spike resembling microburst
  - Check `fidelity.probe.small-burst-concurrency-spike.error_rate` and compare to last 2 hours.
  - If cascade_failure_ratio > 0.01: enable burst-mitigation-mode (toggle in config) which reduces admission concurrency by 30%.

Appendices
A) Probe CLI reference
- `./tools/probes/run_probe.sh --name <probe> --model <model> --requests <N> --seq-dist {small,medium,long} --priority {low,medium,high}`
- Example output: JSON lines with keys: run_id, probe_name, metric_snapshots.

B) Minimal schema for probe telemetry (for downstream consumers)
- All fields are strings / numbers. Example (JSON):
  {"run_id":"20250901-kv-01", "probe_name":"kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy", "metrics": [{"name":"token_save_ratio", "value": 0.17}], "tags": ["canary", "g4dn"] }

C) FAQs
Q: How often should we add new probes?
A: Add probes when introducing a new execution path (kernel plugin, quantization path, eviction policy) or when a new class of regressions is observed. Keep the catalog lean (<= 20 probes).

References and links
- Runtime decision graph: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Latency fingerprinting guide: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/latency-fingerprint-kernel-sensitivity-index-2026

Document change log
- 2025-03-12: created by Priya Kaur
- 2025-06-18: added multi-tenant-memory-eviction-sim probe and runbook excerpts (review by Daniel Mendoza)
- 2025-09-01: updated known issue matrix and CLI examples (review by Ava Chen)

End of document
text:
Overview

Purpose: This document catalogs the set of small, reproducible probes we run against the serving runtime to measure execution fidelity, kernel-scheduling sensitivity, and workload-driven regression risk. The catalog is intended for SREs, runtime engineers, and experimenters who need a concise set of checks to detect performance, correctness, or quality drift after kernel changes, model swaps, or deployment topology updates.

Scope: probes exercise kernel-dispatcher, kvcache behavior, sequence-length scaling, quantization alternatives, and cross-tenant memory policies. Each probe entry includes: objective, trigger conditions, exact workload or CLI invocation, expected telemetry signatures, SLO thresholds, and immediate remediation runbook.

Ownership and cadence
- Owners: runtime-observability (primary), kernel-scheduling (secondary)
- Run cadence: nightly synthetic sweep + post-deploy smoke (canary) + ad-hoc before kernel rollouts
- Storage: probe results are written to metrics: fidelity.probe.<probe_name>.{latency,cost,error_rate} and traces under trace.service=runtime-probe

Probe Catalog (short index)
1) kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy-probe
2) short-seq-preemption-sensitivity
3) quantized-degrade-compare
4) multi-tenant-memory-eviction-sim
5) kernel-context-switch-density
6) small-burst-concurrency-spike

Detailed probes
---
Probe: kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy-probe
Objective: Validate that kvcache warmup reduces repeated prompt token costs and that prefetch hints are honored by the kernel scheduler.
Trigger: nightly sweep; also on any change to kvcache prefetch logic or cache-shard topology.
Workload (CLI example):
  ./tools/probes/run_probe.sh --name kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy --model gpt-open-2B --requests 500 --seq-dist small
Expected telemetry: token_save_ratio >= 0.15, median_latency_drop >= 10ms for warm requests, cache_miss_rate < 5%.
SLOs: token_save_ratio >= 0.12 (warning at 0.10).
Remediation (quick):
  1) Confirm kvcache shard placement with `kubectl get pods -l app=kvcache -o wide` and verify annotations kernel.kvcache/warm=true.
  2) Check dispatcher logs `prod-dispatcher` for prefetch hints being accepted.
  3) If prefetch hints rejected, rollback last kernel build or apply kernel flag `--kvcache-accept-prefetch=true` behind feature-flag.
  4) Re-run probe and escalate to kernel-scheduling if persistent.

---
Probe: short-seq-preemption-sensitivity
Objective: Measure tail-latency impact for sub-64-token sequences when preemption and cooperative-shepherding are active.
Trigger: before any scheduler-change rollout.
Workload: 2000 short requests at 5rps with randomized priority headers. Example request snippet: `{"input": "translate: hello" , "priority": "low"}`
Expected telemetry: 99.9p latency < 60ms on priority=high, 99.9p < 120ms on priority=low. Preemption_drop_count = 0.
SLOs: If high-priority 99.9p exceeds 80ms, block rollout.
Remediation: runbook steps:
  - Check scheduler queue-lengths and kernel credit usage: `metrics.query('kernel.credit.usage')`
  - Temporarily increase scheduler window for high-priority classes: patch config `scheduler.window_ms=40`
  - If credit starvation observed, throttle low-priority ingress or scale dedicated GPU pool.

---
Probe: quantized-degrade-compare
Objective: Detect quality regressions introduced by new quantized-kernel paths (q8/q4 variants) that change decoding probability mass.
Trigger: model-variant onboarding, quantization profile changes.
Workload: run eval-set N=500 examples; compare LM log-prob delta and top-1 answer parity. CLI example:
  ./tools/eval/run_eval.py --evalset fidelity-qprobe-v2 --model-variant gpt-open-2B-q8 --baseline gpt-open-2B-fp16 --metric top1_parity
Expected telemetry: top1_parity >= 0.985 and mean_logprob_delta >= -0.06.
SLOs: parity >= 0.98 required for automatic rollout.
Remediation: revert to fp16 kernel for the variant and open ticket in kernel-regressions with attached eval trace.

---
Probe: multi-tenant-memory-eviction-sim
Objective: Simulate concurrent tenants with adversarial memory patterns to validate eviction fairness and tenant isolation.
Trigger: cluster config changes, eviction logic updates.
Workload description: synthetic tenants A..H with heavy KV allocations and periodic large context requests; run for 30 minutes.
Telemetry: cross_tenant_evictions < 0.5% of requests; tenant_p99_memory_spike_delta < 10%.
Runbook: if violations => pin affected tenant to isolation pool and engage capacity-planning.

---
Probe: kernel-context-switch-density
Objective: Surface regressions where kernel plugin handoffs increase GPU context-switch overhead.
Trigger: new kernel plugin merges or handshake changes.
Workload: synthetic long sequences with interleaved plugin calls; sample traces.
Expected telemetry: context_switch_per_second <= 40 on p99; if > 60, investigate plugin handshake path.

---
Probe: small-burst-concurrency-spike
Objective: Ensure admission controller and cooperative preemption prevent cascading tail latencies during short microbursts.
Trigger: load balancer or admission-controller changes.
Workload: replay recorded microburst trace from production mirrored traffic (use `probes/microburst-corpus`).
Key metric: cascade_failure_ratio < 0.01; upstream_error_rate unaffected.

Operational patterns and probing pipelines
- Synthetic sweep pipeline: nightly-runner constructs a job per probe; jobs are orchestrated in Airflow under DAG: runtime.probes.daily.
- Canary smoke: post-deploy job runs quick set (kv-prefetch, short-seq) and posts alert if any SLO violated.
- Signal aggregation: we maintain consolidated view in Grafana dashboard `Runtime / Probes / Fidelity L0`. Probe-run logs are retained 30 days.

Telemetry signatures and anomaly detection
- Each probe emits three canonical metrics: <probe>.latency_ms, <probe>.error_rate, <probe>.quality_delta. Use anomaly detector `anomaly-engine-v2` with thresholding: 3-sigma over a 14-day baseline for latency, and 5% relative delta for quality metrics.
- Trace anchors: every probe attaches `probe.name` and `probe.run_id` to spans to enable quick trace drilldowns.

Benchmarking and profiling integration
- Bench harness: the probes are complementary to the microbenchmark suite. For kernel authors, run `./bench/runner --probe kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy --platform g4dn` to collect kernel-level counters.
- Profiling playbook excerpt:
  1) Start with stratified tracing (20% sample) to correlate scheduler decisions to kernel latency.
  2) Add per-kernel counters: prefetch_accepted, prefetch_rejected, kvcache_eviction_count.
  3) Use `profiling/convert_to_hdf5.py` to convert raw traces for offline analysis.

Release gating and automated blocking rules
- Pre-merge gating: any PR touching kernel-scheduler or kvcache must include probe-run results on bb-stage for the three core probes.
- Canary gating: if short-seq-preemption-sensitivity or kv-prefetch-warmup-entropy fail in canary, the release is automatically paused by runtime-ci and the rollout coordinator is notified.

Known issue matrix (current as of last_updated)

- Issue: rare priority inversion when low-priority background jobs are pinned to the same GPU as high-priority inference, p99 increases ~30ms. Workaround: enforce strict affinity for high-priority with `affinity.strict=true`. Owner: runtime-core.

Runbook excerpts (for urgent triage)
1) Canary probe fails (automated alert)
  - Acknowledge alert in PagerDuty and post to #runtime-canary with run_id.
  - Run `./tools/probes/replay.sh --run-id <run_id> --store traces/tmp` to capture failing trace.
  - If probe shows scheduler credit exhaustion, scale GPU pool or patch scheduler window.
  - If quality metric dropped (quantized-degrade-compare), revert model variant and open unblock in release pipeline.

2) Production spike resembling microburst

  - If cascade_failure_ratio > 0.01: enable burst-mitigation-mode (toggle in config) which reduces admission concurrency by 30%.

Appendices
A) Probe CLI reference
- `./tools/probes/run_probe.sh --name <probe> --model <model> --requests <N> --seq-dist {small,medium,long} --priority {low,medium,high}`
- Example output: JSON lines with keys: run_id, probe_name, metric_snapshots.

B) Minimal schema for probe telemetry (for downstream consumers)
- All fields are strings / numbers. Example (JSON):

A: Add probes when introducing a new execution path (kernel plugin, quantization path, eviction policy) or when a new class of regressions is observed. Keep the catalog lean (<= 20 probes).

source.title: Runtime design intent and contracts catalog
source.author: Lena Thompson
source.content:
Overview

This page captures the design intent and service-level contracts that govern the Redwood serving runtime. It is a team-catalog (reference) describing: service boundaries, RPC/HTTP contracts, scheduling expectations, shard isolation invariants, backpressure signals, preferred observability points, and canonical configuration recommendations. The goal is to provide engineers and oncall responders with a single, readable source of truth about what components are expected to guarantee under normal operation and during degraded modes.

Audience

- Runtime engineers (serving runtime, kernel, scheduler)
- SREs and oncall responders
- Platform/product engineers integrating with the runtime APIs

Design principles (short)

1. Explicit contracts: every surface exposes a minimal set of semantic guarantees (latency/throughput/failure modes).
2. Fail-fast / graceful-fallback: components should fail in a way that preserves upstream routing decisions and provides clear telemetry.
3. Observable invariants: critical invariants are always exported as metrics and trace annotations.
4. Backwards-compatible changes: new fields/flags are opt-in; defaults preserve existing behaviour.

Canonical component names and responsibilities

- request-router: accepts external HTTP/SDK requests, enforces quotas, tags request priority, and routes to batch-aggregator or kernel-executor.
- batch-aggregator: forms batches by model/variant/priority window and hands over to kernel-executor. Performs KV-prefix cache lookup and prefetch hints.
- kernel-executor: runs the optimized kernels on GPUs (or emulated CPUs); exposes kernel health and memory accounting.
- kv-cache-proxy: global LRU cache front for shared KV-prefix entries; supports per-customer namespace isolation.
- shard-manager: decides shard placement, migration, and eviction; owns shard-level leases.

Service contracts (summary)

Each contract is expressed as a set of expectations (what the caller can rely on) and obligations (what the callee must do).

1) request-router -> batch-aggregator
- Protocol: HTTP/1.1 JSON body or internal gRPC (preferred). Required fields: request_id, customer_id, model_variant, priority, max_latency_ms, seq_hint (optional).
- Expectations: batch-aggregator will acknowledge receipt within 5ms (p50) and either accept for batching or reject with BACKPRESSURE with a human-readable reason.
- Obligations: if the aggregator accepts, it must surface an accept_trace_id and the estimated enqueue_delay_ms. If rejecting, it must return retry_after_ms if applicable.

2) batch-aggregator -> kernel-executor
- Protocol: internal gRPC with a binary batch payload and per-request metadata.
- Expectations: kernel-executor will either start execution within the configured scheduling window (default 8ms) or return a PREEMPTION token explaining why (e.g., memory pressure, higher-priority workload).
- Obligations: executor must persistently record kernel_start and kernel_end timestamps for each batch and expose per-kernel memory_high_water_mark bytes.

Scheduling contract (kernel-level)

- Priority levels: system (highest), interactive (default), background (lowest).
- Scheduling window: default 8ms for interactive traffic; configurable per-deployment. Kernels should honor the window and not block longer than window + 2ms for integration steps.
- Max tokens per scheduling slice: 2048 tokens per GPU slice for transformer-based models unless overridden by model_profile.
- Fairness: no single customer may consume more than 30% of kernel credits on a shard unless explicitly reserved.
- Preemption semantics: preemption returns PREEMPTED status with reason code and a deterministic backoff recommendation. Preemption must be idempotent: re-submitting the same request_id within the recommended backoff window must be safe.

Shard isolation invariants

- Lease model: shard-manager grants a lease token to the kernel-executor to operate a shard; leases are renewed every 2s. Loss of lease triggers a soft-eviction where the kernel drains active batches for up to 250ms before halting new accepts.
- Memory isolation: each shard has a soft_limit and hard_limit (bytes). The soft_limit is 80% of hard_limit and triggers eviction/gc policies. Hard_limit must never be exceeded; kernel must fault instead of corrupting other shards.
- Namespace separation: kv-cache-proxy provides tenant namespaces. Cross-tenant cache affinity is prohibited unless explicitly whitelisted.

Telemetry and observability contract

Every runtime binary must export the following metrics and trace annotations:

- metrics (prometheus style):
  - runtime.request.router.latency_ms.p50/p95/p99
  - runtime.batch.aggregator.batch_size_distribution
  - runtime.kernel.executor.kernel_latency_ms.p50/p95/p99
  - runtime.kernel.executor.mem_high_water_mark_bytes
  - runtime.shard.lease_renewal_success_rate
  - runtime.kvcache.hit_ratio_last_5m
  - runtime.scheduling.preemptions_total{reason}
- traces: annotate spans with request_id, accept_trace_id, kernel_start, kernel_end, kernel_slice_id, and preemption_reason.

Suggested alerting thresholds (baseline)

- Alert A1 (request backlog): batch_aggregator.enqueue_depth > 1000 for 2m -> SEV2.
- Alert A2 (kernel memory pressure): kernel.mem_high_water_mark > 90% of hard_limit -> SEV1.
- Alert A3 (preemption spike): preemptions_total increases by > 5x baseline in 5m -> SEV2.
- Alert A4 (lease renewal failures): shard.lease_renewal_success_rate < 99% for 5m -> SEV1.

Configuration catalog (example table)

| config_key | purpose | recommended_default | notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| scheduling.window_ms | target kernel scheduling window | "8" | tuned per-region/instance-class |
| kernel.max_tokens_slice | hard max tokens per slice | "2048" | model_profile may override |
| shard.memory.soft_limit_pct | when to trigger eviction | "80" | percent of shard hard_limit |
| kvcache.eviction_policy | global eviction policy | "segmented-lru" | supports namespace weights |
| router.priority_override | allow special customers to bypass | "disabled" | requires compliance review |

Sample YAML (deployment-level)

deployment: production-us-east-1
components:
  request-router:
    replicas: 6
    max_in_flight_per_replica: 1200
  batch-aggregator:
    scheduling_window_ms: 8
    max_batch_size: 64
  kernel-executor:
    gpu_type: a100-80gb
    max_tokens_slice: 2048
    memory_hard_limit_bytes: 85899345920 # 80 GiB

Behavioral expectations during upgrades

- Backward compatibility: new executor features must accept both old and new request_meta fields. Default behaviour for unspecified fields must match the old implementation.
- Rolling upgrades: shard-manager will move new workloads onto upgraded nodes gradually (10% per 10 minutes) to validate kernel behavior under mixed-version conditions.
- Graceful draining: before terminating a node, the shard-manager should mark shards draining and allow up to 1s for active kernels to complete; any remaining batches should be moved or aborted with clear diagnostics.

Oncall quick runbook (first 10 actions)

1. Check primary dashboards: kernel_latency (p95), kernel_mem_hwm, batch_enqueue_depth.
2. If enqueue_depth > 1000 and growing, scale batch-aggregator replicas or reduce window_ms temporarily: curl -X POST "https://ops-api/deploys/scale?component=batch-aggregator&delta=+2".
3. If kernel.mem_hwm > 90%: identify offending shard via mem_high_water_mark metric and query shard-manager: 
   curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPS_TOKEN" "https://internal-api/shards?min_hwm=80" | jq .
4. If preemptions spike: check recent trace samples for preemption_reason tag and cross-reference customer_id. If caused by noisy tenant, apply rate limit or kernel credit cap.
5. If lease renewals failing: restart shard-manager replica on same AZ and monitor lease_renewal_success_rate.

Recovery play snippets

- Emergency evict and remap (when node unrecoverable):
  1) shard-manager mark-evict --node-id <node> --force
  2) kernel-executor drain --shard <shard-id> --timeout 250ms
  3) shard-manager remap-shard --shard <shard-id> --target <node2>

- Quick cache warmup after failover: run kvcache-prefetcher job with preapproved keys from recent hot prefix telemetry. Example CLI: 
  ./prefetcher --namespace tenant-123 --prefixes-file /tmp/hot-prefixes.json --concurrency 16

Compatibility notes & extension points

- Model profiles: model_onboarding must publish a model_profile JSON (schema v2) listing preferred max_tokens_slice, quantization_hint, and kv_prefixes. Runtimes must consult model_profile before enforcing hard caps.
- Custom schedulers: teams may implement a custom scheduling plugin provided they implement the SchedulingPlugin interface (method signatures listed below) and pass integration tests (scheduling-plugin-conformance).

Integration checklist for downstream teams

- Provide request examples (with request_id and priority) and a 48h synthetic load trace during onboarding.
- Confirm required SLO class with platform: interactive (p95 < 120ms), latency-sensitive (p95 < 80ms), batch (throughput, cost-optimized).
- Verify that any cache namespace sharing is approved and conforms to data residency rules.

Appendix: SchedulingPlugin interface (summary)

- on_batch_ready(batch_metadata) -> scheduling_decision{slice_tokens, priority_override, estimated_wait_ms}
- on_preemption(slices, reason) -> reschedule_hint{backoff_ms, allow_retry}
- health_check() -> {ok|degraded, diagnostics}

Appendix B: Change log (summary)

- 2024-11-05: initial catalog authored.
- 2025-02-12: added preemption idempotency requirement and backoff recommendations.
- 2025-08-10: clarified lease renewal invariants and increased default scheduling_window to 8ms (from 6ms) for interactive workloads after real-world tuning.

If you are adding or changing a contract on this page: update the change log, increment the compatibility note, and add a reviewer from SRE and Scheduling team.

Document owners and contacts

- Primary owner: lena.thompson@redwood.ai
- Secondary: eng-sre@redwood.ai

References

- See also: runtime-architecture/request-multiplexing-backpressure-architecture (detailed backpressure flow)
- Kernel scheduling guidelines (kernel-and-scheduling)
