| kvcache.eviction_policy | global eviction policy | \"segmented-lru\" | supports namespace weights |
| router.priority_override | allow special customers to bypass | \"disabled\" | requires compliance review |

Sample YAML (deployment-level)

deployment: production-us-east-1
components:
  request-router:
    replicas: 6
    max_in_flight_per_replica: 1200
  batch-aggregator:
    scheduling_window_ms: 8
    max_batch_size: 64
  kernel-executor:
    gpu_type: a100-80gb
    max_tokens_slice: 2048
    memory_hard_limit_bytes: 85899345920 # 80 GiB

Behavioral expectations during upgrades

- Backward compatibility: new executor features must accept both old and new request_meta fields. Default behaviour for unspecified fields must match the old implementation.
- Rolling upgrades: shard-manager will move new workloads onto upgraded nodes gradually (10% per 10 minutes) to validate kernel behavior under mixed-version conditions.
- Graceful draining: before terminating a node, the shard-manager should mark shards draining and allow up to 1s for active kernels to complete; any remaining batches should be moved or aborted with clear diagnostics.

Oncall quick runbook (first 10 actions)

1. Check primary dashboards: kernel_latency (p95), kernel_mem_hwm, batch_enqueue_depth.
2. If enqueue_depth > 1000 and growing, scale batch-aggregator replicas or reduce window_ms temporarily: curl -X POST \"https://ops-api/deploys/scale?component=batch-aggregator&delta=+2\".
3. If kernel.mem_hwm > 90%: identify offending shard via mem_high_water_mark metric and query shard-manager: 
   curl -s -H \"Authorization: Bearer $OPS_TOKEN\" \"https://internal-api/shards?min_hwm=80\" | jq .
4. If preemptions spike: check recent trace samples for preemption_reason tag and cross-reference customer_id. If caused by noisy tenant, apply rate limit or kernel credit cap.
5. If lease renewals failing: restart shard-manager replica on same AZ and monitor lease_renewal_success_rate.

Recovery play snippets

- Emergency evict and remap (when node unrecoverable):
  1) shard-manager mark-evict --node-id <node> --force
  2) kernel-executor drain --shard <shard-id> --timeout 250ms
  3) shard-manager remap-shard --shard <shard-id> --target <node2>

- Quick cache warmup after failover: run kvcache-prefetcher job with preapproved keys from recent hot prefix telemetry. Example CLI: 
  ./prefetcher --namespace tenant-123 --prefixes-file /tmp/hot-prefixes.json --concurrency 16

Compatibility notes & extension points

- Model profiles: model_onboarding must publish a model_profile JSON (schema v2) listing preferred max_tokens_slice, quantization_hint, and kv_prefixes. Runtimes must consult model_profile before enforcing hard caps.
- Custom schedulers: teams may implement a custom scheduling plugin provided they implement the SchedulingPlugin interface (method signatures listed below) and pass integration tests (scheduling-plugin-conformance).

Integration checklist for downstream teams

- Provide request examples (with request_id and priority) and a 48h synthetic load trace during onboarding.
- Confirm required SLO class with platform: interactive (p95 < 120ms), latency-sensitive (p95 < 80ms), batch (throughput, cost-optimized).
- Verify that any cache namespace sharing is approved and conforms to data residency rules.

Appendix: SchedulingPlugin interface (summary)

- on_batch_ready(batch_metadata) -> scheduling_decision{slice_tokens, priority_override, estimated_wait_ms}
- on_preemption(slices, reason) -> reschedule_hint{backoff_ms, allow_retry}
- health_check() -> {ok|degraded, diagnostics}

Appendix B: Change log (summary)

- 2024-11-05: initial catalog authored.
- 2025-02-12: added preemption idempotency requirement and backoff recommendations.
- 2025-08-10: clarified lease renewal invariants and increased default scheduling_window to 8ms (from 6ms) for interactive workloads after real-world tuning.

If you are adding or changing a contract on this page: update the change log, increment the compatibility note, and add a reviewer from SRE and Scheduling team.

Document owners and contacts

- Primary owner: lena.thompson@redwood.ai
- Secondary: eng-sre@redwood.ai

References

- See also: runtime-architecture/request-multiplexing-backpressure-architecture (detailed backpressure flow)
- Kernel scheduling guidelines (kernel-and-scheduling)
text:
Overview

This page captures the design intent and service-level contracts that govern the Redwood serving runtime. It is a team-catalog (reference) describing: service boundaries, RPC/HTTP contracts, scheduling expectations, shard isolation invariants, backpressure signals, preferred observability points, and canonical configuration recommendations. The goal is to provide engineers and oncall responders with a single, readable source of truth about what components are expected to guarantee under normal operation and during degraded modes.

Audience

- Runtime engineers (serving runtime, kernel, scheduler)
- SREs and oncall responders
- Platform/product engineers integrating with the runtime APIs

Design principles (short)

1. Explicit contracts: every surface exposes a minimal set of semantic guarantees (latency/throughput/failure modes).
2. Fail-fast / graceful-fallback: components should fail in a way that preserves upstream routing decisions and provides clear telemetry.
3. Observable invariants: critical invariants are always exported as metrics and trace annotations.
4. Backwards-compatible changes: new fields/flags are opt-in; defaults preserve existing behaviour.

Canonical component names and responsibilities

- request-router: accepts external HTTP/SDK requests, enforces quotas, tags request priority, and routes to batch-aggregator or kernel-executor.
- batch-aggregator: forms batches by model/variant/priority window and hands over to kernel-executor. Performs KV-prefix cache lookup and prefetch hints.
- kernel-executor: runs the optimized kernels on GPUs (or emulated CPUs); exposes kernel health and memory accounting.
- kv-cache-proxy: global LRU cache front for shared KV-prefix entries; supports per-customer namespace isolation.
- shard-manager: decides shard placement, migration, and eviction; owns shard-level leases.

Service contracts (summary)

Each contract is expressed as a set of expectations (what the caller can rely on) and obligations (what the callee must do).

1) request-router -> batch-aggregator
- Protocol: HTTP/1.1 JSON body or internal gRPC (preferred). Required fields: request_id, customer_id, model_variant, priority, max_latency_ms, seq_hint (optional).
- Expectations: batch-aggregator will acknowledge receipt within 5ms (p50) and either accept for batching or reject with BACKPRESSURE with a human-readable reason.
- Obligations: if the aggregator accepts, it must surface an accept_trace_id and the estimated enqueue_delay_ms. If rejecting, it must return retry_after_ms if applicable.

2) batch-aggregator -> kernel-executor
- Protocol: internal gRPC with a binary batch payload and per-request metadata.
- Expectations: kernel-executor will either start execution within the configured scheduling window (default 8ms) or return a PREEMPTION token explaining why (e.g., memory pressure, higher-priority workload).
- Obligations: executor must persistently record kernel_start and kernel_end timestamps for each batch and expose per-kernel memory_high_water_mark bytes.

Scheduling contract (kernel-level)

- Priority levels: system (highest), interactive (default), background (lowest).
- Scheduling window: default 8ms for interactive traffic; configurable per-deployment. Kernels should honor the window and not block longer than window + 2ms for integration steps.
- Max tokens per scheduling slice: 2048 tokens per GPU slice for transformer-based models unless overridden by model_profile.
- Fairness: no single customer may consume more than 30% of kernel credits on a shard unless explicitly reserved.
- Preemption semantics: preemption returns PREEMPTED status with reason code and a deterministic backoff recommendation. Preemption must be idempotent: re-submitting the same request_id within the recommended backoff window must be safe.

Shard isolation invariants

- Lease model: shard-manager grants a lease token to the kernel-executor to operate a shard; leases are renewed every 2s. Loss of lease triggers a soft-eviction where the kernel drains active batches for up to 250ms before halting new accepts.
- Memory isolation: each shard has a soft_limit and hard_limit (bytes). The soft_limit is 80% of hard_limit and triggers eviction/gc policies. Hard_limit must never be exceeded; kernel must fault instead of corrupting other shards.
- Namespace separation: kv-cache-proxy provides tenant namespaces. Cross-tenant cache affinity is prohibited unless explicitly whitelisted.

Telemetry and observability contract

Every runtime binary must export the following metrics and trace annotations:

- metrics (prometheus style):
  - runtime.request.router.latency_ms.p50/p95/p99
  - runtime.batch.aggregator.batch_size_distribution
  - runtime.kernel.executor.kernel_latency_ms.p50/p95/p99
  - runtime.kernel.executor.mem_high_water_mark_bytes
  - runtime.shard.lease_renewal_success_rate
  - runtime.kvcache.hit_ratio_last_5m
  - runtime.scheduling.preemptions_total{reason}
- traces: annotate spans with request_id, accept_trace_id, kernel_start, kernel_end, kernel_slice_id, and preemption_reason.

Suggested alerting thresholds (baseline)

- Alert A1 (request backlog): batch_aggregator.enqueue_depth > 1000 for 2m -> SEV2.
- Alert A2 (kernel memory pressure): kernel.mem_high_water_mark > 90% of hard_limit -> SEV1.
- Alert A3 (preemption spike): preemptions_total increases by > 5x baseline in 5m -> SEV2.
- Alert A4 (lease renewal failures): shard.lease_renewal_success_rate < 99% for 5m -> SEV1.

Configuration catalog (example table)

| config_key | purpose | recommended_default | notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| scheduling.window_ms | target kernel scheduling window | \"8\" | tuned per-region/instance-class |
| kernel.max_tokens_slice | hard max tokens per slice | \"2048\" | model_profile may override |
| shard.memory.soft_limit_pct | when to trigger eviction | \"80\" | percent of shard hard_limit |
| kvcache.eviction_policy | global eviction policy | \"segmented-lru\" | supports namespace weights |
| router.priority_override | allow special customers to bypass | \"disabled\" | requires compliance review |

Sample YAML (deployment-level)

deployment: production-us-east-1
components:
  request-router:
    replicas: 6
    max_in_flight_per_replica: 1200
  batch-aggregator:
    scheduling_window_ms: 8
    max_batch_size: 64
  kernel-executor:
    gpu_type: a100-80gb
    max_tokens_slice: 2048
    memory_hard_limit_bytes: 85899345920 # 80 GiB

Behavioral expectations during upgrades

- Backward compatibility: new executor features must accept both old and new request_meta fields. Default behaviour for unspecified fields must match the old implementation.
- Rolling upgrades: shard-manager will move new workloads onto upgraded nodes gradually (10% per 10 minutes) to validate kernel behavior under mixed-version conditions.
- Graceful draining: before terminating a node, the shard-manager should mark shards draining and allow up to 1s for active kernels to complete; any remaining batches should be moved or aborted with clear diagnostics.

Oncall quick runbook (first 10 actions)

1. Check primary dashboards: kernel_latency (p95), kernel_mem_hwm, batch_enqueue_depth.
2. If enqueue_depth > 1000 and growing, scale batch-aggregator replicas or reduce window_ms temporarily: curl -X POST \"https://ops-api/deploys/scale?component=batch-aggregator&delta=+2\".
3. If kernel.mem_hwm > 90%: identify offending shard via mem_high_water_mark metric and query shard-manager: 
   curl -s -H \"Authorization: Bearer $OPS_TOKEN\" \"https://internal-api/shards?min_hwm=80\" | jq .
4. If preemptions spike: check recent trace samples for preemption_reason tag and cross-reference customer_id. If caused by noisy tenant, apply rate limit or kernel credit cap.
5. If lease renewals failing: restart shard-manager replica on same AZ and monitor lease_renewal_success_rate.

Recovery play snippets

- Emergency evict and remap (when node unrecoverable):
  1) shard-manager mark-evict --node-id <node> --force
  2) kernel-executor drain --shard <shard-id> --timeout 250ms
  3) shard-manager remap-shard --shard <shard-id> --target <node2>

- Quick cache warmup after failover: run kvcache-prefetcher job with preapproved keys from recent hot prefix telemetry. Example CLI: 
  ./prefetcher --namespace tenant-123 --prefixes-file /tmp/hot-prefixes.json --concurrency 16

Compatibility notes & extension points

- Model profiles: model_onboarding must publish a model_profile JSON (schema v2) listing preferred max_tokens_slice, quantization_hint, and kv_prefixes. Runtimes must consult model_profile before enforcing hard caps.
- Custom schedulers: teams may implement a custom scheduling plugin provided they implement the SchedulingPlugin interface (method signatures listed below) and pass integration tests (scheduling-plugin-conformance).

Integration checklist for downstream teams

- Provide request examples (with request_id and priority) and a 48h synthetic load trace during onboarding.
- Confirm required SLO class with platform: interactive (p95 < 120ms), latency-sensitive (p95 < 80ms), batch (throughput, cost-optimized).
- Verify that any cache namespace sharing is approved and conforms to data residency rules.

Appendix: SchedulingPlugin interface (summary)

- on_batch_ready(batch_metadata) -> scheduling_decision{slice_tokens, priority_override, estimated_wait_ms}
- on_preemption(slices, reason) -> reschedule_hint{backoff_ms, allow_retry}
- health_check() -> {ok|degraded, diagnostics}

Appendix B: Change log (summary)

- 2024-11-05: initial catalog authored.
- 2025-02-12: added preemption idempotency requirement and backoff recommendations.
- 2025-08-10: clarified lease renewal invariants and increased default scheduling_window to 8ms (from 6ms) for interactive workloads after real-world tuning.

If you are adding or changing a contract on this page: update the change log, increment the compatibility note, and add a reviewer from SRE and Scheduling team.

Document owners and contacts

- Primary owner: lena.thompson@redwood.ai
- Secondary: eng-sre@redwood.ai

References

- See also: runtime-architecture/request-multiplexing-backpressure-architecture (detailed backpressure flow)
- Kernel scheduling guidelines (kernel-and-scheduling)

source.title: Runbook: Benchmark report region sweeps (latency/$)
source.author: Rina Kobayashi
source.body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to execute **region sweeps** for the public benchmark report ("latency/$" and supporting latency/throughput metrics). It covers:
- Capacity reservation and readiness checks per region
- Running the region sweep runner and generating reproducible manifests
- Exporting versioned result artifacts (JSON/CSV) for the web + PDF pipeline
- Stability/variance gates and when to rerun
- Failure recovery and escalation

This is intended for Applied ML/Perf and Runtime/Infra partners running official report sweeps.

## Owners and escalation
- Primary run owner (Benchmark harness): **Rina Kobayashi**
- Technical sign-off (Perf/Evals): **Jonas Weber**, **Claire Dubois**
- Fleet capacity coordination: **Vanessa Ortiz** (backup: **Harish Reddy**)
- SRE on-call / incident owner: **Sean Gallagher**
- Metric correctness (latency/$ normalization): **Nadia Rahman**

Escalate immediately in #eng-runtime and #eng-infra if:
- A region cannot hold reserved capacity for the full sweep window
- p95 latency variance repeatedly exceeds the stability threshold after reruns
- We observe elevated 429/5xx from the benchmark target endpoint

## Preconditions
### 1) Methodology lock
Before an official sweep, confirm the following are locked and referenced in the run manifest:
- Prompt set version (e.g., `promptset_v3_production_mix`)
- Workload definitions (prefill/decode mix; streaming vs non-streaming) 
- Concurrency levels and arrival pattern
- Caching/batching policy used during benchmark runs
- Warmup protocol and run duration

Source of truth: Confluence methodology page (see Related Pages).

### 2) Tooling and access
You need:
- Access to the benchmark runner repo ("benchmark-lab")
- Ability to assume the regional runner role in Redwood cloud accounts
- Write access to the benchmark artifacts bucket (internal)
- Read access to Fleet dashboards for capacity verification

### 3) Capacity reservation is approved and scheduled
Do not begin a region sweep without an approved reservation plan.
- Owner: Vanessa Ortiz
- Required output: reservation ticket/link + reservation IDs per region

Recommended lead time:
- Standard sweep: reserve **48 hours** prior
- Launch-week sweep (or any externally referenced numbers): reserve **5–7 days** prior

## High-level workflow
1) Confirm capacity reservation windows and instance types per region.
2) Smoke-test the target region endpoint and runner environment.
3) Generate a sweep manifest (model × region × workload matrix).
4) Execute sweep (with stability gates enabled).
5) Validate outputs (sanity + outlier checks).
6) Export versioned artifacts (JSON/CSV) and publish to the staging ingestion path.
7) Hand off to validation/review checklist for final sign-off.

## Capacity reservation steps (per region)
### Inputs to provide Fleet
Provide Fleet with:
- Regions (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`, `eu-central`, `apac-sg`, `apac-syd` — only those approved for publication)
- Target instance types and counts
- Sweep schedule window (start/end in UTC)
- Expected load pattern (concurrency levels; whether spikes are expected)
- Whether results will be used for public claims (impacts change control)

### Day-of checks (30–60 minutes before sweep)
In Fleet dashboards (or via the capacity status CLI if available), confirm:
- Reserved capacity is active and pinned to the correct cluster pool
- No known maintenance events overlapping the sweep window
- Baseline GPU utilization in the region is <70% before starting
- No elevated queue depth in the inference scheduler

If any check fails:
- Pause and coordinate with Vanessa/Harish to shift the window or move the sweep to a fallback region set.

## Runner setup
### Recommended execution environment
Use the Dockerized runner image for determinism.
- Host requirements: Linux amd64, Docker 24+
- Recommended: run from a controlled runner VM close to the control plane (not from a laptop)

### Required environment variables
Set the following (names are standardized for the benchmark runner; adjust to the current repo if changed):
- `RW_BENCH_TOKEN` (short-lived token)
- `RW_CONTROL_PLANE_URL` (control plane base URL)
- `RW_ARTIFACT_BUCKET` (internal artifact bucket)
- `RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX` (e.g., `benchmark-report/2025-07/v1/`)
- `RW_RUNNER_REGION` (where the runner executes; does not need to equal target inference region)

### Pinning versions
For official runs:
- Pin the runner image digest (not just a tag)
- Pin benchmark-lab commit SHA
- Pin model versions/IDs exactly as in the methodology

Record all pins in the manifest.

## Generating a sweep manifest
A sweep manifest is the source-of-truth for reproducibility. It must be committed to the artifact store and referenced by all derived datasets.

### Example manifest fields (conceptual)
Include at minimum:
- `schema_version`
- `run_id` (globally unique)
- `created_at_utc`
- `runner_image_digest`
- `benchmark_lab_commit`
- `regions[]`
- `models[]` (with pinned version identifiers)
- `workloads[]` (prompt set + token size distributions + concurrency)
- `caching_policy` and `batching_policy`
- `warmup` (duration and request count)
- `measurement_window` (duration)
- `stability_gates` (thresholds)
- `exclusions` (any planned exclusions must be justified)

### CLI: create manifest
From the benchmark-lab repo root:
1) Pull the pinned runner image:
- `docker pull <runner-image>@sha256:<digest>`

2) Generate the manifest:
- `./bench sweep manifest create \
  --out manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json \
  --report benchmark-report-2025-07 \
  --prompt-set promptset_v3_production_mix \
  --workload production_mix_v1 \
  --regions us-east,us-west,eu-west,eu-central,apac-sg \
  --models rw/llama-3.1-70b:2025-06-15,rw/qwen2.5-72b:2025-06-20,rw/mistral-large:2025-06-10 \
  --concurrency 1,4,16,64 \
  --prefill-tokens 512,1024 \
  --decode-tokens 128,256 \
  --streaming-mode off \
  --cache-policy benchmark_default \
  --batching-policy benchmark_default \
  --warmup-seconds 300 \
  --measure-seconds 900 \
  --stability-p95-variance-pct 7.5 \
  --stability-max-reruns 2`

Notes:
- Concurrency and token sizes must match the methodology.
- If streaming is included, you must run it as a separate workload block and publish streaming-specific caveats.

### Upload manifest
- `./bench artifact upload --file manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json --dest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/manifests/`

## Executing the sweep
### CLI: run sweep
- `./bench sweep run \
  --manifest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json \
  --target redwood \
  --parallelism 6 \
  --respect-capacity-reservations true \
  --emit-metrics true \
  --redact-public-identifiers true`

Recommended guidance:
- Start with `--parallelism 2` for the first region as a smoke test, then ramp.
- Avoid parallelizing across too many regions simultaneously unless Fleet confirms headroom.

### Stability gates (variance detection)
Stability gates should be enabled for official runs.
- Default gate: auto-rerun a cell (model×region×workload×concurrency) when p95 latency variance exceeds threshold.
- Suggested threshold for publication runs: **<= 7.5%** p95 variance (relative) across repeat runs.
- Max auto-reruns: **2** per cell.

If a cell still fails after max reruns:
- Mark it as `unstable` in the results
- Do not silently drop it
- Escalate to Runtime + Infra to determine whether to rerun the entire region window or exclude with an explicit, documented rationale

## Output artifacts
The sweep produces:
- Raw run logs (internal retention)
- Per-cell summaries (latency, throughput, cost components)
- Derived metrics including `latency_per_dollar` and `$ / 1M tokens`
- Export bundles (CSV + JSON) for web ingestion

### Export for web ingestion
After a successful sweep:
- `./bench export results \
  --manifest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json \
  --format json,csv \
  --schema-version 1 \
  --out exports/benchmark-report-2025-07-v1/`

Upload exports:
- `./bench artifact upload --dir exports/benchmark-report-2025-07-v1/ --dest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/exports/benchmark-report-2025-07-v1/`

### Naming and versioning rules
- Use a versioned prefix: `benchmark-report/<YYYY-MM>/<vN>/...`
- Do not overwrite previously published artifacts
- Any re-run intended to replace numbers must bump artifact version (e.g., v1 → v2) and include a changelog note

## Validation (quick sanity checks)
Before handing off to the formal validation checklist:
1) Ensure all expected regions/models are present.
2) Ensure no region is missing an entire model family due to capacity errors.
3) Confirm reasonable ordering:
   - Higher concurrency should not systematically lower p95 latency unless batching behavior is expected and documented.
4) Check for obvious unit/currency mistakes in cost normalization.
5) Confirm redaction: public artifacts must not contain request IDs, tokens, customer identifiers, internal hostnames, or cluster IDs.

If any issue is detected, halt publication pipeline ingestion and rerun after fixing.

## Common failures and recovery
### 1) Region capacity shortfall (queueing, timeouts)
Symptoms:
- Elevated queue depth, increased p95/p99, intermittent timeouts

Actions:
1) Pause the sweep for the impacted region.
2) Verify reservation is still active and correctly assigned.
3) Reduce parallelism and rerun a small subset to confirm stabilization.
4) If still unstable, coordinate with Fleet to:
   - Shift the window
   - Move to a backup region (if allowed by publication plan)
   - Increase reserved capacity (if feasible)

Document the change in the run record and manifest notes.

### 2) Elevated 429s / rate limiting
Symptoms:
- Runner reports `429 Too Many Requests` from the target endpoint

Actions:
1) Confirm the runner is using the benchmark allowlist/quota policy.
2) Reduce parallelism and concurrency to restore steady state.
3) If 429s persist, page on-call (SRE) to confirm no global guardrails were triggered.

Do not mask 429s as successful responses; they invalidate the cell.

### 3) Partial region outage / 5xx
Symptoms:
- Spike in 5xx, runner retries, incomplete cells

Actions:
1) Stop the sweep (do not continue collecting mixed healthy/unhealthy data).
2) Capture timestamps and run IDs.
3) Coordinate with SRE to determine whether to:
   - Rerun only impacted cells (if outage was brief and isolated)
   - Restart the entire region sweep window (preferred for publication runs)

### 4) High variance without obvious infra issues
Symptoms:
- Stability gate fails repeatedly but infra metrics look normal

Actions:
1) Confirm the kernel selection/runtime configuration is pinned and consistent.
2) Verify no background rollout occurred in the region for the serving runtime.
3) Check prompt set distribution and tokenization parity.
4) Run an isolated micro-sweep on a single model to bisect.

Escalate to Runtime owners if suspected regression.

### 5) Artifact export/ingestion mismatch
Symptoms:
- Web ingestion pipeline rejects schema or missing fields

Actions:
1) Confirm export schema version matches the web consumer.
2) Re-run export (no need to rerun the sweep) if raw summaries are correct.
3) If a schema change is required, coordinate with web ingestion owner before regenerating official versioned exports.

## Communication and recordkeeping
For official report runs:
- Post a start notice in #eng-ml and #eng-runtime with:
  - run_id, manifest link, planned regions/models, expected completion time
- Post completion with:
  - artifact export path, summary of any reruns/exclusions, initial sanity check results

All run artifacts (raw + derived) must follow the public benchmark data retention policy.

## Appendix A: Recommended sweep sizing
These are operational defaults to keep region sweeps tractable while still representative:
- Regions per run: 4–6
- Models per run: 3–6
- Concurrency grid: 1, 4, 16, 64 (methodology permitting)
- Measurement window: 15 minutes per cell (after warmup)
- Warmup: 5 minutes per region/model before measurement windows

Adjustments must be approved by the methodology owner.

## Appendix B: Checklist (run owner)
- [ ] Capacity reservation confirmed for all regions
- [ ] Runner image digest + repo SHA pinned
- [ ] Manifest generated and uploaded
- [ ] Smoke test successful in at least one region
- [ ] Sweep completed with stability gates enabled
- [ ] Exports generated (CSV/JSON) and uploaded with versioned prefix
- [ ] Quick sanity checks completed
- [ ] Handed off to formal validation checklist for sign-off

2) Smoke-test the target region endpoint and runner environment.
3) Generate a sweep manifest (model × region × workload matrix).
4) Execute sweep (with stability gates enabled).
5) Validate outputs (sanity + outlier checks).
6) Export versioned artifacts (JSON/CSV) and publish to the staging ingestion path.
7) Hand off to validation/review checklist for final sign-off.

## Capacity reservation steps (per region)
### Inputs to provide Fleet
Provide Fleet with:
- Regions (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`, `eu-central`, `apac-sg`, `apac-syd` — only those approved for publication)
- Target instance types and counts
- Sweep schedule window (start/end in UTC)
- Expected load pattern (concurrency levels; whether spikes are expected)
- Whether results will be used for public claims (impacts change control)

### Day-of checks (30–60 minutes before sweep)
In Fleet dashboards (or via the capacity status CLI if available), confirm:
- Reserved capacity is active and pinned to the correct cluster pool
- No known maintenance events overlapping the sweep window
- Baseline GPU utilization in the region is <70% before starting
- No elevated queue depth in the inference scheduler

If any check fails:
- Pause and coordinate with Vanessa/Harish to shift the window or move the sweep to a fallback region set.

## Runner setup
### Recommended execution environment
Use the Dockerized runner image for determinism.
- Host requirements: Linux amd64, Docker 24+
- Recommended: run from a controlled runner VM close to the control plane (not from a laptop)

### Required environment variables
Set the following (names are standardized for the benchmark runner; adjust to the current repo if changed):
- `RW_BENCH_TOKEN` (short-lived token)
- `RW_CONTROL_PLANE_URL` (control plane base URL)
- `RW_ARTIFACT_BUCKET` (internal artifact bucket)
- `RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX` (e.g., `benchmark-report/2025-07/v1/`)
- `RW_RUNNER_REGION` (where the runner executes; does not need to equal target inference region)

### Pinning versions
For official runs:
- Pin the runner image digest (not just a tag)
- Pin benchmark-lab commit SHA
- Pin model versions/IDs exactly as in the methodology

Record all pins in the manifest.

## Generating a sweep manifest
A sweep manifest is the source-of-truth for reproducibility. It must be committed to the artifact store and referenced by all derived datasets.

### Example manifest fields (conceptual)
Include at minimum:
- `schema_version`
- `run_id` (globally unique)
- `created_at_utc`
- `runner_image_digest`
- `benchmark_lab_commit`
- `regions[]`
- `models[]` (with pinned version identifiers)
- `workloads[]` (prompt set + token size distributions + concurrency)
- `caching_policy` and `batching_policy`
- `warmup` (duration and request count)
- `measurement_window` (duration)
- `stability_gates` (thresholds)
- `exclusions` (any planned exclusions must be justified)

### CLI: create manifest
From the benchmark-lab repo root:
1) Pull the pinned runner image:
- `docker pull <runner-image>@sha256:<digest>`

2) Generate the manifest:
- `./bench sweep manifest create \
  --out manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json \
  --report benchmark-report-2025-07 \
  --prompt-set promptset_v3_production_mix \
  --workload production_mix_v1 \
  --regions us-east,us-west,eu-west,eu-central,apac-sg \
  --models rw/llama-3.1-70b:2025-06-15,rw/qwen2.5-72b:2025-06-20,rw/mistral-large:2025-06-10 \
  --concurrency 1,4,16,64 \
  --prefill-tokens 512,1024 \
  --decode-tokens 128,256 \
  --streaming-mode off \
  --cache-policy benchmark_default \
  --batching-policy benchmark_default \
  --warmup-seconds 300 \
  --measure-seconds 900 \
  --stability-p95-variance-pct 7.5 \
  --stability-max-reruns 2`

Notes:
- Concurrency and token sizes must match the methodology.
- If streaming is included, you must run it as a separate workload block and publish streaming-specific caveats.

### Upload manifest
- `./bench artifact upload --file manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json --dest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/manifests/`

## Executing the sweep
### CLI: run sweep
- `./bench sweep run \
  --manifest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json \
  --target redwood \
  --parallelism 6 \
  --respect-capacity-reservations true \
  --emit-metrics true \
  --redact-public-identifiers true`

Recommended guidance:
- Start with `--parallelism 2` for the first region as a smoke test, then ramp.
- Avoid parallelizing across too many regions simultaneously unless Fleet confirms headroom.

### Stability gates (variance detection)
Stability gates should be enabled for official runs.
- Default gate: auto-rerun a cell (model×region×workload×concurrency) when p95 latency variance exceeds threshold.
- Suggested threshold for publication runs: **<= 7.5%** p95 variance (relative) across repeat runs.
- Max auto-reruns: **2** per cell.

If a cell still fails after max reruns:
- Mark it as `unstable` in the results
- Do not silently drop it
- Escalate to Runtime + Infra to determine whether to rerun the entire region window or exclude with an explicit, documented rationale

## Output artifacts
The sweep produces:
- Raw run logs (internal retention)
- Per-cell summaries (latency, throughput, cost components)
- Derived metrics including `latency_per_dollar` and `$ / 1M tokens`
- Export bundles (CSV + JSON) for web ingestion

### Export for web ingestion
After a successful sweep:
- `./bench export results \
  --manifest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/manifests/region-sweep-2025-07-01.json \
  --format json,csv \
  --schema-version 1 \
  --out exports/benchmark-report-2025-07-v1/`

Upload exports:
- `./bench artifact upload --dir exports/benchmark-report-2025-07-v1/ --dest $RW_ARTIFACT_PREFIX/exports/benchmark-report-2025-07-v1/`

### Naming and versioning rules
- Use a versioned prefix: `benchmark-report/<YYYY-MM>/<vN>/...`
- Do not overwrite previously published artifacts
- Any re-run intended to replace numbers must bump artifact version (e.g., v1 → v2) and include a changelog note

## Validation (quick sanity checks)
Before handing off to the formal validation checklist:
1) Ensure all expected regions/models are present.
2) Ensure no region is missing an entire model family due to capacity errors.
3) Confirm reasonable ordering:
   - Higher concurrency should not systematically lower p95 latency unless batching behavior is expected and documented.
4) Check for obvious unit/currency mistakes in cost normalization.
5) Confirm redaction: public artifacts must not contain request IDs, tokens, customer identifiers, internal hostnames, or cluster IDs.

If any issue is detected, halt publication pipeline ingestion and rerun after fixing.

## Common failures and recovery
### 1) Region capacity shortfall (queueing, timeouts)
Symptoms:
- Elevated queue depth, increased p95/p99, intermittent timeouts

Actions:
1) Pause the sweep for the impacted region.
2) Verify reservation is still active and correctly assigned.
3) Reduce parallelism and rerun a small subset to confirm stabilization.
4) If still unstable, coordinate with Fleet to:
   - Shift the window
   - Move to a backup region (if allowed by publication plan)
   - Increase reserved capacity (if feasible)

Document the change in the run record and manifest notes.

### 2) Elevated 429s / rate limiting
Symptoms:
- Runner reports `429 Too Many Requests` from the target endpoint

Actions:
1) Confirm the runner is using the benchmark allowlist/quota policy.
2) Reduce parallelism and concurrency to restore steady state.
3) If 429s persist, page on-call (SRE) to confirm no global guardrails were triggered.

Do not mask 429s as successful responses; they invalidate the cell.

### 3) Partial region outage / 5xx
Symptoms:
- Spike in 5xx, runner retries, incomplete cells

Actions:
1) Stop the sweep (do not continue collecting mixed healthy/unhealthy data).
2) Capture timestamps and run IDs.
3) Coordinate with SRE to determine whether to:
   - Rerun only impacted cells (if outage was brief and isolated)
   - Restart the entire region sweep window (preferred for publication runs)

### 4) High variance without obvious infra issues
Symptoms:
- Stability gate fails repeatedly but infra metrics look normal

Actions:
1) Confirm the kernel selection/runtime configuration is pinned and consistent.
2) Verify no background rollout occurred in the region for the serving runtime.
3) Check prompt set distribution and tokenization parity.
4) Run an isolated micro-sweep on a single model to bisect.

Escalate to Runtime owners if suspected regression.

### 5) Artifact export/ingestion mismatch
Symptoms:
- Web ingestion pipeline rejects schema or missing fields

Actions:
1) Confirm export schema version matches the web consumer.
2) Re-run export (no need to rerun the sweep) if raw summaries are correct.
3) If a schema change is required, coordinate with web ingestion owner before regenerating official versioned exports.

## Communication and recordkeeping
For official report runs:
- Post a start notice in #eng-ml and #eng-runtime with:
  - run_id, manifest link, planned regions/models, expected completion time
- Post completion with:
  - artifact export path, summary of any reruns/exclusions, initial sanity check results

All run artifacts (raw + derived) must follow the public benchmark data retention policy.

## Appendix A: Recommended sweep sizing
These are operational defaults to keep region sweeps tractable while still representative:
- Regions per run: 4–6
- Models per run: 3–6
- Concurrency grid: 1, 4, 16, 64 (methodology permitting)
- Measurement window: 15 minutes per cell (after warmup)
- Warmup: 5 minutes per region/model before measurement windows

Adjustments must be approved by the methodology owner.

## Appendix B: Checklist (run owner)
- [ ] Capacity reservation confirmed for all regions
- [ ] Runner image digest + repo SHA pinned
- [ ] Manifest generated and uploaded
- [ ] Smoke test successful in at least one region
- [ ] Sweep completed with stability gates enabled
- [ ] Exports generated (CSV/JSON) and uploaded with versioned prefix
- [ ] Quick sanity checks completed
- [ ] Handed off to formal validation checklist for sign-off

## Purpose
This runbook describes how to execute **region sweeps** for the public benchmark report ("latency/$" and supporting latency/throughput metrics). It covers:
- Capacity reservation and readiness checks per region
- Running the region sweep runner and generating reproducible manifests
- Exporting versioned result artifacts (JSON/CSV) for the web + PDF pipeline
- Stability/variance gates and when to rerun
- Failure recovery and escalation

This is intended for Applied ML/Perf and Runtime/Infra partners running official report sweeps.

## Owners and escalation
- Primary run owner (Benchmark harness): **Rina Kobayashi**
- Technical sign-off (Perf/Evals): **Jonas Weber**, **Claire Dubois**
- Fleet capacity coordination: **Vanessa Ortiz** (backup: **Harish Reddy**)
- SRE on-call / incident owner: **Sean Gallagher**
- Metric correctness (latency/$ normalization): **Nadia Rahman**

Escalate immediately in #eng-runtime and #eng-infra if:
- A region cannot hold reserved capacity for the full sweep window
- p95 latency variance repeatedly exceeds the stability threshold after reruns
- We observe elevated 429/5xx from the benchmark target endpoint

## Preconditions
### 1) Methodology lock
Before an official sweep, confirm the following are locked and referenced in the run manifest:
- Prompt set version (e.g., `promptset_v3_production_mix`)
- Workload definitions (prefill/decode mix; streaming vs non-streaming) 
- Concurrency levels and arrival pattern
- Caching/batching policy used during benchmark runs
- Warmup protocol and run duration

Source of truth: Confluence methodology page (see Related Pages).

### 2) Tooling and access
You need:
- Access to the benchmark runner repo ("benchmark-lab")

- Write access to the benchmark artifacts bucket (internal)
- Read access to Fleet dashboards for capacity verification

### 3) Capacity reservation is approved and scheduled

- Owner: Vanessa Ortiz
- Required output: reservation ticket/link + reservation IDs per region

- Standard sweep: reserve **48 hours** prior
- Launch-week sweep (or any externally referenced numbers): reserve **5–7 days** prior

source.title: Runtime precursor signal catalog and escalation checklist
source.author: Lara Chen
source.content:
Overview
--------
This document is a cross-cutting catalog of PRECURSOR signals the runtime team uses to detect emerging problems across the serving runtime, kernel scheduler, profiling pipeline, and benchmarking surface. It pairs each signal with a short investigation checklist, automated queries (PromQL / SQL / CLI examples), threshold guidance, and structured escalation steps. The goal is to surface problems earlier than full SLA violations and standardize triage across on-call, SRE, and runtime engineers.

Scope
------
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where the runtime agent and dispatcher emit telemetry into the telemetry stack.
- Covers signal classes: scheduler contention, kernel regressions, KV cache cold/warm signals, sequence-tail latency drift, single-tenant hot-spotting, resource fragmentation, profiling anomalies, and benchmark/regression triggers.
- Does NOT replace existing runbooks for full outages; this doc is a precursor checklist and routing map.

Owner & Contacts
------------------
- Document owner: Lara Chen (runtime reliability)
- Primary on-call: runtime-oncall rotation (pager duty group: RD_runtime)
- Secondary escalation: eng-sre@redwood.com
- Model-serving kernel owner: Miguel Santos
- Observability steward: Asha Kapoor
- Slack channels: #runtime-oncall, #runtime-observability, #kernel-dev

Signal Catalog (by subsystem)
----------------------------
1) Scheduler and kernel signals
   - Symptom: Per-project kernel queue length climbs steadily and queue drain rate drops.
     - Why this matters: indicates admission or kernel-backpressure misprediction causing latency stacking.
     - PromQL example: 
       sum(rate(runtime_kernel_queue_length[1m])) by (kernel_variant, project) / sum(rate(runtime_kernel_dispatches[1m])) by (kernel_variant, project)
     - Threshold guidance: sustained ratio > 3 for 2m for a single project => investigate.
     - Quick checks:
       1. Check kernel variant logs: kubectl logs -n runtime kernel-<id> --since=5m
       2. Confirm admission tokens and quota-balance: GET /admin/quota?project=<id> (internal API)
       3. Inspect preemption events: PromQL: sum(increase(kernel_preempt_events_total[5m]))
   - Escalation: if ratio persists > 3 for 10m or 50% of requests to that project exceed 95th p50 latency SLO, escalate to kernel-owner.

2) KV cache and session signals
   - Symptom: KV cache hit-rate drops and cold-start ratio increases for median session length > 4 tokens.
     - Why: high cold cache rates increase per-token cost and tail latency.
     - Query examples:
       - Embedding of hitrate: rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m]) / rate(kvcache_lookups_total[5m])
       - Cold start indicator: rate(session_warmup_failures[5m])
     - Thresholds: hit-rate < 80% sustained for 15m bucket on a top-10 route => schedule kvcache warmup investigation.
   - Checklist:
     1. Compare session fingerprint distribution with baseline (last 7d).
     2. Verify async prefetcher logs and backpressure conf (see config table).

3) Latency drift and tail amplification
   - Symptom: P95/P99 shows divergence from P50 by more than historical variance.
     - Diagnostic query:
       - p50: histogram_quantile(0.5, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, route))
       - p95/p99 similar.
     - Guideline: If (p99/p50) increases > 4x baseline for a route for two consecutive 5m windows, run tracer-based decomposition.
   - Quick triage:
     1. Grab a compact trace (trace id sampling endpoint) for a sample of slow requests.
     2. Run `trace-replay --sample <trace-id>` in the dev sandbox to reproduce.

4) Kernel regression and microbenchmark signals
   - Symptom: Microbenchmark suite shows a >3% regression on token throughput for a kernel variant between consecutive releases.
     - Why: kernel-level regression often causes subtle production degradation under burst load.
     - Bench protocol: run microbenchmark generator with synthetic token sequences (stable seed), measure tokens/s median and p99.
     - Promoted action: Block release if regression persists after a quick bisect.

5) Profiling pipeline and telemetry health
   - Symptom: Profiling sampler drop rate > 20% or trace baggage missing fields (kernel_id, slot_id).
     - PromQL: rate(profiling_samples_dropped_total[5m]) / rate(profiling_samples_total[5m])
     - Checklist: verify agent version compatibility, check sidecar obfuscation rules, confirm sampling egress to observability pipeline.

6) Benchmarking/Regression gate signals
   - Symptom: Canary benchmark shows fidelity gap > configured gate or cost per token rises > 8% for top workload.
     - Table: Benchmark gate example thresholds:

   | Metric                      | Gate threshold (minor) | Gate threshold (blocker) | Action         |
   | --------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------ | -------------- |
   | Median tokens/s (workload)  | 3% drop                | >6% drop                 | Triage / Block |
   | Per-token median cost       | 5% increase            | >10% increase            | Block release   |
   | Synthetic P95 latency       | 5% rise                | >12% rise                | Triage / Block |

   - Release rule: If any blocker thresholds hit on canary cluster, stop rollout and open a regression ticket.

Known-issue precursors (examples)
--------------------------------
- NVLink flapping: precursor is increased nvlink_error_rate metric plus asymmetric GPU dispatch times. Mitigation: cordon host and drain sessions with graceful transfer.
- Async kernel deadlock patterns: precursor is sustained increase in kernel_lock_wait_time_avg with no corresponding increase in dispatch rate. Immediate mitigation: escalate and apply emergency kernel patch.

Runbook: rapid triage checklist (on-call)
----------------------------------------
1. Verify alert vs precursor: confirm telemetry source and time window.
2. Scope: Isolated to single project, single cluster, or global? Use these queries: 
   - Projects impacted: topk(10, sum(rate(runtime_request_errors_total[5m])) by (project))
   - Clusters impacted: sum by (cluster) ...
3. Quick containment: apply temporary degradation policy for impacted project (API: POST /admin/degradation?project=<id>&mode=graceful)
4. Collect artifacts: kernel logs, compact traces (up to 10 samples), kvcache snapshots, microbenchmark run used for gating.
5. If > 10% of traffic to any route is impacted and duration > 10m, follow full incident process and notify eng-sre.

Escalation matrix
------------------
- Level 1: runtime-oncall (investigate, collect artifacts, apply containment)
- Level 2 (after 10m of sustained impact or major customers affected): paging to kernel-owner + eng-sre
- Level 3: Pager to oncall-lead and product/security if customer data or SLA breach risk exists

CLI & Admin commands (examples)
------------------------------
- Fetch kernel queue status: curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://admin.redwood.internal/runtime/kernel_status?kernel=KERNEL_ID
- Temporary route degradation: curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"project":"proj-123","mode":"best-effort","ttl":600}' https://admin.redwood.internal/runtime/degradation
- Force kvcache warmup for route: rdc kvcache warmup --route /chat/stream --count 200 --timeout 120s

Post-incident handoff & RCA starter template
-------------------------------------------
- Minimum required artifacts: list of traces (IDs), a reproducible microbenchmark script and seeds, snapshot of kernel variant config, and the canary benchmark run that first flagged the regression.
- RCA template fields: Summary, Timeline (UTC), Root cause hypothesis, Evidence, Corrective actions, Preventative actions, Owners, and Follow-up verification steps.

Automation & Dashboard suggestions
----------------------------------
- Add a combined "pre-cursor index" dashboard that weights signals (scheduler queue ratio, kvcache dip, profiler drop rate, microbenchmark delta) and surfaces top-10 routes/projects with the highest risk score.
- Add automated synthetic trace replays when the pre-cursor index breaches medium threshold for a route.

Appendix: config table (sampling and kvcache)
----------------------------------------------
| Config key                       | Default    | Notes                                   |
|----------------------------------|------------|-----------------------------------------|
| profiling.sampler.rate           | 0.01       | fraction of requests sampled            |
| profiling.max_inflight_traces    | 200        | guard to avoid pipeline saturation      |
| kvcache.async_prefetch.enabled   | true       | improves warmup for conversational apps |
| kvcache.prefetch_concurrency     | 8          | tuned per-cluster based on latency SLOs |

Related pages
-------------
- See: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-incident-orchestration-and-capacity-decision-matrix-2026
- See: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/model-variant-selection-cost-latency-tradeoff-matrix-2024

Revision history
----------------
- 2024-11-15: Created by Lara Chen
- 2025-01-12: Added canary benchmark gates and CLI snippets (reviewers: Maya Alvarez)
- 2025-06-10: Updated escalation timings and thresholds after Q1 incident retro (reviewers: Ethan Park, Jonah Patel)

title: Runtime precursor signal catalog and escalation checklist
preview:
Overview
--------
This document is a cross-cutting catalog of PRECURSOR signals the runtime team uses to detect emerging problems across the serving runtime, kernel scheduler, profiling pipeline, and benchmarking surface. It pairs each signal with a short investigation checklist, automated queries (PromQL / SQL / CLI examples), threshold guidance, and structured escalation steps. The goal is to surface problems earlier than full SLA violations and standardize triage across on-call, SRE, and runtime engineers.

Scope
------
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where the runtime agent and dispatcher emit telemetry into the telemetry stack.
- Covers signal classes: scheduler contention, kernel regressions, KV cache cold/warm signals, sequence-tail latency drift, single-tenant hot-spotting, resource fragmentation, profiling anomalies, and benchmark/regression triggers.
- Does NOT replace existing runbooks for full outages; this doc is a precursor checklist and routing map.

Owner & Contacts
------------------
- Document owner: Lara Chen (runtime reliability)
- Primary on-call: runtime-oncall rotation (pager duty group: RD_runtime)
- Secondary escalation: eng-sre@redwood.com
- Model-serving kernel owner: Miguel Santos
- Observability steward: Asha Kapoor
- Slack channels: #runtime-oncall, #runtime-observability, #kernel-dev

Signal Catalog (by subsystem)
----------------------------
1) Scheduler and kernel signals
   - Symptom: Per-project kernel queue length climbs steadily and queue drain rate drops.
     - Why this matters: indicates admission or kernel-backpressure misprediction causing latency stacking.
     - PromQL example: 
       sum(rate(runtime_kernel_queue_length[1m])) by (kernel_variant, project) / sum(rate(runtime_kernel_dispatches[1m])) by (kernel_variant, project)
     - Threshold guidance: sustained ratio > 3 for 2m for a single project => investigate.
     - Quick checks:
       1. Check kernel variant logs: kubectl logs -n runtime kernel-<id> --since=5m
       2. Confirm admission tokens and quota-balance: GET /admin/quota?project=<id> (internal API)
       3. Inspect preemption events: PromQL: sum(increase(kernel_preempt_events_total[5m]))
   - Escalation: if ratio persists > 3 for 10m or 50% of requests to that project exceed 95th p50 latency SLO, escalate to kernel-owner.

2) KV cache and session signals
   - Symptom: KV cache hit-rate drops and cold-start ratio increases for median session length > 4 tokens.
     - Why: high cold cache rates increase per-token cost and tail latency.
     - Query examples:
       - Embedding of hitrate: rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m]) / rate(kvcache_lookups_total[5m])
       - Cold start indicator: rate(session_warmup_failures[5m])
     - Thresholds: hit-rate < 80% sustained for 15m bucket on a top-10 route => schedule kvcache warmup investigation.
   - Checklist:
     1. Compare session fingerprint distribution with baseline (last 7d).
     2. Verify async prefetcher logs and backpressure conf (see config table).

3) Latency drift and tail amplification
   - Symptom: P95/P99 shows divergence from P50 by more than historical variance.
     - Diagnostic query:
       - p50: histogram_quantile(0.5, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, route))
       - p95/p99 similar.
     - Guideline: If (p99/p50) increases > 4x baseline for a route for two consecutive 5m windows, run tracer-based decomposition.
   - Quick triage:
     1. Grab a compact trace (trace id sampling endpoint) for a sample of slow requests.
     2. Run `trace-replay --sample <trace-id>` in the dev sandbox to reproduce.

4) Kernel regression and microbenchmark signals
   - Symptom: Microbenchmark suite shows a >3% regression on token throughput for a kernel variant between consecutive releases.
     - Why: kernel-level regression often causes subtle production degradation under burst load.
     - Bench protocol: run microbenchmark generator with synthetic token sequences (stable seed), measure tokens/s median and p99.
     - Promoted action: Block release if regression persists after a quick bisect.

5) Profiling pipeline and telemetry health
   - Symptom: Profiling sampler drop rate > 20% or trace baggage missing fields (kernel_id, slot_id).
     - PromQL: rate(profiling_samples_dropped_total[5m]) / rate(profiling_samples_total[5m])
     - Checklist: verify agent version compatibility, check sidecar obfuscation rules, confirm sampling egress to observability pipeline.

6) Benchmarking/Regression gate signals
   - Symptom: Canary benchmark shows fidelity gap > configured gate or cost per token rises > 8% for top workload.
     - Table: Benchmark gate example thresholds:

   | Metric                      | Gate threshold (minor) | Gate threshold (blocker) | Action         |
   | --------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------ | -------------- |
   | Median tokens/s (workload)  | 3% drop                | >6% drop                 | Triage / Block |
   | Per-token median cost       | 5% increase            | >10% increase            | Block release   |
   | Synthetic P95 latency       | 5% rise                | >12% rise                | Triage / Block |

   - Release rule: If any blocker thresholds hit on canary cluster, stop rollout and open a regression ticket.

Known-issue precursors (examples)
--------------------------------
- NVLink flapping: precursor is increased nvlink_error_rate metric plus asymmetric GPU dispatch times. Mitigation: cordon host and drain sessions with graceful transfer.
- Async kernel deadlock patterns: precursor is sustained increase in kernel_lock_wait_time_avg with no corresponding increase in dispatch rate. Immediate mitigation: escalate and apply emergency kernel patch.

Runbook: rapid triage checklist (on-call)
----------------------------------------
1. Verify alert vs precursor: confirm telemetry source and time window.
2. Scope: Isolated to single project, single cluster, or global? Use these queries: 
   - Projects impacted: topk(10, sum(rate(runtime_request_errors_total[5m])) by (project))
   - Clusters impacted: sum by (cluster) ...
3. Quick containment: apply temporary degradation policy for impacted project (API: POST /admin/degradation?project=<id>&mode=graceful)
4. Collect artifacts: kernel logs, compact traces (up to 10 samples), kvcache snapshots, microbenchmark run used for gating.
5. If > 10% of traffic to any route is impacted and duration > 10m, follow full incident process and notify eng-sre.

Escalation matrix
------------------
- Level 1: runtime-oncall (investigate, collect artifacts, apply containment)
- Level 2 (after 10m of sustained impact or major customers affected): paging to kernel-owner + eng-sre
- Level 3: Pager to oncall-lead and product/security if customer data or SLA breach risk exists

CLI & Admin commands (examples)
------------------------------
- Fetch kernel queue status: curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://admin.redwood.internal/runtime/kernel_status?kernel=KERNEL_ID
- Temporary route degradation: curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"project":"proj-123","mode":"best-effort","ttl":600}' https://admin.redwood.internal/runtime/degradation
- Force kvcache warmup for route: rdc kvcache warmup --route /chat/stream --count 200 --timeout 120s

Post-incident handoff & RCA starter template
-------------------------------------------
- Minimum required artifacts: list of traces (IDs), a reproducible microbenchmark script and seeds, snapshot of kernel variant config, and the canary benchmark run that first flagged the regression.
- RCA template fields: Summary, Timeline (UTC), Root cause hypothesis, Evidence, Corrective actions, Preventative actions, Owners, and Follow-up verification steps.

Automation & Dashboard suggestions
----------------------------------
- Add a combined "pre-cursor index" dashboard that weights signals (scheduler queue ratio, kvcache dip, profiler drop rate, microbenchmark delta) and surfaces top-10 routes/projects with the highest risk score.
- Add automated synthetic trace replays when the pre-cursor index breaches medium threshold for a route.

Appendix: config table (sampling and kvcache)
----------------------------------------------
| Config key                       | Default    | Notes                                   |
|----------------------------------|------------|-----------------------------------------|
| profiling.sampler.rate           | 0.01       | fraction of requests sampled            |
| profiling.max_inflight_traces    | 200        | guard to avoid pipeline saturation      |
| kvcache.async_prefetch.enabled   | true       | improves warmup for conversational apps |
| kvcache.prefetch_concurrency     | 8          | tuned per-cluster based on latency SLOs |

Related pages
-------------
- See: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-incident-orchestration-and-capacity-decision-matrix-2026
- See: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/model-variant-selection-cost-latency-tradeoff-matrix-2024

Revision history
----------------
- 2024-11-15: Created by Lara Chen
- 2025-01-12: Added canary benchmark gates and CLI snippets (reviewers: Maya Alvarez)
- 2025-06-10: Updated escalation timings and thresholds after Q1 incident retro (reviewers: Ethan Park, Jonah Patel)
