## Changes by Category
### Security
- **[No user-visible change] Container hardening refresh**
  - What changed: Base image packages updated to include latest non-breaking security patches.
  - Customer impact: No runtime behavior change expected.
  - Risk level: Low
  - Validation: Image scan + runtime smoke suite.
  - References: PR #23305

### Reliability / Correctness
- **[Fix] Prevent crash in KV cache compaction (Sev1)**
  - What changed: Fixed an out-of-bounds access during compaction when eviction coincides with batch boundary.
  - Customer impact: Prevents process crash; reduces request failures under long-context, high-concurrency loads.
  - Risk level: Medium
  - Validation: Added targeted regression test + verified perf gates (no regressions beyond thresholds).
  - References: PR #23341; Support: SUP-18544

- **[Fix] Scheduler starvation edge case under bursty traffic**
  - What changed: Adjusted fairness logic to prevent a specific queue pattern from starving long requests.
  - Customer impact: More predictable p95 under bursts; fewer tail latency spikes.
  - Risk level: Medium
  - Validation: Scheduler regression suite + dedicated burst test.
  - References: PR #23457

### Performance / Efficiency
- **[Behavior change] Disable experimental batching heuristic**
  - What changed: Removed the experimental heuristic from LTS defaults.
  - Customer impact: May reduce peak throughput by ~0–3% on highly uniform short prompts; improves predictability on mixed-length workloads.
  - Risk level: Low
  - Validation: Perf gates on canonical suite; Dedicated canary observed stable p95.
  - References: PR #23366

### Compatibility / Packaging
- **[Improvement] Artifact provenance and checksums for LTS images**
  - What changed: Published SHA256SUMS and provenance attestation for runtime artifacts.
  - Customer impact: Enables verification for regulated environments.
  - Risk level: Low
  - Validation: Release CI verifies checksum generation and publication.
  - References: PR #23305

### Observability / Operability
- **[Improvement] Release-branch scheduler smoke tests**
  - What changed: Added targeted regression tests for backpressure/overload behavior.
  - Customer impact: Reduced risk of regressions in future LTS backports.
  - Risk level: Low
  - Validation: CI gating on release branch.
  - References: PR #23258

### Deprecations / Behavior Changes
- (No entries)

### Documentation
- **[Improvement] Document runtime LTS channel and backport policy**
  - What changed: Added customer-facing explanation of LTS semantics and upgrade guidance.
  - Customer impact: Clarifies pinning and support window.
  - Risk level: Low
  - Validation: Doc review.
  - References: PR #1442 (docs)

## Performance Notes
- Perf gate status: Pass
- Bench suite: LTS Canonical v3 (perf-canary artifact: https://perf.redwood.example/gates/runtime-1.18-lts/1.18.1)
- GPU coverage: A100 80GB, H100 SXM, L40S
- Summary deltas vs 1.18.0-lts baseline:
  - Latency p95: +0.4% (within thresholds)
  - Throughput tokens/sec: -1.2% (expected due to batching heuristic disable; within thresholds)
  - TTFT p95: -0.3% (no meaningful change)
- Variance note: All benchmarks rerun 2x; variance < policy threshold.

## Upgrade Notes
### Hosted API
- Rollout: Phased rollout started 2025-03-17; global rollout expected within 72 hours barring SLO alerts.
- Customer action: None.

### Dedicated
- Recommended: Canary 10% traffic for 2 hours, then 50% for 4 hours, then full.
- Monitor: p95 latency, request error rate, scheduler queue depth, GPU utilization.
- Rollback: Pin runtime image back to `1.18.0-lts` (previous patch) and restart runtime pods.

### Private
- Pinning:
  - Installer: set `runtime.imageTag=1.18.1-lts` and verify SHA256SUMS before apply.
  - Helm: set `runtime.channel=lts` and `runtime.tag=1.18.1-lts` (chart `redwood-runtime-0.33.0+`).
- Validation checklist:
  - Run `/healthz` and `/readyz`
  - Execute smoke prompts (short + long context)
  - Confirm p95 latency within local baseline +/- 5%
- Rollback:
  - Revert tag to `1.18.0-lts`, re-apply, and restart runtime.
  - Keep the prior image cached for faster rollback where possible.

## Known Issues
- **Intermittent perf gate flake on L40S under runner contention**
  - Affected versions: benchmarking infrastructure only; runtime unaffected
  - Symptoms: perf gate variance spikes causing reruns
  - Mitigation: rerun gate on dedicated runner pool
  - Status: Workaround available
  - Support guidance: include perf-canary run ID and runner host ID

## Security Notes
- No CVEs addressed in this release.

## Appendix
- Full PR list: https://github.redwood.example/redwood/releases/tag/runtime-v1.18.1-lts
- Backport tracker: https://docs.redwood.example/sheets/lts-backports/runtime-1.18-lts

---

## Publication checklist (release notes specific)
Before publishing LTS notes:
1) Ensure required metadata fields are complete (especially provenance + checksums).
2) Confirm “Performance Notes” includes gate status and links.
3) Confirm “Known Issues” is snapshotted from the living list.
4) Ensure any behavior change includes rollback guidance.
5) If security-related, ensure CVE format matches standard and comms is aligned with Security Response.

## Ownership
- Format owner: Elliot Price (Docs / Release Notes)
- Release execution: Connor O'Brien (Release Eng)
- Perf gates/policy: Marcus Reed (Perf suite) + Jonas Weber (advisor)
- Compatibility matrix: Quentin Harris
- Security notes: Dr. Aisha Rahman (policy sponsor) + Hanae Suzuki (coordination)

source.title: Quantization profile selection in runtime (v1)
source.author: Noah Patel
source.content:
## Summary
This document describes how the Serving Runtime selects and applies an opt-in Quantization Profile (QP) for a request. The goals for v1 are:

- Deterministic selection and application (same inputs => same profile decision).
- Safety by default (feature off => no behavior change; incompatible/failed apply => safe fallback).
- Low overhead at high QPS (aggressive caching; bounded fetch retries; no thundering herd).
- Clear observability (request-level attribution and aggregate metrics for applied vs fallback).

This page focuses on the runtime path only. Compatibility policy evaluation and route config resolution occur in the control plane/policy engine; the runtime consumes the resolved decision.

## Terminology
- **Route**: customer-configured endpoint mapping to a model variant + settings.
- **Resolved quant decision**: the policy engine output attached to the request (or retrieved from runtime-side config cache) that includes whether quantization is enabled and which profile ref (if any) is approved.
- **Profile ref**: stable identifier referencing a specific, versioned profile artifact in the model registry (e.g., `qp:llama3.1-70b-instruct:int8_w8a16:v3`).
- **Baseline**: default precision path for the model variant when QP is disabled or cannot be applied.
- **Fallback**: runtime behavior where it runs baseline even if QP was requested, due to incompatibility, kill-switch, fetch/verification failure, or runtime constraints.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Auto-tuning or per-tenant profile recommendations.
- Runtime-side “best effort” selection among multiple profiles.
- Silent upgrades from one profile version to another without an explicit ref change.

## High-level design
### Inputs to runtime
The runtime makes its decision using the following inputs, in priority order:

1) **Global kill-switch** (platform config propagated to runtime): if disabled, always run baseline.
2) **Resolved quant decision** for this request (from the router / policy evaluation layer): includes `enabled` + `profile_ref` (versioned) or `default`.
3) **Local runtime capability + current execution regime**: GPU SKU, driver/toolkit capabilities, max context constraints, kernel support, and memory/KV cache constraints.

The runtime does **not** independently choose a profile. It either:
- Applies the exact versioned `profile_ref`, or
- Falls back to baseline (with explicit reason codes).

### Components
- **QuantProfileManager**: runtime subsystem responsible for fetching, verifying, caching, and exposing an in-memory compiled representation of a profile artifact.
- **ExecutionPlanner**: selects kernel path and sets execution flags (e.g., int8 GEMMs, fp8 attention path) based on (model, profile, GPU capabilities, sequence lengths).
- **RequestContext annotator**: propagates `quant_profile_id`, apply outcome, and reason to logs/traces/metrics.

### Data flow (text diagram)
1. Request arrives with `route_id`, `model_id`, `model_variant`, `region`, and policy-resolved fields.
2. Runtime checks kill-switch and request-level decision.
3. If QP is enabled and a versioned `profile_ref` is present:
   - QuantProfileManager loads profile from cache or fetches from registry.
   - Verifies signature + schema version.
   - Produces `CompiledProfile` (normalized, validated fields; precomputed kernel constraints).
4. ExecutionPlanner merges:
   - model architecture traits,
   - CompiledProfile settings,
   - runtime hardware capabilities,
   - request regime (prompt length, max_tokens, streaming mode),
   into an `ExecutionPlan`.
5. Runtime executes with plan; emits signals.
6. On any failure condition, runtime switches to baseline for that request and records fallback.

## Determinism guarantees
Determinism is defined as: for a given request (route, model variant, region/tier) under a fixed runtime build + fixed platform config + fixed profile artifact version, the runtime will make the same apply/fallback decision and execute the same quant mode.

### Determinism rules
- The runtime only accepts **versioned** profile refs for application. Aliases like `recommended` or `latest` must be resolved to a versioned ref by the control plane before reaching runtime.
- Profile artifacts are content-addressed and signed. The runtime pins the exact artifact digest in memory once fetched.
- The apply decision is purely a function of:
  - `kill_switch_state`,
  - request’s `profile_ref` (versioned),
  - runtime capabilities (GPU SKU class, supported kernels),
  - profile constraints (min driver/toolkit, max context, known caveats).
- Runtime time-of-day, cache state, or background refresh must not change the selected profile version for a request.

### Cache determinism
- Cache miss may add latency but must not change the decision outcome. If a profile cannot be fetched/verified within bounds, runtime falls back for that request.
- Background refresh may prefetch newer versions, but v1 does not switch versions without an updated `profile_ref` in policy output.

## Profile load path
### Profile artifact types
A v1 profile is represented as a registry artifact bundle (conceptual):
- `profile.json` (schema v1, signed)
- optional calibration metadata references
- runtime-specific kernel hint fields (e.g., allowed quant methods)

### Fetch and verification
QuantProfileManager performs:

1) **Lookup** by `profile_ref` in local in-memory LRU cache.
2) On miss: **fetch** profile metadata from model registry (via registry client with:
   - request coalescing,
   - per-host rate limits,
   - jittered retries,
   - circuit breaker).
3) **Integrity checks**:
   - signature verification,
   - schema version check,
   - digest match.
4) **Normalization/compile** into `CompiledProfile`:
   - canonical enums,
   - derived constraints (e.g., max supported context for this profile),
   - prevalidated compatibility flags.
5) Store in cache keyed by (`profile_ref`, `artifact_digest`).

### Timeouts and retry policy
- Metadata fetch timeout: 150ms (p95 target), hard cap 300ms.
- Artifact fetch timeout: 300ms (p95 target), hard cap 600ms.
- Retries: up to 2 with exponential backoff + jitter; retries are disabled when circuit breaker is open.
- If any stage exceeds hard cap, runtime falls back to baseline for that request.

### Prefetching
Runtime may prefetch profiles for hot routes during warmup based on:
- recently observed `profile_ref`s,
- rollout canary configuration,
- explicit prewarm list in runtime config.

Prefetching is best-effort and must not block readiness.

## Apply decision logic
### Decision table
| Condition | Outcome | Observability reason |
|---|---|---|
| kill-switch disabled | baseline | `kill_switch_disabled` |
| request quant disabled | baseline | `disabled_by_route` |
| request quant enabled, but no versioned profile_ref | baseline | `missing_profile_ref` |
| profile fetch/verify fails | baseline | `profile_load_failed` |
| runtime capability incompatible (kernel/GPU/toolkit) | baseline | `runtime_incompatible` |
| request regime violates profile constraints (e.g., context) | baseline | `request_regime_incompatible` |
| apply succeeds | quantized | `applied` |

### Pseudocode
```
if !GlobalConfig.quant_profiles_enabled:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=kill_switch_disabled)

if !req.quant.enabled:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=disabled_by_route)

ref = req.quant.profile_ref
if ref is null or not is_versioned(ref):
  return BaselinePlan(reason=missing_profile_ref)

profile = QuantProfileManager.get(ref)  // may fetch/verify
if profile.error:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=profile_load_failed, err=profile.error_code)

if !CapabilityChecker.supports(profile, gpu, toolkit, driver):
  return BaselinePlan(reason=runtime_incompatible)

if !RegimeChecker.supports(profile, req.max_context, req.seq_len_distribution_hint):
  return BaselinePlan(reason=request_regime_incompatible)

plan = ExecutionPlanner.plan(model, profile, gpu, req)
if plan.error:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=runtime_incompatible, err=plan.error_code)

return plan.with_quant(profile_id=profile.id)
```

## Kernel selection interactions
### Overview
Quantization profiles constrain kernel selection. The ExecutionPlanner treats the profile as an additional set of hard constraints and preferences.

Key interactions:
- Mixed precision paths (e.g., **INT8 weights** + **FP16 activations**) must select kernels that match both precision and layout assumptions.
- Some attention kernels require specific accumulation precision; profiles may force alternative kernels.
- Profiles may cap max context length due to KV cache format or kernel limitations.

### Supported quant methods (v1)
The runtime supports a limited set per model family (enforced by policy + runtime guards). Examples:
- `int8_w8a16` (weights INT8, activations FP16/BF16)
- `fp8_w8a8` (weights FP8, activations FP8) where supported
- `int4_w4a16` is NOT supported in v1 runtime apply path unless explicitly allowlisted per model family (guarded).

### Selection rules
- If the profile specifies a method not supported by the runtime build on this host, fallback.
- If the profile is supported but a particular request regime (context length) exceeds kernel constraints, fallback for that request.
- Kernel selection is deterministic given:
  - model architecture,
  - profile method,
  - GPU SKU class,
  - request regime.

### KV cache and memory interactions
Quantization profiles can affect:
- **VRAM footprint**: reduced weight memory; potential increased workspace for some kernels.
- **KV cache format**: v1 does not change KV precision as part of QP unless explicitly encoded and supported; otherwise KV cache remains baseline format.

Runtime guardrails:
- If applying a profile would force an unsupported KV cache format transition mid-stream, fallback.
- If the request would exceed a memory safety threshold (including KV cache reservation), fallback rather than OOM.

### Continuous batching
When continuous batching combines multiple requests:
- The runtime groups requests by compatible `ExecutionPlan` characteristics.
- Requests with different `quant_profile_id` are not co-batched unless the kernel path and layout are identical (v1: treat different profile IDs as different batch groups).
- If batching pressure would violate latency SLOs, the scheduler may reduce batch size but must not change quant mode.

## Failure modes and safe fallbacks
### Fallback principles
- Fallback is per-request and must be fast.
- No retry loops between quantized and baseline execution.
- Fallback never attempts to “pick another profile” in v1.

### Common failure modes
- Registry metadata fetch latency spike (mitigated by caching, coalescing, and rate limiting).
- Signature verification failure (treated as hard failure; fallback + alert).
- Kernel mismatch due to driver/toolkit mismatch on a subset of hosts.
- Unexpected request regime (very long context) violating profile constraints.

### Error handling
- If failure occurs before execution starts: fallback to baseline.
- If failure occurs during execution plan compilation: fallback to baseline.
- If failure occurs after partial execution has started: v1 does not attempt mid-flight switching. The request fails with a structured error if it is unsafe to continue; this is counted separately from fallback metrics and should page only if it breaches error budget.

## Observability
### Request annotations
The runtime attaches the following fields to logs and traces:
- `quant_profile_id`: resolved profile id (string) when applied; empty when baseline.
- `quant_profile_ref`: the ref requested (versioned) when present.
- `quant_outcome`: `applied | fallback | disabled`.
- `quant_fallback_reason`: enum reason code (see Decision table).
- `quant_profile_digest`: artifact digest when applied (for forensic pinning).

### Metrics (recommended names)
Counters:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.requests_total{outcome, reason, model_id, route_id, region}`
- `runtime.quant_profiles.profile_load_failures_total{error_code, model_id, region}`

Gauges:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.cache_entries{region}`
- `runtime.quant_profiles.kill_switch_enabled{region}`

Histograms:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.profile_fetch_latency_ms{stage}` (metadata vs artifact vs verify)
- `runtime.latency.decode_ms{quant_enabled}` (to compare tail)
- `runtime.cost.tokens_per_second{quant_enabled}` (where available)

### Dashboards and alerts
Runtime-owned panels should include:
- Applied rate vs fallback rate by route/model.
- Fallback reason breakdown (top N) to quickly identify incompatibility vs registry issues.
- Profile load p95/p99 latency.
- Correlation between quant enabled and p95 decode latency.

Alerts:
- High `fallback_rate` for a specific route/model (suggests incompatibility or regression).
- Spike in `profile_load_failures_total` (suggests registry/signing/caching issue).
- Any signature verification failures > 0 in a 10m window (security + correctness).

## Performance considerations
- Cache lookup must be O(1) and avoid allocations in the hot path.
- Fetch path is isolated and rate limited; do not allow high-QPS routes to overwhelm registry.
- Profile compilation should be cheap; heavy preprocessing belongs in build-time artifact generation.

## Security and integrity
- All profile artifacts must be signed by the model registry signing key.
- Runtime verifies signature and digest before applying.
- Verification failures are treated as suspicious; emit a security audit event (internal) and fallback.

## Rollout considerations
- Feature must be safe when disabled.
- Kill-switch must immediately force baseline without requiring profile cache invalidation.
- During canary, monitor:
  - applied rate,
  - fallback reason distribution,
  - tail latency deltas,
  - error rate deltas.

## Testing strategy (runtime)
- Unit tests:
  - determinism of decision function given fixed inputs,
  - compatibility checks for supported/unsupported GPU SKUs,
  - caching behavior (single fetch per ref under concurrency).
- Integration tests:
  - end-to-end: policy-resolved profile ref -> runtime apply -> log annotations.
  - kill-switch overrides any route enablement.
- Perf canary scenarios:
  - baseline vs quant enabled for representative prompt+decode lengths.
  - regression thresholds on decode p95 and throughput.

## Open questions / future work
- Allow co-batching across different profile IDs when kernel/layout are identical (may improve throughput; requires careful accounting).
- Extend regime hints from router (sequence length distribution) to reduce fallback surprises.
- Support optional KV cache precision changes as a separate, explicitly gated profile capability.

- Fallback is per-request and must be fast.
- No retry loops between quantized and baseline execution.
- Fallback never attempts to “pick another profile” in v1.

### Common failure modes
- Registry metadata fetch latency spike (mitigated by caching, coalescing, and rate limiting).
- Signature verification failure (treated as hard failure; fallback + alert).
- Kernel mismatch due to driver/toolkit mismatch on a subset of hosts.
- Unexpected request regime (very long context) violating profile constraints.

### Error handling
- If failure occurs before execution starts: fallback to baseline.
- If failure occurs during execution plan compilation: fallback to baseline.
- If failure occurs after partial execution has started: v1 does not attempt mid-flight switching. The request fails with a structured error if it is unsafe to continue; this is counted separately from fallback metrics and should page only if it breaches error budget.

## Observability
### Request annotations
The runtime attaches the following fields to logs and traces:
- `quant_profile_id`: resolved profile id (string) when applied; empty when baseline.
- `quant_profile_ref`: the ref requested (versioned) when present.
- `quant_outcome`: `applied | fallback | disabled`.
- `quant_fallback_reason`: enum reason code (see Decision table).
- `quant_profile_digest`: artifact digest when applied (for forensic pinning).

### Metrics (recommended names)
Counters:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.requests_total{outcome, reason, model_id, route_id, region}`
- `runtime.quant_profiles.profile_load_failures_total{error_code, model_id, region}`

Gauges:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.cache_entries{region}`
- `runtime.quant_profiles.kill_switch_enabled{region}`

Histograms:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.profile_fetch_latency_ms{stage}` (metadata vs artifact vs verify)
- `runtime.latency.decode_ms{quant_enabled}` (to compare tail)
- `runtime.cost.tokens_per_second{quant_enabled}` (where available)

### Dashboards and alerts
Runtime-owned panels should include:
- Applied rate vs fallback rate by route/model.
- Fallback reason breakdown (top N) to quickly identify incompatibility vs registry issues.
- Profile load p95/p99 latency.
- Correlation between quant enabled and p95 decode latency.

Alerts:
- High `fallback_rate` for a specific route/model (suggests incompatibility or regression).
- Spike in `profile_load_failures_total` (suggests registry/signing/caching issue).
- Any signature verification failures > 0 in a 10m window (security + correctness).

## Performance considerations
- Cache lookup must be O(1) and avoid allocations in the hot path.
- Fetch path is isolated and rate limited; do not allow high-QPS routes to overwhelm registry.
- Profile compilation should be cheap; heavy preprocessing belongs in build-time artifact generation.

## Security and integrity
- All profile artifacts must be signed by the model registry signing key.
- Runtime verifies signature and digest before applying.
- Verification failures are treated as suspicious; emit a security audit event (internal) and fallback.

## Rollout considerations
- Feature must be safe when disabled.
- Kill-switch must immediately force baseline without requiring profile cache invalidation.
- During canary, monitor:
  - applied rate,
  - fallback reason distribution,
  - tail latency deltas,
  - error rate deltas.

## Testing strategy (runtime)
- Unit tests:
  - determinism of decision function given fixed inputs,
  - compatibility checks for supported/unsupported GPU SKUs,
  - caching behavior (single fetch per ref under concurrency).
- Integration tests:
  - end-to-end: policy-resolved profile ref -> runtime apply -> log annotations.
  - kill-switch overrides any route enablement.
- Perf canary scenarios:
  - baseline vs quant enabled for representative prompt+decode lengths.
  - regression thresholds on decode p95 and throughput.

## Open questions / future work
- Allow co-batching across different profile IDs when kernel/layout are identical (may improve throughput; requires careful accounting).
- Extend regime hints from router (sequence length distribution) to reduce fallback surprises.
- Support optional KV cache precision changes as a separate, explicitly gated profile capability.
text:
## Summary
This document describes how the Serving Runtime selects and applies an opt-in Quantization Profile (QP) for a request. The goals for v1 are:

- Deterministic selection and application (same inputs => same profile decision).
- Safety by default (feature off => no behavior change; incompatible/failed apply => safe fallback).
- Low overhead at high QPS (aggressive caching; bounded fetch retries; no thundering herd).
- Clear observability (request-level attribution and aggregate metrics for applied vs fallback).

This page focuses on the runtime path only. Compatibility policy evaluation and route config resolution occur in the control plane/policy engine; the runtime consumes the resolved decision.

## Terminology
- **Route**: customer-configured endpoint mapping to a model variant + settings.
- **Resolved quant decision**: the policy engine output attached to the request (or retrieved from runtime-side config cache) that includes whether quantization is enabled and which profile ref (if any) is approved.
- **Profile ref**: stable identifier referencing a specific, versioned profile artifact in the model registry (e.g., `qp:llama3.1-70b-instruct:int8_w8a16:v3`).
- **Baseline**: default precision path for the model variant when QP is disabled or cannot be applied.
- **Fallback**: runtime behavior where it runs baseline even if QP was requested, due to incompatibility, kill-switch, fetch/verification failure, or runtime constraints.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Auto-tuning or per-tenant profile recommendations.
- Runtime-side “best effort” selection among multiple profiles.
- Silent upgrades from one profile version to another without an explicit ref change.

## High-level design
### Inputs to runtime
The runtime makes its decision using the following inputs, in priority order:

1) **Global kill-switch** (platform config propagated to runtime): if disabled, always run baseline.
2) **Resolved quant decision** for this request (from the router / policy evaluation layer): includes `enabled` + `profile_ref` (versioned) or `default`.
3) **Local runtime capability + current execution regime**: GPU SKU, driver/toolkit capabilities, max context constraints, kernel support, and memory/KV cache constraints.

The runtime does **not** independently choose a profile. It either:
- Applies the exact versioned `profile_ref`, or
- Falls back to baseline (with explicit reason codes).

### Components
- **QuantProfileManager**: runtime subsystem responsible for fetching, verifying, caching, and exposing an in-memory compiled representation of a profile artifact.
- **ExecutionPlanner**: selects kernel path and sets execution flags (e.g., int8 GEMMs, fp8 attention path) based on (model, profile, GPU capabilities, sequence lengths).
- **RequestContext annotator**: propagates `quant_profile_id`, apply outcome, and reason to logs/traces/metrics.

### Data flow (text diagram)
1. Request arrives with `route_id`, `model_id`, `model_variant`, `region`, and policy-resolved fields.
2. Runtime checks kill-switch and request-level decision.
3. If QP is enabled and a versioned `profile_ref` is present:
   - QuantProfileManager loads profile from cache or fetches from registry.
   - Verifies signature + schema version.
   - Produces `CompiledProfile` (normalized, validated fields; precomputed kernel constraints).
4. ExecutionPlanner merges:
   - model architecture traits,
   - CompiledProfile settings,
   - runtime hardware capabilities,
   - request regime (prompt length, max_tokens, streaming mode),
   into an `ExecutionPlan`.
5. Runtime executes with plan; emits signals.
6. On any failure condition, runtime switches to baseline for that request and records fallback.

## Determinism guarantees
Determinism is defined as: for a given request (route, model variant, region/tier) under a fixed runtime build + fixed platform config + fixed profile artifact version, the runtime will make the same apply/fallback decision and execute the same quant mode.

### Determinism rules
- The runtime only accepts **versioned** profile refs for application. Aliases like `recommended` or `latest` must be resolved to a versioned ref by the control plane before reaching runtime.
- Profile artifacts are content-addressed and signed. The runtime pins the exact artifact digest in memory once fetched.
- The apply decision is purely a function of:
  - `kill_switch_state`,
  - request’s `profile_ref` (versioned),
  - runtime capabilities (GPU SKU class, supported kernels),
  - profile constraints (min driver/toolkit, max context, known caveats).
- Runtime time-of-day, cache state, or background refresh must not change the selected profile version for a request.

### Cache determinism
- Cache miss may add latency but must not change the decision outcome. If a profile cannot be fetched/verified within bounds, runtime falls back for that request.
- Background refresh may prefetch newer versions, but v1 does not switch versions without an updated `profile_ref` in policy output.

## Profile load path
### Profile artifact types
A v1 profile is represented as a registry artifact bundle (conceptual):
- `profile.json` (schema v1, signed)
- optional calibration metadata references
- runtime-specific kernel hint fields (e.g., allowed quant methods)

### Fetch and verification
QuantProfileManager performs:

1) **Lookup** by `profile_ref` in local in-memory LRU cache.
2) On miss: **fetch** profile metadata from model registry (via registry client with:
   - request coalescing,
   - per-host rate limits,
   - jittered retries,
   - circuit breaker).
3) **Integrity checks**:
   - signature verification,
   - schema version check,
   - digest match.
4) **Normalization/compile** into `CompiledProfile`:
   - canonical enums,
   - derived constraints (e.g., max supported context for this profile),
   - prevalidated compatibility flags.
5) Store in cache keyed by (`profile_ref`, `artifact_digest`).

### Timeouts and retry policy
- Metadata fetch timeout: 150ms (p95 target), hard cap 300ms.
- Artifact fetch timeout: 300ms (p95 target), hard cap 600ms.
- Retries: up to 2 with exponential backoff + jitter; retries are disabled when circuit breaker is open.
- If any stage exceeds hard cap, runtime falls back to baseline for that request.

### Prefetching
Runtime may prefetch profiles for hot routes during warmup based on:
- recently observed `profile_ref`s,
- rollout canary configuration,
- explicit prewarm list in runtime config.

Prefetching is best-effort and must not block readiness.

## Apply decision logic
### Decision table
| Condition | Outcome | Observability reason |
|---|---|---|
| kill-switch disabled | baseline | `kill_switch_disabled` |
| request quant disabled | baseline | `disabled_by_route` |
| request quant enabled, but no versioned profile_ref | baseline | `missing_profile_ref` |
| profile fetch/verify fails | baseline | `profile_load_failed` |
| runtime capability incompatible (kernel/GPU/toolkit) | baseline | `runtime_incompatible` |
| request regime violates profile constraints (e.g., context) | baseline | `request_regime_incompatible` |
| apply succeeds | quantized | `applied` |

### Pseudocode
```
if !GlobalConfig.quant_profiles_enabled:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=kill_switch_disabled)

if !req.quant.enabled:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=disabled_by_route)

ref = req.quant.profile_ref
if ref is null or not is_versioned(ref):
  return BaselinePlan(reason=missing_profile_ref)

profile = QuantProfileManager.get(ref)  // may fetch/verify
if profile.error:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=profile_load_failed, err=profile.error_code)

if !CapabilityChecker.supports(profile, gpu, toolkit, driver):
  return BaselinePlan(reason=runtime_incompatible)

if !RegimeChecker.supports(profile, req.max_context, req.seq_len_distribution_hint):
  return BaselinePlan(reason=request_regime_incompatible)

plan = ExecutionPlanner.plan(model, profile, gpu, req)
if plan.error:
  return BaselinePlan(reason=runtime_incompatible, err=plan.error_code)

return plan.with_quant(profile_id=profile.id)
```

## Kernel selection interactions
### Overview
Quantization profiles constrain kernel selection. The ExecutionPlanner treats the profile as an additional set of hard constraints and preferences.

Key interactions:
- Mixed precision paths (e.g., **INT8 weights** + **FP16 activations**) must select kernels that match both precision and layout assumptions.
- Some attention kernels require specific accumulation precision; profiles may force alternative kernels.
- Profiles may cap max context length due to KV cache format or kernel limitations.

### Supported quant methods (v1)
The runtime supports a limited set per model family (enforced by policy + runtime guards). Examples:
- `int8_w8a16` (weights INT8, activations FP16/BF16)
- `fp8_w8a8` (weights FP8, activations FP8) where supported
- `int4_w4a16` is NOT supported in v1 runtime apply path unless explicitly allowlisted per model family (guarded).

### Selection rules
- If the profile specifies a method not supported by the runtime build on this host, fallback.
- If the profile is supported but a particular request regime (context length) exceeds kernel constraints, fallback for that request.
- Kernel selection is deterministic given:
  - model architecture,
  - profile method,
  - GPU SKU class,
  - request regime.

### KV cache and memory interactions
Quantization profiles can affect:
- **VRAM footprint**: reduced weight memory; potential increased workspace for some kernels.
- **KV cache format**: v1 does not change KV precision as part of QP unless explicitly encoded and supported; otherwise KV cache remains baseline format.

Runtime guardrails:
- If applying a profile would force an unsupported KV cache format transition mid-stream, fallback.
- If the request would exceed a memory safety threshold (including KV cache reservation), fallback rather than OOM.

### Continuous batching
When continuous batching combines multiple requests:
- The runtime groups requests by compatible `ExecutionPlan` characteristics.
- Requests with different `quant_profile_id` are not co-batched unless the kernel path and layout are identical (v1: treat different profile IDs as different batch groups).
- If batching pressure would violate latency SLOs, the scheduler may reduce batch size but must not change quant mode.

## Failure modes and safe fallbacks
### Fallback principles
- Fallback is per-request and must be fast.
- No retry loops between quantized and baseline execution.
- Fallback never attempts to “pick another profile” in v1.

### Common failure modes
- Registry metadata fetch latency spike (mitigated by caching, coalescing, and rate limiting).
- Signature verification failure (treated as hard failure; fallback + alert).
- Kernel mismatch due to driver/toolkit mismatch on a subset of hosts.
- Unexpected request regime (very long context) violating profile constraints.

### Error handling
- If failure occurs before execution starts: fallback to baseline.
- If failure occurs during execution plan compilation: fallback to baseline.
- If failure occurs after partial execution has started: v1 does not attempt mid-flight switching. The request fails with a structured error if it is unsafe to continue; this is counted separately from fallback metrics and should page only if it breaches error budget.

## Observability
### Request annotations
The runtime attaches the following fields to logs and traces:
- `quant_profile_id`: resolved profile id (string) when applied; empty when baseline.
- `quant_profile_ref`: the ref requested (versioned) when present.
- `quant_outcome`: `applied | fallback | disabled`.
- `quant_fallback_reason`: enum reason code (see Decision table).
- `quant_profile_digest`: artifact digest when applied (for forensic pinning).

### Metrics (recommended names)
Counters:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.requests_total{outcome, reason, model_id, route_id, region}`
- `runtime.quant_profiles.profile_load_failures_total{error_code, model_id, region}`

Gauges:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.cache_entries{region}`
- `runtime.quant_profiles.kill_switch_enabled{region}`

Histograms:
- `runtime.quant_profiles.profile_fetch_latency_ms{stage}` (metadata vs artifact vs verify)
- `runtime.latency.decode_ms{quant_enabled}` (to compare tail)
- `runtime.cost.tokens_per_second{quant_enabled}` (where available)

### Dashboards and alerts
Runtime-owned panels should include:
- Applied rate vs fallback rate by route/model.
- Fallback reason breakdown (top N) to quickly identify incompatibility vs registry issues.
- Profile load p95/p99 latency.
- Correlation between quant enabled and p95 decode latency.

Alerts:
- High `fallback_rate` for a specific route/model (suggests incompatibility or regression).
- Spike in `profile_load_failures_total` (suggests registry/signing/caching issue).
- Any signature verification failures > 0 in a 10m window (security + correctness).

## Performance considerations
- Cache lookup must be O(1) and avoid allocations in the hot path.
- Fetch path is isolated and rate limited; do not allow high-QPS routes to overwhelm registry.
- Profile compilation should be cheap; heavy preprocessing belongs in build-time artifact generation.

## Security and integrity
- All profile artifacts must be signed by the model registry signing key.
- Runtime verifies signature and digest before applying.
- Verification failures are treated as suspicious; emit a security audit event (internal) and fallback.

## Rollout considerations
- Feature must be safe when disabled.
- Kill-switch must immediately force baseline without requiring profile cache invalidation.
- During canary, monitor:
  - applied rate,
  - fallback reason distribution,
  - tail latency deltas,
  - error rate deltas.

## Testing strategy (runtime)
- Unit tests:
  - determinism of decision function given fixed inputs,
  - compatibility checks for supported/unsupported GPU SKUs,
  - caching behavior (single fetch per ref under concurrency).
- Integration tests:
  - end-to-end: policy-resolved profile ref -> runtime apply -> log annotations.
  - kill-switch overrides any route enablement.
- Perf canary scenarios:
  - baseline vs quant enabled for representative prompt+decode lengths.
  - regression thresholds on decode p95 and throughput.

## Open questions / future work
- Allow co-batching across different profile IDs when kernel/layout are identical (may improve throughput; requires careful accounting).
- Extend regime hints from router (sequence length distribution) to reduce fallback surprises.
- Support optional KV cache precision changes as a separate, explicitly gated profile capability.

source.title: Runtime LTS branch strategy and backport policy
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.body:
## Summary
This document defines Redwood Inference’s Long Term Support (LTS) strategy for the serving runtime and the policy for backporting fixes onto LTS branches.

The LTS channel exists to support Dedicated and Private customers who need a stable kernel + scheduling baseline for 6–12+ months with:
- Predictable patch cadence
- Clear eligibility criteria for backports (security/Sev1/Sev2)
- Strong correctness + performance gates
- Reproducible, auditable release artifacts

This policy is intentionally conservative: LTS prioritizes stability and operational predictability over feature parity with main.

---

## Goals
- Provide an LTS branch line (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`) with a defined support window.
- Establish a predictable patch cadence (monthly) plus an out-of-band (OOB) process for urgent security fixes.
- Define what is eligible for backport, how risk is assessed, required testing/perf gates, and approvals.
- Ensure traceability from release artifacts → git commit(s) → release notes → test/perf evidence.

## Non-goals
- Maintaining a long-lived fork or divergent architecture.
- Backporting new features, experimental performance optimizations, or large refactors.
- Guaranteeing that LTS includes every improvement that lands on main.

---

## Target customers and scope
### Who LTS is for
- **Dedicated** customers requiring predictable throughput/latency and planned change control.
- **Private** (VPC/on-prem) customers with regulated environments, slower upgrade cycles, and audit requirements.

### Where LTS is consumed
- Hosted: LTS may be available as an opt-in runtime track for qualifying customers, but Hosted generally rolls forward more frequently. (See Product launch brief for customer-facing positioning.)
- Dedicated: LTS is the recommended baseline for enterprise tiers when explicitly requested or contractually required.
- Private: LTS is the default recommended baseline when customers require version pinning + artifact verification.

---

## Definitions
- **Mainline**: the default runtime development branch (typically `main`) and its regular release line.
- **LTS branch**: a release branch cut from a mainline minor release and supported for a longer window (example: `runtime-1.18-lts`).
- **Patch release**: incremental updates on an LTS branch (example tag: `1.18.1-lts`).
- **Backport**: applying a fix that landed on mainline onto the LTS branch via cherry-pick (or minimal equivalent change).
- **Sev1/Sev2**: severity definitions aligned with SRE + Support incident classification.

---

## Support window and cadence
### Support window
- Each LTS minor line is supported for **12 months from GA** (General Availability of the initial LTS tag), with an optional extension up to **15 months** for contractually obligated customers (requires VP Eng Runtime + SRE Director sign-off).
- Redwood targets **one active LTS line** at a time. A second overlapping LTS line may be maintained during transitions for up to **90 days**.

### Patch cadence
- **Regular patches**: monthly (target week: 2nd Tuesday), time-boxed release window.
- **Out-of-band (OOB) patches**: as needed for urgent security or critical customer-impacting issues (see Security OOB flow).

### Patch classification (customer-facing)
- **Optional**: recommended upgrade, but not required to meet SLO.
- **Recommended**: meaningful risk reduction; upgrade is strongly encouraged.
- **Mandatory**: required to remain within SLO/security posture; used for severe regressions or active exploitation. Criteria and communication are defined in the LTS SLO & escalation doc.

---

## Branching model
### Naming
- LTS branch naming: `runtime-<major>.<minor>-lts` (example: `runtime-1.18-lts`).
- Branches are cut from a specific mainline tag (example: `v1.18.0`).

### What changes are allowed on an LTS branch
Allowed:
- Security fixes (including dependency patching when required).
- Correctness fixes for Sev1/Sev2 issues.
- Stability fixes for kernels/scheduler when tied to correctness or reliability.
- Small performance fixes *only* when they address a regression introduced after the branch point or are required for SLO compliance (see perf rubric below).

Not allowed:
- Feature additions (new APIs, new scheduling modes, new experimental heuristics).
- Large refactors, broad dependency upgrades unrelated to security, sweeping formatting/cleanup.

### Relationship to mainline
- All fixes should land on mainline first whenever feasible.
- LTS receives fixes by backport PRs. Direct commits to LTS branches are prohibited except for build/release infrastructure emergencies approved by Release Eng lead.

---

## Versioning and tagging
### Runtime version scheme
- LTS tags use the format: `<major>.<minor>.<patch>-lts` (example: `1.18.1-lts`).
- Container images and artifacts must include the exact LTS tag and immutable digest references.

### Patch numbering rules
- Patch increments monotonically on the LTS branch.
- OOB patches still increment the patch number (no “hotfix suffixes”).

### Compatibility expectations
- The LTS minor line is a compatibility boundary. Within an LTS minor line, patch releases must maintain:
  - Wire/API compatibility for supported client surfaces
  - Stable config semantics (scheduler + kernel flags)
  - Backward compatibility for supported model serving configs unless explicitly called out in upgrade notes

---

## Backport eligibility policy
Backports are accepted when they meet **eligibility** *and* pass required **risk + gate** checks.

### Eligibility categories
#### 1) Security
Eligible if any of the following are true:
- Critical/High severity vulnerability with credible exploitation path in customer environments.
- Medium severity with high likelihood of exploitation or regulatory requirement.
- Supply chain issue requiring patching/rebuild for provenance integrity.

Security backports must include:
- CVE identifier (or Redwood Security advisory ID if CVE pending)
- Affected components and impact
- Verification steps and detection/mitigation guidance

#### 2) Sev1 (Correctness/Availability)
Eligible if the issue causes any of:
- Runtime crash, data corruption, or correctness failure with no safe workaround.
- Sustained outage or severe degradation for LTS customers.
- Scheduler deadlock/starvation leading to request timeouts beyond SLO.

#### 3) Sev2 (Stability/Degradation)
Eligible if the issue causes:
- Significant latency regression (p95) or throughput drop beyond LTS perf thresholds on canonical workloads.
- Memory leak / resource exhaustion that escalates to Sev1 without intervention.
- Correctness edge cases with viable workaround but high operational burden.

Sev2 backports are prioritized when:
- Multiple customers are affected, or
- Dedicated/Private customers cannot reasonably roll forward to a newer minor line, and
- A minimal, low-risk fix exists.

### Not eligible (default)
- New features or behavior changes “for convenience.”
- Performance improvements that are not regressions (unless required for SLO compliance and low risk).
- Large dependency bumps without a security driver.
- “Cleanup” (refactors, renames, formatting-only diffs).

---

## Risk rubric (must be completed for every backport PR)
Backport PRs must include a completed rubric in the PR template.

### Risk scoring
**Risk score = Change surface (1–3) + Test confidence (1–3) + Perf sensitivity (1–3)**
- Lower is better. Any score **≥7** requires explicit VP Eng Runtime or delegate approval.

| Dimension | 1 (Low) | 2 (Medium) | 3 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Change surface | Localized fix, small diff, no API/config change | Touches multiple modules or non-trivial control flow | Broad refactor, concurrency-sensitive, or behavior change |
| Test confidence | Repro + new targeted test + existing suite coverage | Repro or test exists but partial coverage | No repro, relies on reasoning only |
| Perf sensitivity | No hot path changes | Hot path touched with expected neutrality | Hot path change with uncertain impact |

### “Minimal diff” principle
- Prefer a cherry-pick of the exact mainline fix.
- If the cherry-pick does not apply cleanly, the backport must remain semantically equivalent and limited to what is necessary for correctness/security.
- If the backport requires additional refactoring, it should be rejected or deferred unless Sev1/security requires it.

---

## Required gates (per backport and per patch release)
Gates apply both to individual backport PRs and to the final patch cut.

### Required for all backport PRs
- ✅ Unit + integration test suite green on the LTS branch CI.
- ✅ Targeted regression test added when feasible (especially scheduler and KV cache issues).
- ✅ Static analysis/lint gates as configured for release branches.
- ✅ Release notes entry stub (label-driven) with customer-impact summary.

### Scheduler-specific gates (when scheduler code is touched)
- ✅ Release-branch scheduler regression suite (mixed prompt lengths, backpressure, overload/load-shedding scenarios).
- ✅ Evidence in PR: before/after behavior and expected failure mode.

### Kernel/performance-sensitive gates (when kernel/autotune/hot path is touched)
- ✅ Perf-canary gate run on canonical workloads + required GPU SKUs.
- ✅ Regression thresholds must meet LTS perf policy (see perf gates doc).
- ✅ If performance moves outside thresholds, a waiver requires SRE + Perf owner approval and must be documented in release notes.

### Artifact and provenance gates (per patch release)
- ✅ Reproducible build inputs captured (toolchain version, commit SHA, build params).
- ✅ Checksums + signatures published for all customer-consumable artifacts.
- ✅ SBOM generated (or documented stub if pending) and linked from release notes.

---

## Approval workflow
### Roles
- **Runtime owners**: kernel + scheduling leads (Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova) and runtime architect (Noah Patel) as needed.
- **Release Engineering**: LTS release lead (Connor O’Brien) owns branch health, release cadence, and final tag/publish.
- **SRE**: on-call ownership and perf/SLO sign-off (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes).
- **Security**: policy sponsor and vuln response coordination (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Hanae Suzuki).

### Standard backport workflow (non-security)
1) **Identify candidate**
   - Source: incident, Support ticket, perf regression report, or mainline fix.
   - Open/attach to tracking issue with severity + customer impact.
2) **Confirm eligibility**
   - Must meet Sev1/Sev2 criteria and not violate “not eligible” list.
3) **Prepare backport PR**
   - Use labels: `backport-candidate`, `lts`, plus one of `security`, `sev1`, `sev2`.
   - Include rubric, linked mainline PR, test plan, and rollout/rollback notes.
4) **Runtime review**
   - Required: code owner(s) for touched subsystems.
5) **Release Eng review**
   - Required: verifies minimal diff, labels, and release note metadata.
6) **SRE review (conditional)**
   - Required when: perf-sensitive changes, customer-impacting reliability fixes, or waiver requests.
7) **Merge + validate**
   - Merge only after gates pass.
8) **Patch cut inclusion**
   - Release Eng assembles the patch set and runs patch-level gates.

### Security backport workflow (expedited)
1) Security triage assigns severity and target LTS line(s).
2) Release Eng schedules OOB patch window and locks the patch set.
3) Mandatory reviews:
   - Security reviewer (Dr. Aisha Rahman or delegate)
   - Runtime subsystem owner
   - Release Eng lead
   - SRE approver for rollout risk
4) Gates:
   - Minimum required tests must pass; perf gates may be narrowed only with explicit waiver.
5) Communication:
   - Internal: Support/CS notified with advisory language and customer impact.
   - External: customer advisory per Security comms plan (timing may be constrained).

---

## Documentation requirements
Every LTS patch must ship with:
- Slower-change LTS release notes (categorized, with known issues and mitigations)
- Compatibility matrix updates (runtime ↔ driver/CUDA ↔ supported deployment artifacts)
- Upgrade and rollback notes (especially for Private)
- Evidence links (CI run IDs, perf-canary summary, artifact checksums/signatures)

---

## Exceptions and waivers
Waivers are the exception; the default is to hold the backport or require more evidence.

### When waivers are allowed
- Sev1 or Security fixes where delaying the patch increases customer risk, and the remaining uncertainty is bounded.

### Required documentation for a waiver
- What gate is waived and why
- Risk assessment and blast radius
- Mitigation/rollback plan
- Owner for post-release monitoring

### Approvers for waivers
- Release Eng lead (Connor O’Brien) and SRE on-call manager (Rafael Mendes)
- Plus Runtime VP delegate when risk score ≥ 7

---

## Ownership and escalation
- LTS branch health and patch execution: **Release Engineering** (Connor O’Brien).
- Runtime correctness/stability: **Runtime team** (Mateo Alvarez; subsystem owners).
- Production monitoring and SLO compliance during rollouts: **SRE** (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes).
- Security response: **Security & Compliance** (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Hanae Suzuki).

Escalations should follow the Runtime LTS SLO & escalation expectations page.

---

## Appendix A: Required PR metadata (template)
Backport PRs to LTS must include:
- Mainline PR link and commit SHA(s)
- Category: Security / Sev1 / Sev2
- Risk rubric (change surface, test confidence, perf sensitivity) and total score
- Test plan + evidence links
- Perf impact statement (and perf-canary link when applicable)
- Release notes summary (2–5 sentences, customer-facing)
- Rollback considerations

---

## Appendix B: Common rejection reasons (to reduce churn)
- “Fix” is primarily a refactor or includes opportunistic changes beyond the bug.
- No repro, no targeted test, and change touches concurrency/hot path.
- Perf-sensitive change without perf-canary evidence.
- Backport requires large dependency upgrades without security justification.
- Change alters scheduling/batching heuristics for non-regression optimization.

---

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Whether we maintain a second active LTS line during major hardware transitions.
- Final customer-facing language for “mandatory” patches and timelines by support tier.

(For current status and decisions, see ADR-027 and the change-management page.)

title: Runtime LTS branch strategy and backport policy
preview:
## Summary
This document defines Redwood Inference’s Long Term Support (LTS) strategy for the serving runtime and the policy for backporting fixes onto LTS branches.

The LTS channel exists to support Dedicated and Private customers who need a stable kernel + scheduling baseline for 6–12+ months with:
- Predictable patch cadence
- Clear eligibility criteria for backports (security/Sev1/Sev2)
- Strong correctness + performance gates
- Reproducible, auditable release artifacts

This policy is intentionally conservative: LTS prioritizes stability and operational predictability over feature parity with main.

---

## Goals
- Provide an LTS branch line (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`) with a defined support window.
- Establish a predictable patch cadence (monthly) plus an out-of-band (OOB) process for urgent security fixes.
- Define what is eligible for backport, how risk is assessed, required testing/perf gates, and approvals.
- Ensure traceability from release artifacts → git commit(s) → release notes → test/perf evidence.

## Non-goals
- Maintaining a long-lived fork or divergent architecture.
- Backporting new features, experimental performance optimizations, or large refactors.
- Guaranteeing that LTS includes every improvement that lands on main.

---

## Target customers and scope
### Who LTS is for
- **Dedicated** customers requiring predictable throughput/latency and planned change control.
- **Private** (VPC/on-prem) customers with regulated environments, slower upgrade cycles, and audit requirements.

### Where LTS is consumed
- Hosted: LTS may be available as an opt-in runtime track for qualifying customers, but Hosted generally rolls forward more frequently. (See Product launch brief for customer-facing positioning.)
- Dedicated: LTS is the recommended baseline for enterprise tiers when explicitly requested or contractually required.
- Private: LTS is the default recommended baseline when customers require version pinning + artifact verification.

---

## Definitions
- **Mainline**: the default runtime development branch (typically `main`) and its regular release line.
- **LTS branch**: a release branch cut from a mainline minor release and supported for a longer window (example: `runtime-1.18-lts`).
- **Patch release**: incremental updates on an LTS branch (example tag: `1.18.1-lts`).
- **Backport**: applying a fix that landed on mainline onto the LTS branch via cherry-pick (or minimal equivalent change).
- **Sev1/Sev2**: severity definitions aligned with SRE + Support incident classification.

---

## Support window and cadence
### Support window
- Each LTS minor line is supported for **12 months from GA** (General Availability of the initial LTS tag), with an optional extension up to **15 months** for contractually obligated customers (requires VP Eng Runtime + SRE Director sign-off).
- Redwood targets **one active LTS line** at a time. A second overlapping LTS line may be maintained during transitions for up to **90 days**.

### Patch cadence
- **Regular patches**: monthly (target week: 2nd Tuesday), time-boxed release window.
- **Out-of-band (OOB) patches**: as needed for urgent security or critical customer-impacting issues (see Security OOB flow).

### Patch classification (customer-facing)
- **Optional**: recommended upgrade, but not required to meet SLO.
- **Recommended**: meaningful risk reduction; upgrade is strongly encouraged.
- **Mandatory**: required to remain within SLO/security posture; used for severe regressions or active exploitation. Criteria and communication are defined in the LTS SLO & escalation doc.

---

## Branching model
### Naming
- LTS branch naming: `runtime-<major>.<minor>-lts` (example: `runtime-1.18-lts`).
- Branches are cut from a specific mainline tag (example: `v1.18.0`).

### What changes are allowed on an LTS branch
Allowed:
- Security fixes (including dependency patching when required).
- Correctness fixes for Sev1/Sev2 issues.
- Stability fixes for kernels/scheduler when tied to correctness or reliability.
- Small performance fixes *only* when they address a regression introduced after the branch point or are required for SLO compliance (see perf rubric below).

Not allowed:
- Feature additions (new APIs, new scheduling modes, new experimental heuristics).
- Large refactors, broad dependency upgrades unrelated to security, sweeping formatting/cleanup.

### Relationship to mainline
- All fixes should land on mainline first whenever feasible.
- LTS receives fixes by backport PRs. Direct commits to LTS branches are prohibited except for build/release infrastructure emergencies approved by Release Eng lead.

---

## Versioning and tagging
### Runtime version scheme
- LTS tags use the format: `<major>.<minor>.<patch>-lts` (example: `1.18.1-lts`).
- Container images and artifacts must include the exact LTS tag and immutable digest references.

### Patch numbering rules
- Patch increments monotonically on the LTS branch.
- OOB patches still increment the patch number (no “hotfix suffixes”).

### Compatibility expectations
- The LTS minor line is a compatibility boundary. Within an LTS minor line, patch releases must maintain:
  - Wire/API compatibility for supported client surfaces
  - Stable config semantics (scheduler + kernel flags)
  - Backward compatibility for supported model serving configs unless explicitly called out in upgrade notes

---

## Backport eligibility policy
Backports are accepted when they meet **eligibility** *and* pass required **risk + gate** checks.

### Eligibility categories
#### 1) Security
Eligible if any of the following are true:
- Critical/High severity vulnerability with credible exploitation path in customer environments.
- Medium severity with high likelihood of exploitation or regulatory requirement.
- Supply chain issue requiring patching/rebuild for provenance integrity.

Security backports must include:
- CVE identifier (or Redwood Security advisory ID if CVE pending)
- Affected components and impact
- Verification steps and detection/mitigation guidance

#### 2) Sev1 (Correctness/Availability)
Eligible if the issue causes any of:
- Runtime crash, data corruption, or correctness failure with no safe workaround.
- Sustained outage or severe degradation for LTS customers.
- Scheduler deadlock/starvation leading to request timeouts beyond SLO.

#### 3) Sev2 (Stability/Degradation)
Eligible if the issue causes:
- Significant latency regression (p95) or throughput drop beyond LTS perf thresholds on canonical workloads.
- Memory leak / resource exhaustion that escalates to Sev1 without intervention.
- Correctness edge cases with viable workaround but high operational burden.

Sev2 backports are prioritized when:
- Multiple customers are affected, or
- Dedicated/Private customers cannot reasonably roll forward to a newer minor line, and
- A minimal, low-risk fix exists.

### Not eligible (default)
- New features or behavior changes “for convenience.”
- Performance improvements that are not regressions (unless required for SLO compliance and low risk).
- Large dependency bumps without a security driver.
- “Cleanup” (refactors, renames, formatting-only diffs).

---

## Risk rubric (must be completed for every backport PR)
Backport PRs must include a completed rubric in the PR template.

### Risk scoring
**Risk score = Change surface (1–3) + Test confidence (1–3) + Perf sensitivity (1–3)**
- Lower is better. Any score **≥7** requires explicit VP Eng Runtime or delegate approval.

| Dimension | 1 (Low) | 2 (Medium) | 3 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Change surface | Localized fix, small diff, no API/config change | Touches multiple modules or non-trivial control flow | Broad refactor, concurrency-sensitive, or behavior change |
| Test confidence | Repro + new targeted test + existing suite coverage | Repro or test exists but partial coverage | No repro, relies on reasoning only |
| Perf sensitivity | No hot path changes | Hot path touched with expected neutrality | Hot path change with uncertain impact |

### “Minimal diff” principle
- Prefer a cherry-pick of the exact mainline fix.
- If the cherry-pick does not apply cleanly, the backport must remain semantically equivalent and limited to what is necessary for correctness/security.
- If the backport requires additional refactoring, it should be rejected or deferred unless Sev1/security requires it.

---

## Required gates (per backport and per patch release)
Gates apply both to individual backport PRs and to the final patch cut.

### Required for all backport PRs
- ✅ Unit + integration test suite green on the LTS branch CI.
- ✅ Targeted regression test added when feasible (especially scheduler and KV cache issues).
- ✅ Static analysis/lint gates as configured for release branches.
- ✅ Release notes entry stub (label-driven) with customer-impact summary.

### Scheduler-specific gates (when scheduler code is touched)
- ✅ Release-branch scheduler regression suite (mixed prompt lengths, backpressure, overload/load-shedding scenarios).
- ✅ Evidence in PR: before/after behavior and expected failure mode.

### Kernel/performance-sensitive gates (when kernel/autotune/hot path is touched)
- ✅ Perf-canary gate run on canonical workloads + required GPU SKUs.
- ✅ Regression thresholds must meet LTS perf policy (see perf gates doc).
- ✅ If performance moves outside thresholds, a waiver requires SRE + Perf owner approval and must be documented in release notes.

### Artifact and provenance gates (per patch release)
- ✅ Reproducible build inputs captured (toolchain version, commit SHA, build params).
- ✅ Checksums + signatures published for all customer-consumable artifacts.
- ✅ SBOM generated (or documented stub if pending) and linked from release notes.

---

## Approval workflow
### Roles
- **Runtime owners**: kernel + scheduling leads (Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova) and runtime architect (Noah Patel) as needed.
- **Release Engineering**: LTS release lead (Connor O’Brien) owns branch health, release cadence, and final tag/publish.
- **SRE**: on-call ownership and perf/SLO sign-off (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes).
- **Security**: policy sponsor and vuln response coordination (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Hanae Suzuki).

### Standard backport workflow (non-security)
1) **Identify candidate**
   - Source: incident, Support ticket, perf regression report, or mainline fix.
   - Open/attach to tracking issue with severity + customer impact.
2) **Confirm eligibility**
   - Must meet Sev1/Sev2 criteria and not violate “not eligible” list.
3) **Prepare backport PR**
   - Use labels: `backport-candidate`, `lts`, plus one of `security`, `sev1`, `sev2`.
   - Include rubric, linked mainline PR, test plan, and rollout/rollback notes.
4) **Runtime review**
   - Required: code owner(s) for touched subsystems.
5) **Release Eng review**
   - Required: verifies minimal diff, labels, and release note metadata.
6) **SRE review (conditional)**
   - Required when: perf-sensitive changes, customer-impacting reliability fixes, or waiver requests.
7) **Merge + validate**
   - Merge only after gates pass.
8) **Patch cut inclusion**
   - Release Eng assembles the patch set and runs patch-level gates.

### Security backport workflow (expedited)
1) Security triage assigns severity and target LTS line(s).
2) Release Eng schedules OOB patch window and locks the patch set.
3) Mandatory reviews:
   - Security reviewer (Dr. Aisha Rahman or delegate)
   - Runtime subsystem owner
   - Release Eng lead
   - SRE approver for rollout risk
4) Gates:
   - Minimum required tests must pass; perf gates may be narrowed only with explicit waiver.
5) Communication:
   - Internal: Support/CS notified with advisory language and customer impact.
   - External: customer advisory per Security comms plan (timing may be constrained).

---

## Documentation requirements
Every LTS patch must ship with:
- Slower-change LTS release notes (categorized, with known issues and mitigations)
- Compatibility matrix updates (runtime ↔ driver/CUDA ↔ supported deployment artifacts)
- Upgrade and rollback notes (especially for Private)
- Evidence links (CI run IDs, perf-canary summary, artifact checksums/signatures)

---

## Exceptions and waivers
Waivers are the exception; the default is to hold the backport or require more evidence.

### When waivers are allowed
- Sev1 or Security fixes where delaying the patch increases customer risk, and the remaining uncertainty is bounded.

### Required documentation for a waiver
- What gate is waived and why
- Risk assessment and blast radius
- Mitigation/rollback plan
- Owner for post-release monitoring

### Approvers for waivers
- Release Eng lead (Connor O’Brien) and SRE on-call manager (Rafael Mendes)
- Plus Runtime VP delegate when risk score ≥ 7

---

## Ownership and escalation
- LTS branch health and patch execution: **Release Engineering** (Connor O’Brien).
- Runtime correctness/stability: **Runtime team** (Mateo Alvarez; subsystem owners).
- Production monitoring and SLO compliance during rollouts: **SRE** (Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes).
- Security response: **Security & Compliance** (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Hanae Suzuki).

Escalations should follow the Runtime LTS SLO & escalation expectations page.

---

## Appendix A: Required PR metadata (template)
Backport PRs to LTS must include:
- Mainline PR link and commit SHA(s)
- Category: Security / Sev1 / Sev2
- Risk rubric (change surface, test confidence, perf sensitivity) and total score
- Test plan + evidence links
- Perf impact statement (and perf-canary link when applicable)
- Release notes summary (2–5 sentences, customer-facing)
- Rollback considerations

---

## Appendix B: Common rejection reasons (to reduce churn)
- “Fix” is primarily a refactor or includes opportunistic changes beyond the bug.
- No repro, no targeted test, and change touches concurrency/hot path.
- Perf-sensitive change without perf-canary evidence.
- Backport requires large dependency upgrades without security justification.
- Change alters scheduling/batching heuristics for non-regression optimization.

---

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Whether we maintain a second active LTS line during major hardware transitions.
- Final customer-facing language for “mandatory” patches and timelines by support tier.

(For current status and decisions, see ADR-027 and the change-management page.)
