source.title: TP stall triage playbook (tensor-parallel hangs/timeouts)
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.body:
## Purpose
This playbook covers step-by-step triage for **tensor-parallel (TP) stalls** (a.k.a. hangs/timeouts) in the serving runtime. It is written for on-call SRE + runtime engineers to quickly answer:
- Is the stall **compute**, **NCCL/collectives**, or **scheduler/starvation**?
- Which replicas/ranks are stuck, and is it isolated or systemic?
- What data should we capture (metrics/logs/traces/NCCL debug) without making the incident worse?

This is **triage + profiling**. For mitigation actions (drain/replace, concurrency caps, feature flags), use the runbook: *Runtime TP hang mitigation*.

---

## Definitions (what we mean by “TP stall”)
A TP stall is a condition where a multi-GPU replica stops making forward progress for a request or decode loop. Typical external symptoms:
- Streaming responses stop mid-stream (client sees partial tokens then timeout).
- Request p99 and timeout rate spike for a subset of replicas (often a single model + HW type).
- GPU utilization becomes skewed by rank (one GPU at high util while others idle) or all GPUs look “busy” but throughput drops.

Internal signatures commonly fall into:
1) **Collective mismatch / ordering bug** (ranks enter different collectives or different order)
2) **Stream/event sync hazard** (collective enqueued but waits on an event never recorded / wrong stream)
3) **Scheduler starvation** (collectives or rank coordination starved by prefill-heavy batches or unfair queueing)
4) **Memory/KV cache pressure divergence** (rank divergence due to compaction/fragmentation edge cases)

---

## Before you start (scope + safety)
### Don’t worsen the blast radius
- Avoid enabling verbose NCCL debug on broad fleets. Prefer **single replica**, **short window**, **Dedicated-first**, and **off-peak** when possible.
- NCCL debug can create large logs and measurable overhead; it can also expose topology and environment details. Treat as internal-only.
- If customer traffic is impacted (timeouts), prioritize mitigation (drain/replace, cap concurrency) before deep profiling.

### What to capture (minimum useful packet)
Collect these for every suspected TP stall:
- Cluster/region, model name/version, runtime version, GPU type, TP degree (e.g., TP=4/8)
- Affected replica IDs + node IDs
- Time window (start, peak, end), and whether retries/fallback triggered
- Links to:
  - TP Stall & Timeout dashboard panels
  - Request trace IDs (at least 2–3 from timeouts)
  - Logs for the affected replica(s)

---

## Fast triage (5–10 minutes)
### Step 1: Confirm it’s TP-related vs generic overload
Use the dashboard: **TP stall and timeout dashboard**.

Look for:
- **Request timeouts** concentrated on TP replicas vs all replicas
- **TP rank heartbeat gaps** (per-rank progress stops)
- **Replica drain events** spiking (watchdog/abort path firing)
- **Scheduler queue depth** rising without corresponding throughput

If timeouts are uniform across all replicas, treat as broader capacity/overload and use standard overload playbooks.

### Step 2: Identify the blast radius
Answer:
- Is it **one replica** (likely wedged) or **many replicas** (likely systemic regression)?
- Is it tied to **one model**, **one GPU SKU**, **one cluster**, or **one recent rollout**?

Checklist:
- Compare Dedicated vs Hosted clusters
- Compare A100/H100/L40S pools
- Check recent runtime canary changes and feature flags

### Step 3: Determine stall class with 3 signals
Use these three signals together to classify quickly:

1) **Rank heartbeat age** (progress signal)
- Symptom: heartbeat age increasing on some/all ranks.

2) **In-flight collective counters** (comms signal)
- Symptom: collectives “in-flight” stuck (not decrementing) or ranks report different collective sequence.

3) **Scheduler queue depth / fairness** (scheduler signal)
- Symptom: queue depth grows; prefill-heavy batches dominate; decode iterations stop being scheduled.

If (1) is bad and (2) shows stuck collectives → suspect NCCL/collectives ordering or stream sync.
If (1) is bad but (2) is normal and (3) is bad → suspect scheduler starvation.
If (1) is bad and memory/KV errors appear in logs → suspect KV cache divergence/compaction.

---

## Metrics to use (names + interpretation)
Metric names may differ slightly by environment; prefer the dashboard panels as source of truth.

### Core TP health
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds{model,replica_id,rank}`
  - **Actionable:** if > 10–30s sustained for any rank, suspect stall.
- `runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{model,replica_id,reason}`
  - **Reason codes** (common): `COMMS_COLLECTIVE_STUCK`, `SCHED_STARVATION`, `KV_ALLOC_WAIT`, `CANCEL_PATH_WAIT`, `UNKNOWN`.
- `runtime_tp_inflight_collectives{model,replica_id,rank,collective}`
  - Rising and never decreasing → stuck collective.

### Scheduler/backpressure
- `runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{queue,model}`
- `runtime_scheduler_prefill_decode_ratio{model}`
- `runtime_scheduler_starvation_events_total{model}`

### Timeouts + mitigations
- `api_request_timeouts_total{route,model,cluster}`
- `runtime_replica_drain_events_total{reason,model}`
- `runtime_overload_rejections_total{reason,model}`

---

## Logs (what to grep for)
### Structured runtime log fields to pull
For the suspected replica, gather logs around the stall window and extract:
- `replica_id`, `rank`, `tp_degree`, `model`, `request_id`, `trace_id`
- `stall_reason`, `collective_seq`, `collective_type`, `stream_id`, `cuda_graph_enabled`
- `kv_cache_bytes_free`, `kv_compaction_in_progress`, `oom/alloc_wait`

### Common log patterns
- Collective mismatch / ordering:
  - “collective sequence mismatch”
  - “rank entered ALLREDUCE but peer entered ALLGATHER”
  - “expected collective_id=X observed Y”
- Stream/event sync:
  - “waiting on cuda event” with long durations
  - “stream synchronize timed out”
- Scheduler starvation:
  - “prefill batch admitted while decode pending”
  - “fairness guardrail triggered” (if enabled)
- Cancellation path deadlock:
  - “client disconnected” followed by stuck collective / no further progress

---

## Traces (how to correlate stalls to collectives)
### What to look for in traces
For 2–3 timed-out requests:
- Identify the server-side spans for decode iterations.
- Check span attributes:
  - `tp.rank`, `tp.degree`
  - `tp.collective.inflight` counters
  - `tp.collective.seq` (if present)
  - `stall_reason` tag (if emitted)

### Minimal trace workflow
1) Pull a timeout request ID from API logs.
2) Find the associated `trace_id`.
3) Open trace and confirm where time is spent:
   - compute kernel spans vs NCCL spans vs scheduler wait spans.

If trace shows long gaps without spans, rely on heartbeat + logs; the process may be stuck below instrumentation.

---

## Node/replica inspection (commands)
The exact tooling differs by cluster. The commands below are representative for Redwood’s Kubernetes-based runtime clusters.

### Identify the pod and node
- Find pods for a model:
  - `kubectl -n runtime get pods -l app=redwood-runtime,model=<MODEL> -o wide`
- Filter to a specific replica:
  - `kubectl -n runtime get pod | grep <replica_id>`

### Quick GPU health (on the node)
If you have node access (or via privileged debug pod):
- `nvidia-smi -L`
- `nvidia-smi pmon -c 1`
- `nvidia-smi dmon -s pucvmt -c 5`

What you’re looking for:
- Rank imbalance (one GPU pegged while others idle)
- PCIe/NVLink issues (if visible)
- Memory near OOM or rapid allocation churn

### Collect pod logs around the stall
- `kubectl -n runtime logs <pod> --since=30m > /tmp/<pod>.log`
- If multi-container:
  - `kubectl -n runtime logs <pod> -c runtime --since=30m`

### Capture a lightweight runtime debug snapshot (preferred over heavy profilers)
If the runtime exposes a debug endpoint (varies by env):
- `kubectl -n runtime port-forward <pod> 6060:6060`
- Then (examples):
  - `curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/tp/status`
  - `curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/scheduler/queues`
  - `curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/kv/summary`

If endpoints are unavailable, rely on logs + metrics.

---

## Safely capturing NCCL debug traces
### When to enable NCCL debug
Enable NCCL debug only when:
- The issue reproduces and **appears comms/collective-related**.
- The blast radius is controlled (single replica or small canary).
- You have a clear rollback plan and a short time window (10–30 minutes).

### Recommended NCCL debug settings (balanced)
Use the least-verbose settings that still show collective sequencing.

Suggested env vars:
- `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL,INIT`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_FILE=/var/log/redwood/nccl/%h_%p_rank%r.log`

Optional (only if needed):
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL,INIT,GRAPH`
- `NCCL_DEBUG=WARN` (if INFO is too noisy)

Avoid unless specifically requested by kernel team:
- `NCCL_DEBUG=TRACE` (very noisy)
- Broad `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=ALL`

### Operational safety checklist
Before enabling:
1) Confirm **disk space** for logs on node/pod (NCCL logs can be large).
2) Confirm log retention and export path (avoid shipping huge logs to default pipelines).
3) Get explicit approval in the incident channel if customer impact is ongoing.

During capture:
- Keep capture window short.
- Prefer a canary replica with synthetic load or limited customer traffic.

After capture:
1) Turn off NCCL debug env vars.
2) Collect logs from the replica only.
3) Scrub and store logs in the incident artifact location (internal only).

### How to enable in practice
Preferred: use Redwood runtime config / feature flag mechanism for canary-only enablement.

If a rollout-based toggle exists (example):
- Enable flag: `runtime.nccl_debug_logging=canary` or `runtime.tp.nccl_debug=enabled` (exact names vary by release).
- Validate by searching logs for “NCCL version” and “NET/Topology” lines.

If you must patch a single deployment (last resort):
- Patch env vars on one replica deployment and restart only that replica.
- Coordinate with release/on-call owner to avoid triggering autoscaling thrash.

---

## Decision tree (compute vs comms vs scheduler)
### A) Suspect comms/collectives
Indicators:
- Heartbeats stop and `inflight_collectives` is stuck.
- Many timeouts cluster on a single replica; draining fixes temporarily.
- Logs show collective sequence mismatch or long waits on stream sync.

Next actions:
1) Capture NCCL debug logs (safe mode) on a canary replica.
2) Capture trace IDs for 2–3 timeouts.
3) Escalate to Kernel team with: model, TP degree, GPU type, runtime version, NCCL logs, and stall window.

### B) Suspect scheduler starvation
Indicators:
- Queue depth increases; prefill-heavy admission continues while decode progress halts.
- `stall_reason_total{reason="SCHED_STARVATION"}` increases.
- Switching workload mix (reduce long prefill / lower concurrency) improves.

Next actions:
1) Capture scheduler queue snapshot (`/debug/scheduler/queues`) if available.
2) Capture metrics: queue depth, prefill/decode ratio, starvation events.
3) Escalate to Scheduling team with workload distribution details (prompt length mix, streaming enabled, concurrency pattern).

### C) Suspect KV cache / memory pressure divergence
Indicators:
- Errors/warnings around compaction, allocation waits, or near-OOM.
- Stalls appear under long context + high concurrency.

Next actions:
1) Capture KV cache summary (`/debug/kv/summary`) if available.
2) Capture memory metrics + logs around compaction.
3) Escalate to KV/cache owner with stress suite parameters (context length, batch size, concurrency, cache settings).

---

## What to include in an escalation (copy/paste template)
**Subject:** TP stall suspected (comms/scheduler/KV) – <cluster> <model> TP=<N> GPU=<SKU> runtime=<ver>

- Cluster/region:
- Customer impact (Dedicated/Hosted, accounts):
- Model + version:
- Runtime version + recent changes/flags:
- GPU type + TP degree:
- Start time / peak / end:
- Symptoms:
  - Timeout rate:
  - p99 latency:
  - Streaming affected (yes/no):
- Suspected stall class: comms / scheduler / KV / unknown
- Evidence:
  - Dashboard links:
  - Metrics screenshots/exports:
  - Trace IDs:
  - Replica IDs / pod names:
  - Logs attached (runtime logs, NCCL logs if captured):
- Mitigations attempted (drain, caps, flags) + outcome:

---

## Common pitfalls
- Treating a wedged replica as overload: draining the replica may restore service but hides a systemic regression. Always capture artifacts first if feasible.
- Enabling NCCL TRACE broadly: can overload logging, increase latency, and create noisy data that’s hard to interpret.
- Failing to capture per-rank context: TP stalls are often rank-specific; always record `rank` and `replica_id`.

---

## Appendices
### A. Expected “healthy” baselines
These vary by model and workload, but generally:
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds` stays near 0–2s with brief spikes under GC/compaction.
- `runtime_tp_inflight_collectives` oscillates but returns to baseline; it should not monotonically increase.
- Queue depth should correlate with throughput; sustained queue growth with flat throughput is suspicious.

### B. When to declare a suspected regression
Strong indicators of a regression (vs isolated node failure):
- Same stall signature across multiple replicas after a rollout
- Correlated with a specific GPU SKU or NCCL/CUDA change
- Reproducible in perf-canary or benchmark-lab using the mixed prefill/decode workload

If regression is suspected, coordinate with release owner to pause rollout and set a clear go/no-go checkpoint.

- Streaming responses stop mid-stream (client sees partial tokens then timeout).
- Request p99 and timeout rate spike for a subset of replicas (often a single model + HW type).
- GPU utilization becomes skewed by rank (one GPU at high util while others idle) or all GPUs look “busy” but throughput drops.

Internal signatures commonly fall into:
1) **Collective mismatch / ordering bug** (ranks enter different collectives or different order)
2) **Stream/event sync hazard** (collective enqueued but waits on an event never recorded / wrong stream)
3) **Scheduler starvation** (collectives or rank coordination starved by prefill-heavy batches or unfair queueing)
4) **Memory/KV cache pressure divergence** (rank divergence due to compaction/fragmentation edge cases)

---

## Before you start (scope + safety)
### Don’t worsen the blast radius
- Avoid enabling verbose NCCL debug on broad fleets. Prefer **single replica**, **short window**, **Dedicated-first**, and **off-peak** when possible.
- NCCL debug can create large logs and measurable overhead; it can also expose topology and environment details. Treat as internal-only.
- If customer traffic is impacted (timeouts), prioritize mitigation (drain/replace, cap concurrency) before deep profiling.

### What to capture (minimum useful packet)
Collect these for every suspected TP stall:
- Cluster/region, model name/version, runtime version, GPU type, TP degree (e.g., TP=4/8)
- Affected replica IDs + node IDs
- Time window (start, peak, end), and whether retries/fallback triggered
- Links to:
  - TP Stall & Timeout dashboard panels
  - Request trace IDs (at least 2–3 from timeouts)
  - Logs for the affected replica(s)

---

## Fast triage (5–10 minutes)
### Step 1: Confirm it’s TP-related vs generic overload
Use the dashboard: **TP stall and timeout dashboard**.

Look for:
- **Request timeouts** concentrated on TP replicas vs all replicas
- **TP rank heartbeat gaps** (per-rank progress stops)
- **Replica drain events** spiking (watchdog/abort path firing)
- **Scheduler queue depth** rising without corresponding throughput

If timeouts are uniform across all replicas, treat as broader capacity/overload and use standard overload playbooks.

### Step 2: Identify the blast radius
Answer:
- Is it **one replica** (likely wedged) or **many replicas** (likely systemic regression)?
- Is it tied to **one model**, **one GPU SKU**, **one cluster**, or **one recent rollout**?

Checklist:
- Compare Dedicated vs Hosted clusters
- Compare A100/H100/L40S pools
- Check recent runtime canary changes and feature flags

### Step 3: Determine stall class with 3 signals
Use these three signals together to classify quickly:

1) **Rank heartbeat age** (progress signal)
- Symptom: heartbeat age increasing on some/all ranks.

2) **In-flight collective counters** (comms signal)
- Symptom: collectives “in-flight” stuck (not decrementing) or ranks report different collective sequence.

3) **Scheduler queue depth / fairness** (scheduler signal)
- Symptom: queue depth grows; prefill-heavy batches dominate; decode iterations stop being scheduled.

If (1) is bad and (2) shows stuck collectives → suspect NCCL/collectives ordering or stream sync.
If (1) is bad but (2) is normal and (3) is bad → suspect scheduler starvation.
If (1) is bad and memory/KV errors appear in logs → suspect KV cache divergence/compaction.

---

## Metrics to use (names + interpretation)
Metric names may differ slightly by environment; prefer the dashboard panels as source of truth.

### Core TP health
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds{model,replica_id,rank}`
  - **Actionable:** if > 10–30s sustained for any rank, suspect stall.
- `runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{model,replica_id,reason}`
  - **Reason codes** (common): `COMMS_COLLECTIVE_STUCK`, `SCHED_STARVATION`, `KV_ALLOC_WAIT`, `CANCEL_PATH_WAIT`, `UNKNOWN`.
- `runtime_tp_inflight_collectives{model,replica_id,rank,collective}`
  - Rising and never decreasing → stuck collective.

### Scheduler/backpressure
- `runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{queue,model}`
- `runtime_scheduler_prefill_decode_ratio{model}`
- `runtime_scheduler_starvation_events_total{model}`

### Timeouts + mitigations
- `api_request_timeouts_total{route,model,cluster}`
- `runtime_replica_drain_events_total{reason,model}`
- `runtime_overload_rejections_total{reason,model}`

---

## Logs (what to grep for)
### Structured runtime log fields to pull
For the suspected replica, gather logs around the stall window and extract:
- `replica_id`, `rank`, `tp_degree`, `model`, `request_id`, `trace_id`
- `stall_reason`, `collective_seq`, `collective_type`, `stream_id`, `cuda_graph_enabled`
- `kv_cache_bytes_free`, `kv_compaction_in_progress`, `oom/alloc_wait`

### Common log patterns
- Collective mismatch / ordering:
  - “collective sequence mismatch”
  - “rank entered ALLREDUCE but peer entered ALLGATHER”
  - “expected collective_id=X observed Y”
- Stream/event sync:
  - “waiting on cuda event” with long durations
  - “stream synchronize timed out”
- Scheduler starvation:
  - “prefill batch admitted while decode pending”
  - “fairness guardrail triggered” (if enabled)
- Cancellation path deadlock:
  - “client disconnected” followed by stuck collective / no further progress

---

## Traces (how to correlate stalls to collectives)
### What to look for in traces
For 2–3 timed-out requests:
- Identify the server-side spans for decode iterations.
- Check span attributes:
  - `tp.rank`, `tp.degree`
  - `tp.collective.inflight` counters
  - `tp.collective.seq` (if present)
  - `stall_reason` tag (if emitted)

### Minimal trace workflow
1) Pull a timeout request ID from API logs.
2) Find the associated `trace_id`.
3) Open trace and confirm where time is spent:
   - compute kernel spans vs NCCL spans vs scheduler wait spans.

If trace shows long gaps without spans, rely on heartbeat + logs; the process may be stuck below instrumentation.

---

## Node/replica inspection (commands)
The exact tooling differs by cluster. The commands below are representative for Redwood’s Kubernetes-based runtime clusters.

### Identify the pod and node
- Find pods for a model:
  - `kubectl -n runtime get pods -l app=redwood-runtime,model=<MODEL> -o wide`
- Filter to a specific replica:
  - `kubectl -n runtime get pod | grep <replica_id>`

### Quick GPU health (on the node)
If you have node access (or via privileged debug pod):
- `nvidia-smi -L`
- `nvidia-smi pmon -c 1`
- `nvidia-smi dmon -s pucvmt -c 5`

What you’re looking for:
- Rank imbalance (one GPU pegged while others idle)
- PCIe/NVLink issues (if visible)
- Memory near OOM or rapid allocation churn

### Collect pod logs around the stall
- `kubectl -n runtime logs <pod> --since=30m > /tmp/<pod>.log`
- If multi-container:
  - `kubectl -n runtime logs <pod> -c runtime --since=30m`

### Capture a lightweight runtime debug snapshot (preferred over heavy profilers)
If the runtime exposes a debug endpoint (varies by env):
- `kubectl -n runtime port-forward <pod> 6060:6060`
- Then (examples):
  - `curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/tp/status`
  - `curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/scheduler/queues`
  - `curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/kv/summary`

If endpoints are unavailable, rely on logs + metrics.

---

## Safely capturing NCCL debug traces
### When to enable NCCL debug
Enable NCCL debug only when:
- The issue reproduces and **appears comms/collective-related**.
- The blast radius is controlled (single replica or small canary).
- You have a clear rollback plan and a short time window (10–30 minutes).

### Recommended NCCL debug settings (balanced)
Use the least-verbose settings that still show collective sequencing.

Suggested env vars:
- `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL,INIT`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_FILE=/var/log/redwood/nccl/%h_%p_rank%r.log`

Optional (only if needed):
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL,INIT,GRAPH`
- `NCCL_DEBUG=WARN` (if INFO is too noisy)

Avoid unless specifically requested by kernel team:
- `NCCL_DEBUG=TRACE` (very noisy)
- Broad `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=ALL`

### Operational safety checklist
Before enabling:
1) Confirm **disk space** for logs on node/pod (NCCL logs can be large).
2) Confirm log retention and export path (avoid shipping huge logs to default pipelines).
3) Get explicit approval in the incident channel if customer impact is ongoing.

During capture:
- Keep capture window short.
- Prefer a canary replica with synthetic load or limited customer traffic.

After capture:
1) Turn off NCCL debug env vars.
2) Collect logs from the replica only.
3) Scrub and store logs in the incident artifact location (internal only).

### How to enable in practice
Preferred: use Redwood runtime config / feature flag mechanism for canary-only enablement.

If a rollout-based toggle exists (example):
- Enable flag: `runtime.nccl_debug_logging=canary` or `runtime.tp.nccl_debug=enabled` (exact names vary by release).
- Validate by searching logs for “NCCL version” and “NET/Topology” lines.

If you must patch a single deployment (last resort):
- Patch env vars on one replica deployment and restart only that replica.
- Coordinate with release/on-call owner to avoid triggering autoscaling thrash.

---

## Decision tree (compute vs comms vs scheduler)
### A) Suspect comms/collectives
Indicators:
- Heartbeats stop and `inflight_collectives` is stuck.
- Many timeouts cluster on a single replica; draining fixes temporarily.
- Logs show collective sequence mismatch or long waits on stream sync.

Next actions:
1) Capture NCCL debug logs (safe mode) on a canary replica.
2) Capture trace IDs for 2–3 timeouts.
3) Escalate to Kernel team with: model, TP degree, GPU type, runtime version, NCCL logs, and stall window.

### B) Suspect scheduler starvation
Indicators:
- Queue depth increases; prefill-heavy admission continues while decode progress halts.
- `stall_reason_total{reason="SCHED_STARVATION"}` increases.
- Switching workload mix (reduce long prefill / lower concurrency) improves.

Next actions:
1) Capture scheduler queue snapshot (`/debug/scheduler/queues`) if available.
2) Capture metrics: queue depth, prefill/decode ratio, starvation events.
3) Escalate to Scheduling team with workload distribution details (prompt length mix, streaming enabled, concurrency pattern).

### C) Suspect KV cache / memory pressure divergence
Indicators:
- Errors/warnings around compaction, allocation waits, or near-OOM.
- Stalls appear under long context + high concurrency.

Next actions:
1) Capture KV cache summary (`/debug/kv/summary`) if available.
2) Capture memory metrics + logs around compaction.
3) Escalate to KV/cache owner with stress suite parameters (context length, batch size, concurrency, cache settings).

---

## What to include in an escalation (copy/paste template)
**Subject:** TP stall suspected (comms/scheduler/KV) – <cluster> <model> TP=<N> GPU=<SKU> runtime=<ver>

- Cluster/region:
- Customer impact (Dedicated/Hosted, accounts):
- Model + version:
- Runtime version + recent changes/flags:
- GPU type + TP degree:
- Start time / peak / end:
- Symptoms:
  - Timeout rate:
  - p99 latency:
  - Streaming affected (yes/no):
- Suspected stall class: comms / scheduler / KV / unknown
- Evidence:
  - Dashboard links:
  - Metrics screenshots/exports:
  - Trace IDs:
  - Replica IDs / pod names:
  - Logs attached (runtime logs, NCCL logs if captured):
- Mitigations attempted (drain, caps, flags) + outcome:

---

## Common pitfalls
- Treating a wedged replica as overload: draining the replica may restore service but hides a systemic regression. Always capture artifacts first if feasible.
- Enabling NCCL TRACE broadly: can overload logging, increase latency, and create noisy data that’s hard to interpret.
- Failing to capture per-rank context: TP stalls are often rank-specific; always record `rank` and `replica_id`.

---

## Appendices
### A. Expected “healthy” baselines
These vary by model and workload, but generally:
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds` stays near 0–2s with brief spikes under GC/compaction.
- `runtime_tp_inflight_collectives` oscillates but returns to baseline; it should not monotonically increase.
- Queue depth should correlate with throughput; sustained queue growth with flat throughput is suspicious.

### B. When to declare a suspected regression
Strong indicators of a regression (vs isolated node failure):
- Same stall signature across multiple replicas after a rollout
- Correlated with a specific GPU SKU or NCCL/CUDA change
- Reproducible in perf-canary or benchmark-lab using the mixed prefill/decode workload

If regression is suspected, coordinate with release owner to pause rollout and set a clear go/no-go checkpoint.

source.title: Known issue: Tensor-parallel hangs under sustained load
source.author: Omar El-Sayed
related_pages: Linear: ENG-4821 Multi-GPU (TP) stability fixes initiative
source.content:
## Summary
Tensor-parallel (TP) replicas can intermittently **hang (no forward progress)** under sustained, mixed workloads (prefill + decode, long-context, bursty concurrency). This typically presents as **streaming responses stalling** and eventually **request timeouts**. In the worst case, one or more GPUs appear “wedged” until the replica is drained/restarted.

This page documents:
- Observable symptoms and common signatures
- Affected configurations (models, TP sizes, hardware)
- How to detect and confirm a TP stall
- Recommended mitigations (operational + config)
- Fix status by runtime version and feature flags

> If you are actively in an incident: jump to **Immediate mitigations** and then follow **Triage**.

---

## Impact / why we care
- **Customer impact:** Streaming completions stall; end-users see long hangs then timeouts. Enterprise Dedicated accounts are most affected due to higher sustained concurrency.
- **Operational impact:** Autoscaler thrash (p99 latency spikes trigger scale-up; wedged replicas never recover), elevated error rates, Sev1 potential.
- **Cost impact:** Stalled replicas waste GPU capacity and increase retry/duplicate compute.

---

## Symptoms (what you’ll see)
### External (API / client-facing)
- Streaming response starts and then stops emitting tokens mid-decode.
- Requests remain in-flight until client timeout (commonly 60–300s depending on route).
- Retry storms (client SDK retries or application-level retries) amplify load.

### Internal (replica-level)
- **No tokens produced** while GPU utilization may remain non-zero or drop unexpectedly.
- One TP rank appears stuck while others continue to enqueue work (or also stop).
- NCCL may not throw an explicit error; hangs can be “silent” without async error handling.

### Common failure signatures (taxonomy)
We currently bucket stalls into these high-level signatures (see `runtime.tp_stall_reason` below):
1. **collective_wait**: rank waiting for NCCL collective completion.
2. **collective_mismatch**: collective ordering divergence across ranks (often manifests as silent wait).
3. **scheduler_starvation**: collectives not scheduled fairly under prefill-heavy batching.
4. **stream_sync_gap**: CUDA stream/event ordering issue around NCCL boundary.
5. **kv_compaction_divergence**: KV cache compaction/fragmentation causes rank-side divergence.
6. **cancelled_stream_deadlock**: client disconnect/cancellation leaves ranks in inconsistent step state.

---

## Affected configurations
This issue has been observed most reliably under the following conditions.

### Workload characteristics
- Mixed prompt lengths (high variance) + streaming decode
- Long context (8k–32k) + high concurrent requests
- Bursty arrival patterns (spikes causing deep scheduler queues)
- Prefill-heavy traffic interleaved with ongoing decodes

### Deployment modes
- **Dedicated:** most common due to steady saturation and strict p99 expectations.
- **Hosted:** seen in a smaller number of high-throughput routes during peak bursts.

### Models and TP sizes (most affected)
| Category | Examples | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Large dense, long-context | Llama-70B class, Qwen-72B class | Higher risk when TP=4/8 and context > 8k |
| MoE models | Mixtral 8x7B class | Sensitive to scheduling/fairness under mixed prefill/decode |
| Any model with aggressive batching | Any high-throughput route | Risk increases with deep queues and long prefill batches |

### Hardware / topology
| Hardware | Topologies seen | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| A100 80GB | NVLink intra-node | Most reproductions and customer reports |
| H100 80GB | NVLink intra-node | Also observed; typically at higher throughput |
| PCIe-only multi-GPU | Mixed | Less common in production; higher sensitivity to comms latency |

### Runtime versions
- Observed most often on **runtime 1.18.x** in the presence of CUDA graph capture + mixed workloads.
- Some signatures also present on early **1.19.0 canaries** prior to watchdog + ordering hardening.

---

## Detection signals (metrics, logs, traces)
### Dashboards
- See: **TP stall and timeout dashboard** (Confluence: `tp-stall-and-timeout-dashboard`).

### Key metrics (authoritative)
> Metric names reflect the current standards agreed with SRE; older versions may differ.

1) **Per-rank heartbeat**
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_seconds{replica_id, tp_rank, model, cluster}`
- Signal: heartbeats stop updating for one or more ranks.
- Rule of thumb: **gap > 10s** during active traffic is suspicious; **gap > 30s** is a likely stall.

2) **Stall reason counters**
- `runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{reason, model, tp_size, cluster}`
- Watch for spikes in `collective_wait`, `scheduler_starvation`, `cancelled_stream_deadlock`.

3) **In-flight collective counters**
- `runtime_nccl_inflight_collectives{replica_id, tp_rank, collective}`
- Signal: counter remains >0 with no progress (and heartbeat gap).

4) **Scheduler pressure indicators**
- `runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{replica_id, queue}`
- `runtime_scheduler_prefill_decode_ratio{replica_id}`
- Signal: queue depth grows while decode throughput collapses.

5) **Customer-visible indicators**
- `gateway_request_timeout_total{route, model}`
- `gateway_streaming_disconnect_total{route, model}`
- `request_p99_latency_ms{route, model}`

### Logs (what to grep)
Look for structured fields (availability depends on runtime version):
- `tp_stall_detected=true`
- `tp_rank=<int>` and `tp_size=<int>`
- `stall_reason=<enum>`
- `inflight_collectives=<int>`
- `last_progress_ms=<int>`

### NCCL debug (use with care)
Use only for time-boxed windows due to verbosity/perf impact. Coordinate with SRE (see INT: enable extra NCCL debug logging).
Suggested minimal settings for targeted capture:
- `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1`
- `NCCL_DEBUG=WARN`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL`

Do NOT leave `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` enabled broadly on production Dedicated clusters.

---

## Immediate mitigations (incident-safe)
These mitigations prioritize restoring service and preventing global GPU wedging.

### 1) Drain/replace suspect replicas
- Drain the affected replica(s) at the orchestrator level (preferred) rather than waiting for recovery.
- If multiple replicas show heartbeat gaps, drain in a staggered fashion to avoid full capacity loss.

### 2) Reduce concurrency and/or batch size (fastest risk reduction)
Recommended temporary caps (starting point; adjust per model/hardware):
- Reduce `tp.max_concurrency_per_replica` by **30–50%**
- Reduce `max_batch_prefill_tokens` (or equivalent) by **25–40%**
- Prefer limiting **prefill batch growth** over decode to preserve streaming responsiveness

### 3) Disable high-risk execution paths (if available)
Depending on runtime version and flags, consider:
- Disable CUDA graphs for TP decode on affected routes:
  - `runtime.tp.cuda_graphs_decode.enabled=false`
- Enable conservative stream/event synchronization around NCCL:
  - `runtime.tp.nccl_stream_sync_hardening=on`

### 4) Enable watchdog + safe abort (where supported)
If the watchdog feature is available in your runtime build, enabling it can prevent indefinite hangs:
- `runtime.tp_watchdog.enabled=true`
- `runtime.tp_watchdog.timeout_ms=<workload-specific>` (see below)

> Watchdog enables **controlled abort + replica drain** semantics instead of indefinite waits. This will increase error counts during true stalls but improves overall availability.

### 5) Load shedding / routing fallback (platform-level)
- If stall signals are present, enable overload protection to **reject/redirect new requests** away from the wedged replica pool.
- Prefer fallback to a compatible model variant/region if configured for Dedicated/Hosted.

---

## Recommended default mitigations (non-incident)
For clusters with frequent TP stall history, keep these guardrails enabled until runtime 1.19.2+ is fully deployed.

### Configuration guardrails
| Guardrail | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrency cap | Set conservative `tp.max_concurrency_per_replica` | Reduces scheduler pressure and mixed-step divergence risk |
| Prefill batch cap | Limit `max_batch_prefill_tokens` | Prevents prefill-heavy batches from starving collectives/decodes |
| Async error handling | `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1` | Surfaces errors sooner; reduces silent waits |
| Stream sync hardening | Enable if perf impact acceptable | Avoids event/stream ordering gaps around collectives |
| Watchdog | Enable with staged rollout | Converts indefinite hang into bounded failure + recovery |

### Watchdog timeout guidance
Use model/workload-specific defaults; avoid overly aggressive thresholds that create false positives under long-context.
- Small/medium (≤13B dense): start at **8–12s**
- Large dense (≥70B) TP=4/8: start at **15–25s**
- Long-context (≥16k): start at **25–40s**

Always stage changes:
1) Canary one cluster/route
2) Validate stall detection + error rate impact
3) Expand to Dedicated
4) Expand to Hosted

---

## Triage checklist (confirming a TP hang)
Follow the detailed steps in the playbook (Confluence: `tp-stall-triage-playbook`). Quick checklist:

1) **Confirm customer symptom**
- Streaming stuck mid-response, elevated timeouts, p99 jump.

2) **Check TP heartbeat gaps**
- Identify impacted `replica_id` and `tp_rank`.

3) **Correlate with in-flight collectives**
- `runtime_nccl_inflight_collectives` stuck >0 indicates comms boundary involvement.

4) **Check scheduler pressure**
- Queue depth rising while throughput drops suggests scheduler starvation component.

5) **Search logs for stall reason**
- Confirm `stall_reason` and `last_progress_ms`.

6) **Mitigate**
- Drain replica(s) and apply caps/flags.

7) **Capture artifacts** (only if safe)
- Minimal NCCL WARN logs
- Trace IDs for representative stuck requests
- Replica runtime logs around the stall window

---

## Fix status / roadmap
This is an active reliability initiative tracked in Linear: **ENG-4821**.

### Fixes delivered / in-flight
| Area | Status | References |
|---|---|---|
| Heartbeat + stall reason tagging | Landed | PR-21834; dashboard panels in observability pack PR-412 |
| Scheduler fairness / collective ordering guardrails | Landed | PR-21841; mitigates `scheduler_starvation` and ordering divergence |
| NCCL stream/event sync hardening | Landed (feature-flagged) | PR-21853; evaluate perf impact per model |
| Watchdog + safe abort + replica drain | Landed (feature-flagged) | PR-21860; ADR-0127 defines semantics |
| KV cache compaction safety | Landed | PR-21866; targets `kv_compaction_divergence` |
| Cancelled streaming deadlock fix | Landed | PR-21890; targets disconnect-triggered stalls |
| Improved NCCL error surfacing | In rollout | PR-21912; improves customer-facing diagnostics |
| Load shed on TP stall signal | In rollout | PR-21875 |

### Version guidance (operational)
| Runtime version | Guidance |
|---|---|
| ≤ 1.18.7 | Higher risk. Keep concurrency/prefill caps. Prefer disabling CUDA graphs for TP decode on affected routes. Drain-on-stall is primary recovery. |
| 1.19.0–1.19.1 | Use only with watchdog and stream sync hardening flags in canary first. Validate stall reasons and noise. |
| 1.19.2+ | Target stable baseline for Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted. Keep monitoring; reduce guardrails only after 2+ weeks without stall alerts on target workloads. |

### Rollout plan
- Dedicated-first rollout with explicit go/no-go gates: see Linear **ENG-4829**.
- Canary success metrics:
  - TP stall alerts reduced by **>80%** week-over-week (normalized)
  - Request timeouts attributable to TP stalls reduced to **<0.1%** of requests on canary routes
  - No sustained p99 regression > **5%** after enabling stream sync hardening/watchdog

---

## Customer communication notes (Support/CS)
When communicating with customers, avoid over-specifying a single root cause unless confirmed by artifacts. Recommended phrasing:
- “We have identified a class of TP forward-progress stalls under specific mixed workloads. We are rolling out runtime changes to improve collective ordering, stream synchronization, and add bounded-time watchdog recovery.”

If asking for customer artifacts, request:
- Affected model + TP size, GPU type, context length distribution
- Approx concurrency at time of stall
- Example request IDs / timestamps
- Whether stalls correlate with client disconnects

Support-facing summary and templated mitigations are maintained in: **customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/tp-timeouts-workaround-dedicated**.

---

## Owner / escalation
- Primary owner: **Eng Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling)**
- Incident escalation: **#eng-sre** + **#eng-runtime** (include replica IDs, model, TP size, and stall reason metrics)
- Contacts:
  - Runtime TL: Noah Patel
  - Kernel: Hiro Tanaka / Grace Kim
  - Scheduling: Sofia Ivanova / Julian Moore
  - SRE lead: Rafael Mendes / Aisha Bello

---

## Changelog
- 2025-03-04: Updated version guidance for 1.19.2+ rollout gates; added cancelled-streaming deadlock signature and mitigation.
- 2025-02-20: Added heartbeat and stall reason metric names; linked dashboard and ADR.
- 2024-11-12: Initial publication based on Dedicated TP timeout reports and early repro findings.

This page documents:
- Observable symptoms and common signatures
- Affected configurations (models, TP sizes, hardware)

---

3. **scheduler_starvation**: collectives not scheduled fairly under prefill-heavy batching.
4. **stream_sync_gap**: CUDA stream/event ordering issue around NCCL boundary.
5. **kv_compaction_divergence**: KV cache compaction/fragmentation causes rank-side divergence.
6. **cancelled_stream_deadlock**: client disconnect/cancellation leaves ranks in inconsistent step state.

---

## Affected configurations
This issue has been observed most reliably under the following conditions.

### Workload characteristics
- Mixed prompt lengths (high variance) + streaming decode
- Long context (8k–32k) + high concurrent requests
- Bursty arrival patterns (spikes causing deep scheduler queues)
- Prefill-heavy traffic interleaved with ongoing decodes

### Deployment modes
- **Dedicated:** most common due to steady saturation and strict p99 expectations.
- **Hosted:** seen in a smaller number of high-throughput routes during peak bursts.

### Models and TP sizes (most affected)
| Category | Examples | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Large dense, long-context | Llama-70B class, Qwen-72B class | Higher risk when TP=4/8 and context > 8k |
| MoE models | Mixtral 8x7B class | Sensitive to scheduling/fairness under mixed prefill/decode |
| Any model with aggressive batching | Any high-throughput route | Risk increases with deep queues and long prefill batches |

### Hardware / topology
| Hardware | Topologies seen | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| A100 80GB | NVLink intra-node | Most reproductions and customer reports |
| H100 80GB | NVLink intra-node | Also observed; typically at higher throughput |
| PCIe-only multi-GPU | Mixed | Less common in production; higher sensitivity to comms latency |

### Runtime versions
- Observed most often on **runtime 1.18.x** in the presence of CUDA graph capture + mixed workloads.
- Some signatures also present on early **1.19.0 canaries** prior to watchdog + ordering hardening.

---

## Detection signals (metrics, logs, traces)
### Dashboards
- See: **TP stall and timeout dashboard** (Confluence: `tp-stall-and-timeout-dashboard`).

### Key metrics (authoritative)
> Metric names reflect the current standards agreed with SRE; older versions may differ.

1) **Per-rank heartbeat**
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_seconds{replica_id, tp_rank, model, cluster}`
- Signal: heartbeats stop updating for one or more ranks.
- Rule of thumb: **gap > 10s** during active traffic is suspicious; **gap > 30s** is a likely stall.

2) **Stall reason counters**
- `runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{reason, model, tp_size, cluster}`
- Watch for spikes in `collective_wait`, `scheduler_starvation`, `cancelled_stream_deadlock`.

3) **In-flight collective counters**
- `runtime_nccl_inflight_collectives{replica_id, tp_rank, collective}`
- Signal: counter remains >0 with no progress (and heartbeat gap).

4) **Scheduler pressure indicators**
- `runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{replica_id, queue}`
- `runtime_scheduler_prefill_decode_ratio{replica_id}`
- Signal: queue depth grows while decode throughput collapses.

5) **Customer-visible indicators**
- `gateway_request_timeout_total{route, model}`
- `gateway_streaming_disconnect_total{route, model}`
- `request_p99_latency_ms{route, model}`

### Logs (what to grep)
Look for structured fields (availability depends on runtime version):
- `tp_stall_detected=true`
- `tp_rank=<int>` and `tp_size=<int>`
- `stall_reason=<enum>`
- `inflight_collectives=<int>`
- `last_progress_ms=<int>`

### NCCL debug (use with care)
Use only for time-boxed windows due to verbosity/perf impact. Coordinate with SRE (see INT: enable extra NCCL debug logging).
Suggested minimal settings for targeted capture:
- `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1`
- `NCCL_DEBUG=WARN`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL`

Do NOT leave `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` enabled broadly on production Dedicated clusters.

---

## Immediate mitigations (incident-safe)
These mitigations prioritize restoring service and preventing global GPU wedging.

### 1) Drain/replace suspect replicas
- Drain the affected replica(s) at the orchestrator level (preferred) rather than waiting for recovery.
- If multiple replicas show heartbeat gaps, drain in a staggered fashion to avoid full capacity loss.

### 2) Reduce concurrency and/or batch size (fastest risk reduction)
Recommended temporary caps (starting point; adjust per model/hardware):
- Reduce `tp.max_concurrency_per_replica` by **30–50%**
- Reduce `max_batch_prefill_tokens` (or equivalent) by **25–40%**
- Prefer limiting **prefill batch growth** over decode to preserve streaming responsiveness

### 3) Disable high-risk execution paths (if available)
Depending on runtime version and flags, consider:
- Disable CUDA graphs for TP decode on affected routes:
  - `runtime.tp.cuda_graphs_decode.enabled=false`
- Enable conservative stream/event synchronization around NCCL:
  - `runtime.tp.nccl_stream_sync_hardening=on`

### 4) Enable watchdog + safe abort (where supported)
If the watchdog feature is available in your runtime build, enabling it can prevent indefinite hangs:
- `runtime.tp_watchdog.enabled=true`
- `runtime.tp_watchdog.timeout_ms=<workload-specific>` (see below)

> Watchdog enables **controlled abort + replica drain** semantics instead of indefinite waits. This will increase error counts during true stalls but improves overall availability.

### 5) Load shedding / routing fallback (platform-level)
- If stall signals are present, enable overload protection to **reject/redirect new requests** away from the wedged replica pool.
- Prefer fallback to a compatible model variant/region if configured for Dedicated/Hosted.

---

## Recommended default mitigations (non-incident)
For clusters with frequent TP stall history, keep these guardrails enabled until runtime 1.19.2+ is fully deployed.

### Configuration guardrails
| Guardrail | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrency cap | Set conservative `tp.max_concurrency_per_replica` | Reduces scheduler pressure and mixed-step divergence risk |
| Prefill batch cap | Limit `max_batch_prefill_tokens` | Prevents prefill-heavy batches from starving collectives/decodes |
| Async error handling | `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1` | Surfaces errors sooner; reduces silent waits |
| Stream sync hardening | Enable if perf impact acceptable | Avoids event/stream ordering gaps around collectives |
| Watchdog | Enable with staged rollout | Converts indefinite hang into bounded failure + recovery |

### Watchdog timeout guidance
Use model/workload-specific defaults; avoid overly aggressive thresholds that create false positives under long-context.
- Small/medium (≤13B dense): start at **8–12s**
- Large dense (≥70B) TP=4/8: start at **15–25s**
- Long-context (≥16k): start at **25–40s**

Always stage changes:
1) Canary one cluster/route
2) Validate stall detection + error rate impact
3) Expand to Dedicated
4) Expand to Hosted

---

## Triage checklist (confirming a TP hang)
Follow the detailed steps in the playbook (Confluence: `tp-stall-triage-playbook`). Quick checklist:

1) **Confirm customer symptom**
- Streaming stuck mid-response, elevated timeouts, p99 jump.

2) **Check TP heartbeat gaps**
- Identify impacted `replica_id` and `tp_rank`.

3) **Correlate with in-flight collectives**
- `runtime_nccl_inflight_collectives` stuck >0 indicates comms boundary involvement.

4) **Check scheduler pressure**
- Queue depth rising while throughput drops suggests scheduler starvation component.

5) **Search logs for stall reason**
- Confirm `stall_reason` and `last_progress_ms`.

6) **Mitigate**
- Drain replica(s) and apply caps/flags.

7) **Capture artifacts** (only if safe)
- Minimal NCCL WARN logs
- Trace IDs for representative stuck requests
- Replica runtime logs around the stall window

---

## Fix status / roadmap
This is an active reliability initiative tracked in Linear: **ENG-4821**.

### Fixes delivered / in-flight
| Area | Status | References |
|---|---|---|
| Heartbeat + stall reason tagging | Landed | PR-21834; dashboard panels in observability pack PR-412 |
| Scheduler fairness / collective ordering guardrails | Landed | PR-21841; mitigates `scheduler_starvation` and ordering divergence |
| NCCL stream/event sync hardening | Landed (feature-flagged) | PR-21853; evaluate perf impact per model |
| Watchdog + safe abort + replica drain | Landed (feature-flagged) | PR-21860; ADR-0127 defines semantics |
| KV cache compaction safety | Landed | PR-21866; targets `kv_compaction_divergence` |
| Cancelled streaming deadlock fix | Landed | PR-21890; targets disconnect-triggered stalls |
| Improved NCCL error surfacing | In rollout | PR-21912; improves customer-facing diagnostics |
| Load shed on TP stall signal | In rollout | PR-21875 |

### Version guidance (operational)
| Runtime version | Guidance |
|---|---|
| ≤ 1.18.7 | Higher risk. Keep concurrency/prefill caps. Prefer disabling CUDA graphs for TP decode on affected routes. Drain-on-stall is primary recovery. |
| 1.19.0–1.19.1 | Use only with watchdog and stream sync hardening flags in canary first. Validate stall reasons and noise. |
| 1.19.2+ | Target stable baseline for Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted. Keep monitoring; reduce guardrails only after 2+ weeks without stall alerts on target workloads. |

### Rollout plan
- Dedicated-first rollout with explicit go/no-go gates: see Linear **ENG-4829**.
- Canary success metrics:
  - TP stall alerts reduced by **>80%** week-over-week (normalized)
  - Request timeouts attributable to TP stalls reduced to **<0.1%** of requests on canary routes
  - No sustained p99 regression > **5%** after enabling stream sync hardening/watchdog

---

## Customer communication notes (Support/CS)
When communicating with customers, avoid over-specifying a single root cause unless confirmed by artifacts. Recommended phrasing:
- “We have identified a class of TP forward-progress stalls under specific mixed workloads. We are rolling out runtime changes to improve collective ordering, stream synchronization, and add bounded-time watchdog recovery.”

If asking for customer artifacts, request:
- Affected model + TP size, GPU type, context length distribution
- Approx concurrency at time of stall
- Example request IDs / timestamps
- Whether stalls correlate with client disconnects

Support-facing summary and templated mitigations are maintained in: **customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/tp-timeouts-workaround-dedicated**.

---

## Owner / escalation
- Primary owner: **Eng Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling)**
- Incident escalation: **#eng-sre** + **#eng-runtime** (include replica IDs, model, TP size, and stall reason metrics)
- Contacts:
  - Runtime TL: Noah Patel
  - Kernel: Hiro Tanaka / Grace Kim
  - Scheduling: Sofia Ivanova / Julian Moore
  - SRE lead: Rafael Mendes / Aisha Bello

---

## Changelog
- 2025-03-04: Updated version guidance for 1.19.2+ rollout gates; added cancelled-streaming deadlock signature and mitigation.
- 2025-02-20: Added heartbeat and stall reason metric names; linked dashboard and ADR.
- 2024-11-12: Initial publication based on Dedicated TP timeout reports and early repro findings.
text:
## Summary
Tensor-parallel (TP) replicas can intermittently **hang (no forward progress)** under sustained, mixed workloads (prefill + decode, long-context, bursty concurrency). This typically presents as **streaming responses stalling** and eventually **request timeouts**. In the worst case, one or more GPUs appear “wedged” until the replica is drained/restarted.

This page documents:
- Observable symptoms and common signatures
- Affected configurations (models, TP sizes, hardware)
- How to detect and confirm a TP stall
- Recommended mitigations (operational + config)
- Fix status by runtime version and feature flags

> If you are actively in an incident: jump to **Immediate mitigations** and then follow **Triage**.

---

## Impact / why we care
- **Customer impact:** Streaming completions stall; end-users see long hangs then timeouts. Enterprise Dedicated accounts are most affected due to higher sustained concurrency.
- **Operational impact:** Autoscaler thrash (p99 latency spikes trigger scale-up; wedged replicas never recover), elevated error rates, Sev1 potential.
- **Cost impact:** Stalled replicas waste GPU capacity and increase retry/duplicate compute.

---

## Symptoms (what you’ll see)
### External (API / client-facing)
- Streaming response starts and then stops emitting tokens mid-decode.
- Requests remain in-flight until client timeout (commonly 60–300s depending on route).
- Retry storms (client SDK retries or application-level retries) amplify load.

### Internal (replica-level)
- **No tokens produced** while GPU utilization may remain non-zero or drop unexpectedly.
- One TP rank appears stuck while others continue to enqueue work (or also stop).
- NCCL may not throw an explicit error; hangs can be “silent” without async error handling.

### Common failure signatures (taxonomy)
We currently bucket stalls into these high-level signatures (see `runtime.tp_stall_reason` below):
1. **collective_wait**: rank waiting for NCCL collective completion.
2. **collective_mismatch**: collective ordering divergence across ranks (often manifests as silent wait).
3. **scheduler_starvation**: collectives not scheduled fairly under prefill-heavy batching.
4. **stream_sync_gap**: CUDA stream/event ordering issue around NCCL boundary.
5. **kv_compaction_divergence**: KV cache compaction/fragmentation causes rank-side divergence.
6. **cancelled_stream_deadlock**: client disconnect/cancellation leaves ranks in inconsistent step state.

---

## Affected configurations
This issue has been observed most reliably under the following conditions.

### Workload characteristics
- Mixed prompt lengths (high variance) + streaming decode
- Long context (8k–32k) + high concurrent requests
- Bursty arrival patterns (spikes causing deep scheduler queues)
- Prefill-heavy traffic interleaved with ongoing decodes

### Deployment modes
- **Dedicated:** most common due to steady saturation and strict p99 expectations.
- **Hosted:** seen in a smaller number of high-throughput routes during peak bursts.

### Models and TP sizes (most affected)
| Category | Examples | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Large dense, long-context | Llama-70B class, Qwen-72B class | Higher risk when TP=4/8 and context > 8k |
| MoE models | Mixtral 8x7B class | Sensitive to scheduling/fairness under mixed prefill/decode |
| Any model with aggressive batching | Any high-throughput route | Risk increases with deep queues and long prefill batches |

### Hardware / topology
| Hardware | Topologies seen | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| A100 80GB | NVLink intra-node | Most reproductions and customer reports |
| H100 80GB | NVLink intra-node | Also observed; typically at higher throughput |
| PCIe-only multi-GPU | Mixed | Less common in production; higher sensitivity to comms latency |

### Runtime versions
- Observed most often on **runtime 1.18.x** in the presence of CUDA graph capture + mixed workloads.
- Some signatures also present on early **1.19.0 canaries** prior to watchdog + ordering hardening.

---

## Detection signals (metrics, logs, traces)
### Dashboards
- See: **TP stall and timeout dashboard** (Confluence: `tp-stall-and-timeout-dashboard`).

### Key metrics (authoritative)
> Metric names reflect the current standards agreed with SRE; older versions may differ.

1) **Per-rank heartbeat**
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_seconds{replica_id, tp_rank, model, cluster}`
- Signal: heartbeats stop updating for one or more ranks.
- Rule of thumb: **gap > 10s** during active traffic is suspicious; **gap > 30s** is a likely stall.

2) **Stall reason counters**
- `runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{reason, model, tp_size, cluster}`
- Watch for spikes in `collective_wait`, `scheduler_starvation`, `cancelled_stream_deadlock`.

3) **In-flight collective counters**
- `runtime_nccl_inflight_collectives{replica_id, tp_rank, collective}`
- Signal: counter remains >0 with no progress (and heartbeat gap).

4) **Scheduler pressure indicators**
- `runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{replica_id, queue}`
- `runtime_scheduler_prefill_decode_ratio{replica_id}`
- Signal: queue depth grows while decode throughput collapses.

5) **Customer-visible indicators**
- `gateway_request_timeout_total{route, model}`
- `gateway_streaming_disconnect_total{route, model}`
- `request_p99_latency_ms{route, model}`

### Logs (what to grep)
Look for structured fields (availability depends on runtime version):
- `tp_stall_detected=true`
- `tp_rank=<int>` and `tp_size=<int>`
- `stall_reason=<enum>`
- `inflight_collectives=<int>`
- `last_progress_ms=<int>`

### NCCL debug (use with care)
Use only for time-boxed windows due to verbosity/perf impact. Coordinate with SRE (see INT: enable extra NCCL debug logging).
Suggested minimal settings for targeted capture:
- `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1`
- `NCCL_DEBUG=WARN`
- `NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL`

Do NOT leave `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` enabled broadly on production Dedicated clusters.

---

## Immediate mitigations (incident-safe)
These mitigations prioritize restoring service and preventing global GPU wedging.

### 1) Drain/replace suspect replicas
- Drain the affected replica(s) at the orchestrator level (preferred) rather than waiting for recovery.
- If multiple replicas show heartbeat gaps, drain in a staggered fashion to avoid full capacity loss.

### 2) Reduce concurrency and/or batch size (fastest risk reduction)
Recommended temporary caps (starting point; adjust per model/hardware):
- Reduce `tp.max_concurrency_per_replica` by **30–50%**
- Reduce `max_batch_prefill_tokens` (or equivalent) by **25–40%**
- Prefer limiting **prefill batch growth** over decode to preserve streaming responsiveness

### 3) Disable high-risk execution paths (if available)
Depending on runtime version and flags, consider:
- Disable CUDA graphs for TP decode on affected routes:
  - `runtime.tp.cuda_graphs_decode.enabled=false`
- Enable conservative stream/event synchronization around NCCL:
  - `runtime.tp.nccl_stream_sync_hardening=on`

### 4) Enable watchdog + safe abort (where supported)
If the watchdog feature is available in your runtime build, enabling it can prevent indefinite hangs:
- `runtime.tp_watchdog.enabled=true`
- `runtime.tp_watchdog.timeout_ms=<workload-specific>` (see below)

> Watchdog enables **controlled abort + replica drain** semantics instead of indefinite waits. This will increase error counts during true stalls but improves overall availability.

### 5) Load shedding / routing fallback (platform-level)
- If stall signals are present, enable overload protection to **reject/redirect new requests** away from the wedged replica pool.
- Prefer fallback to a compatible model variant/region if configured for Dedicated/Hosted.

---

## Recommended default mitigations (non-incident)
For clusters with frequent TP stall history, keep these guardrails enabled until runtime 1.19.2+ is fully deployed.

### Configuration guardrails
| Guardrail | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrency cap | Set conservative `tp.max_concurrency_per_replica` | Reduces scheduler pressure and mixed-step divergence risk |
| Prefill batch cap | Limit `max_batch_prefill_tokens` | Prevents prefill-heavy batches from starving collectives/decodes |
| Async error handling | `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1` | Surfaces errors sooner; reduces silent waits |
| Stream sync hardening | Enable if perf impact acceptable | Avoids event/stream ordering gaps around collectives |
| Watchdog | Enable with staged rollout | Converts indefinite hang into bounded failure + recovery |

### Watchdog timeout guidance
Use model/workload-specific defaults; avoid overly aggressive thresholds that create false positives under long-context.
- Small/medium (≤13B dense): start at **8–12s**
- Large dense (≥70B) TP=4/8: start at **15–25s**
- Long-context (≥16k): start at **25–40s**

Always stage changes:
1) Canary one cluster/route
2) Validate stall detection + error rate impact
3) Expand to Dedicated
4) Expand to Hosted

---

## Triage checklist (confirming a TP hang)
Follow the detailed steps in the playbook (Confluence: `tp-stall-triage-playbook`). Quick checklist:

1) **Confirm customer symptom**
- Streaming stuck mid-response, elevated timeouts, p99 jump.

2) **Check TP heartbeat gaps**
- Identify impacted `replica_id` and `tp_rank`.

3) **Correlate with in-flight collectives**
- `runtime_nccl_inflight_collectives` stuck >0 indicates comms boundary involvement.

4) **Check scheduler pressure**
- Queue depth rising while throughput drops suggests scheduler starvation component.

5) **Search logs for stall reason**
- Confirm `stall_reason` and `last_progress_ms`.

6) **Mitigate**
- Drain replica(s) and apply caps/flags.

7) **Capture artifacts** (only if safe)
- Minimal NCCL WARN logs
- Trace IDs for representative stuck requests
- Replica runtime logs around the stall window

---

## Fix status / roadmap
This is an active reliability initiative tracked in Linear: **ENG-4821**.

### Fixes delivered / in-flight
| Area | Status | References |
|---|---|---|
| Heartbeat + stall reason tagging | Landed | PR-21834; dashboard panels in observability pack PR-412 |
| Scheduler fairness / collective ordering guardrails | Landed | PR-21841; mitigates `scheduler_starvation` and ordering divergence |
| NCCL stream/event sync hardening | Landed (feature-flagged) | PR-21853; evaluate perf impact per model |
| Watchdog + safe abort + replica drain | Landed (feature-flagged) | PR-21860; ADR-0127 defines semantics |
| KV cache compaction safety | Landed | PR-21866; targets `kv_compaction_divergence` |
| Cancelled streaming deadlock fix | Landed | PR-21890; targets disconnect-triggered stalls |
| Improved NCCL error surfacing | In rollout | PR-21912; improves customer-facing diagnostics |
| Load shed on TP stall signal | In rollout | PR-21875 |

### Version guidance (operational)
| Runtime version | Guidance |
|---|---|
| ≤ 1.18.7 | Higher risk. Keep concurrency/prefill caps. Prefer disabling CUDA graphs for TP decode on affected routes. Drain-on-stall is primary recovery. |
| 1.19.0–1.19.1 | Use only with watchdog and stream sync hardening flags in canary first. Validate stall reasons and noise. |
| 1.19.2+ | Target stable baseline for Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted. Keep monitoring; reduce guardrails only after 2+ weeks without stall alerts on target workloads. |

### Rollout plan
- Dedicated-first rollout with explicit go/no-go gates: see Linear **ENG-4829**.
- Canary success metrics:
  - TP stall alerts reduced by **>80%** week-over-week (normalized)
  - Request timeouts attributable to TP stalls reduced to **<0.1%** of requests on canary routes
  - No sustained p99 regression > **5%** after enabling stream sync hardening/watchdog

---

## Customer communication notes (Support/CS)
When communicating with customers, avoid over-specifying a single root cause unless confirmed by artifacts. Recommended phrasing:
- “We have identified a class of TP forward-progress stalls under specific mixed workloads. We are rolling out runtime changes to improve collective ordering, stream synchronization, and add bounded-time watchdog recovery.”

If asking for customer artifacts, request:
- Affected model + TP size, GPU type, context length distribution
- Approx concurrency at time of stall
- Example request IDs / timestamps

Support-facing summary and templated mitigations are maintained in: **customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/tp-timeouts-workaround-dedicated**.

- Primary owner: **Eng Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling)**

  - Runtime TL: Noah Patel
  - Kernel: Hiro Tanaka / Grace Kim
